findings.location: Description: "Update project documentation for the test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate."
findings.finding: The plan states WHAT is being done (update docs) but does not explicitly name WHO benefits or WHAT problem is solved. The implied consumer — developers/contributors who need to understand the test quality gate and pre-commit enforcement — is reasonably inferable for a documentation task, so a benign reading partially satisfies this. However, it is left implicit rather than stated.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence naming the beneficiary and problem, e.g. "So that contributors understand the test quality gate and avoid commits being unexpectedly blocked by the pre-commit hook."

findings.location: File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit"; Acceptance Criteria (2 items)
Acceptance Criteria: "bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)" and "grep -q \"test.quality\" CLAUDE.md"
Title "Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs for test quality enforcement" vs Description File Impact "CLAUDE.md — Edit"
findings.finding: The title promises updates to both CLAUDE.md AND "workflow docs", but the File Impact section lists only CLAUDE.md as edited, and both acceptance criteria reference only CLAUDE.md. This is a potential scope mismatch: either the title overstates the work (no separate workflow docs touched) or a workflow-doc file is missing from the plan. Cannot decide without knowing whether "workflow docs" is meant to be a section within CLAUDE.md or a distinct file.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify whether "workflow docs" refers to a section inside CLAUDE.md (in which case narrow the title) or a separate file (in which case add it to File Impact and add a corresponding acceptance criterion).

findings.location: File Impact: 'CLAUDE.md — Edit' and AC 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate'
findings.finding: The plan assumes the 'test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate' already exist as referenceable mechanisms in the codebase (the title also mentions 'workflow docs' but only CLAUDE.md is listed under File Impact). It also assumes tests/run-all.sh exists and passes. These cannot be confirmed from the plan text alone; an executor should verify (1) that the test-quality gate / pre-commit hook actually exists so the docs describe reality, and (2) that tests/run-all.sh is present and currently green.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile the title ('and workflow docs') with the File Impact list (only CLAUDE.md), and add a verification step confirming the pre-commit gate/overlay being documented actually exists before writing docs about it.

findings.location: Description: "Update project documentation for the test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate."
findings.finding: The plan states WHAT (update CLAUDE.md/workflow docs) but only implies WHO benefits and WHY. The beneficiary (developers/contributors who need to know about the test quality gate and pre-commit enforcement) is plausibly implied for this kind of doc task, but it is not made explicit, nor is the problem being solved (e.g., contributors are unaware of the new gate, causing failed commits). Under a benign reading, the implied technical consumer counts, so this is borderline.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence naming the beneficiary and problem, e.g., 'So that contributors understand and comply with the new test-quality pre-commit gate and don't waste cycles on rejected commits.'

findings.location: File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit"; Acceptance Criteria (two items)
Acceptance Criteria: 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)' and 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md'
Title 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs for test quality enforcement' vs File Impact 'CLAUDE.md — Edit'
findings.finding: The title promises updating 'CLAUDE.md AND workflow docs', but the File Impact section and acceptance criteria only reference CLAUDE.md. Either workflow docs are out of scope (title overstates the work) or a file is missing from the plan. This cannot be resolved without knowing the intended scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Either add the specific workflow doc file(s) to File Impact and a corresponding acceptance criterion, or narrow the title to reflect that only CLAUDE.md is changed (e.g. 'Update CLAUDE.md for test quality enforcement').

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)' and 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md'
findings.finding: The plan assumes (1) a test runner exists at tests/run-all.sh and currently passes, and (2) CLAUDE.md exists and is the correct file to document the gate. It also references a 'test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate' as if those artifacts already exist, but does not point to where they are defined. The grep pattern "test.quality" must match whatever wording is actually added — an executor should confirm the chosen heading/phrasing satisfies the grep. These assertions need verification against the live codebase before relying on them.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm tests/run-all.sh exists and passes before edits; state where the 'test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate' are implemented so the docs accurately describe them; ensure the CLAUDE.md text deliberately contains a token matching the grep pattern 'test.quality'.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: "grep -q \"test.quality\" CLAUDE.md" vs Description "test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate"
findings.finding: The verification grep pattern "test.quality" (with a literal/regex dot) is loosely coupled to the human-readable AC "CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate". The plan's Description also mentions documenting both "the test quality enforcement overlay AND pre-commit gate", but the acceptance criteria only verify a single grep for "test.quality" and say nothing about documenting the pre-commit gate. So the success criteria under-cover what the Description says must be documented.
findings.suggested_edit: Suggestion: add an acceptance criterion covering the pre-commit gate documentation (e.g. grep for "pre-commit" in CLAUDE.md) so the criteria match the Description's stated scope of both the enforcement overlay and the pre-commit gate.

findings.location: File Impact: 'CLAUDE.md — Edit'; AC 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate'
findings.finding: The title states 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs for test quality enforcement', implying multiple documentation files (workflow docs) plus possibly pre-commit gate config. However the File Impact section names only CLAUDE.md as an edit target. If 'workflow docs' (e.g., a docs/ directory, CONTRIBUTING, or .pre-commit-config) exist in the repo, they are likely also in scope but are not enumerated. Confirming whether other workflow docs need editing requires live codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Suggestion: reconcile title vs File Impact — either explicitly list each workflow doc file to edit (e.g., docs/workflow.md, CONTRIBUTING.md) or narrow the title to 'Update CLAUDE.md' if only that file changes.

findings.location: AC: 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' with Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: The plan does not specify WHAT content to add to CLAUDE.md about the test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate. The only concrete requirement is that the string matched by grep "test.quality" appears — which an executor could satisfy by inserting a single trivial line, without documenting the actual gate behavior, the pre-commit hook, or how to run/bypass it. 'document test quality gate' is underspecified and pushes the substantive content decision onto the executor. Also the verify regex "test.quality" matches 'test quality', 'test-quality', etc., so it does not pin a meaningful section.
findings.suggested_edit: Suggestion: enumerate the required documentation content (e.g., what the test quality overlay enforces, how the pre-commit gate runs, how to run tests/run-all.sh, how to bypass the hook if needed) and tighten the verify to assert presence of a named section heading rather than a loose substring.

findings.location: File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit"; Description mentions "workflow docs" (title) and "pre-commit gate"
findings.finding: The edit-set names only CLAUDE.md, which is plausible and low blast-radius for a docs change. However, the title says "Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs" (plural) and the description references a "pre-commit gate" — suggesting other workflow/doc files (e.g., CONTRIBUTING.md, a pre-commit config, or docs/ workflow guides) may also need updating but are not listed in File Impact. Confirming whether those additional docs exist and need edits requires the live repo.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile the title and File Impact: either narrow the title to "Update CLAUDE.md" or enumerate the specific "workflow docs" files (and any pre-commit config) in the File Impact section.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: "CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate" Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: The plan defines the deliverable only by a grep for the literal-ish pattern "test.quality", which is underspecified: it does not state what content about the test quality gate / pre-commit gate must be documented (e.g., how the gate works, how to run it, how to bypass, what it enforces). An executor could satisfy the grep by inserting the string "test.quality" with minimal/meaningless prose, or could be unsure what depth of documentation is expected. The title's "workflow docs" and "pre-commit gate" also lack any concrete spec, leaving the executor to decide scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Specify the required documentation content for CLAUDE.md (e.g., a section titled "Test quality gate" covering what the pre-commit gate enforces, how to run it locally, and how/when to override). Note the grep is a smoke check, not the full acceptance bar, and list the substantive points that must appear.

findings.location: File Impact lists only 'CLAUDE.md — Edit', but the title says 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs'; AC 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' / Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: Two ambiguities force the executor to make undocumented decisions. (1) The title promises 'workflow docs' updates, but the File Impact section and acceptance criteria only cover CLAUDE.md — the executor cannot tell whether other workflow docs must be edited, and which files those are. (2) The actual content to add is undefined: 'documents test quality gate' / 'documents test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate' provides no specifics about what the gate does, how it is invoked, or what overlay behavior to describe; the grep check only verifies a literal 'test.quality' substring exists, which can pass without any meaningful documentation. The executor would have to invent the documentation content.
findings.suggested_edit: Either remove 'workflow docs' from scope or enumerate the exact workflow doc files to edit. Add a concrete outline of what CLAUDE.md must say (e.g., name the pre-commit hook/script, the command to run the gate, and the overlay's enforcement rules), and strengthen the AC beyond a substring grep.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)' and 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md'
Acceptance Criteria verify steps: 'bash tests/run-all.sh' and 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md'
Title and Description mention 'workflow docs' and 'pre-commit gate'; File Impact lists only 'CLAUDE.md — Edit'; AC2 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate'
findings.finding: The plan body promises more than the acceptance criteria and file impact cover. The title says 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs', and the description mentions 'pre-commit gate', but File Impact lists only CLAUDE.md (no workflow docs) and there is no criterion covering any 'workflow docs' or the 'pre-commit gate' content. This is described work with no corresponding criterion (and a scope/terminology mismatch between 'workflow docs' in the title and the single CLAUDE.md edit). Also AC1 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes' is an orphan criterion — nothing in the description discusses tests being run/affected by this docs-only change.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) narrow the title/description to CLAUDE.md only and drop 'workflow docs'/'pre-commit gate' references, or (b) add the specific workflow doc file(s) to File Impact and add an acceptance criterion verifying the pre-commit gate documentation. Also clarify why AC1 (run-all.sh) applies to a docs-only change or remove it.

findings.location: AC2 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' with Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: There is a gap between the described end-state and its verification. The description says to document the 'test quality enforcement overlay AND pre-commit gate', but the only verification for content is grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md, which passes if a single matching string exists and does not confirm the pre-commit gate or the 'enforcement overlay' is actually documented. Done can be declared without reaching the stated end-state. Additionally, 'test.quality' is a regex (the '.' matches any character), which may not match the intended literal phrase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit verification for each documented concept, e.g. grep -qi 'pre-commit' CLAUDE.md and grep -qi 'test quality' CLAUDE.md (use a fixed-string grep -F or escaped pattern for the literal), so the union of steps demonstrably reaches the described end-state.

findings.location: Acceptance criterion 2 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' with Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md, vs Description scope mentioning 'pre-commit gate' and 'workflow docs'
findings.finding: There is a mild internal inconsistency between the stated scope and the verification. The title and description say to update 'workflow docs' and document the 'pre-commit gate', and the File Impact list contains only 'CLAUDE.md — Edit'. The acceptance criteria only check CLAUDE.md content, with no criterion covering the 'pre-commit gate' or any 'workflow docs' beyond CLAUDE.md. Either the scope mentions of pre-commit gate / workflow docs are aspirational (benign) or the plan is missing file impact entries and ACs. This ambiguity could cause the agent to under- or over-build.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile scope and ACs: either remove 'workflow docs'/'pre-commit gate' from the description if CLAUDE.md is the only target, or add the affected workflow-doc files to File Impact and add an AC verifying the pre-commit gate is documented.

findings.location: AC2: 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' — Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: AC2 is mechanically measurable and in-session completable (a grep), but the grep is a weak proxy for the intent. The regex "test.quality" (where '.' matches any char) would pass on an unrelated phrase like 'test_quality_metrics' or even a single mention, without the section actually documenting the gate's behavior, enforcement, or how to satisfy it. This lets a trivially-passing edit satisfy the criterion without meeting the documentation goal. AC1 (bash tests/run-all.sh exit 0) is measurable and in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Tighten AC2 to verify substantive content, e.g. grep for a specific heading like 'Test Quality Gate' or multiple required terms (grep -qi 'test quality' AND grep for 'pre-commit'/'enforcement'), or specify the minimum content the section must contain.

findings.location: Description 'File Impact: CLAUDE.md — Edit' and title 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs'; AC: 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes' and 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate'
findings.finding: Two coherence gaps. (1) The title promises updating 'workflow docs' and the description mentions a 'pre-commit gate', but no acceptance criterion or File Impact entry covers any workflow doc or pre-commit gate documentation — only CLAUDE.md is listed and verified. This is described/promised work with no matching criterion. (2) The first AC 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)' is an orphan criterion: nothing in the description body explains why a docs-only change (CLAUDE.md Edit) should run/affect the test suite, nor what those tests assert about the documentation. (3) Terminology inconsistency: the work is referred to as 'test quality gate' (AC), 'test quality enforcement overlay', and 'pre-commit gate' (description) — three names for what appear to be related concepts, and the grep verifies 'test.quality' which matches none of these phrases exactly.
findings.suggested_edit: Either remove 'workflow docs' from the title and 'pre-commit gate' from the description if out of scope, or add a File Impact entry and an acceptance criterion covering the workflow doc/pre-commit gate update. Justify the run-all.sh criterion (e.g., a docs-lint test) or drop it. Standardize on one term (e.g., 'test quality gate') and ensure the grep pattern matches the actual documented phrase.

findings.location: AC: 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md' and 'bash tests/run-all.sh'; description 'documents the test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate'
findings.finding: End-state reachability gap. The described end-state is that CLAUDE.md (and per the title, workflow docs) document the test quality enforcement overlay AND pre-commit gate. The verification 'grep -q "test.quality"' only confirms a single string token appears somewhere in CLAUDE.md — it does not verify the overlay is documented, the pre-commit gate is documented, or that workflow docs were touched. The union of described steps (edit CLAUDE.md) plus the two ACs does not reach the full stated end-state: a passing grep for one token and a green test suite can both be true while the pre-commit gate and workflow docs remain undocumented. There is no clear stopping condition tied to the actual content required.
findings.suggested_edit: Add verifiable criteria for each documented element (e.g., grep for the pre-commit gate section heading and the overlay description), add a criterion for any workflow doc if in scope, and tighten the grep to the exact phrase the doc uses rather than the regex-matching token 'test.quality' (note '.' matches any char).

findings.location: File Impact: 'CLAUDE.md — Edit' vs Title/Description: 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs'
findings.finding: The plan's title and description say to update 'CLAUDE.md and workflow docs', but the File Impact section lists only CLAUDE.md. An executing agent cannot tell whether other workflow docs (e.g. a pre-commit/workflow file, CONTRIBUTING, docs/) must also be edited, or whether 'workflow docs' is just loose phrasing for CLAUDE.md. This forces a real scope decision onto the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Either remove 'and workflow docs' from the title/description, or enumerate the specific workflow doc files (paths) that must be edited in File Impact.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' / Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: The first AC ('bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)') is measurable and in-session. The second AC's verify command 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md' is a weak proxy: the regex 'test.quality' (where '.' matches any char) only checks that the string exists, not that the documentation is meaningful or covers the pre-commit gate mentioned in the description. It is mechanically verifiable in-session, but does not actually assert that the gate is properly documented. Whether the grep proxy is acceptable depends on the team's content expectations, hence AMBIGUOUS rather than FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Consider strengthening the verify to assert the substantive content, e.g. grep for both 'test quality' and 'pre-commit', and/or add a criterion that the section describes how the gate is enforced (overlay + pre-commit) rather than just that a matching string exists.

findings.location: Title: "Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs for test quality enforcement" vs Description File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit"
findings.finding: The title promises updating both "CLAUDE.md and workflow docs", but the body's File Impact section and acceptance criteria only cover CLAUDE.md. There is no mention of which "workflow docs" are to be updated, nor any acceptance criterion verifying changes to them. This is a scope mismatch: the body does less than the title promises (workflow docs are dropped). A benign reading is that "workflow docs" was intended to be folded into CLAUDE.md, but that is not stated.
findings.suggested_edit: Either remove "and workflow docs" from the title, or add the specific workflow doc file(s) to the File Impact list and add a corresponding acceptance criterion (e.g., grep verifying the relevant content in that file).

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: "bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)" / Verify: bash tests/run-all.sh, and "grep -q \"test.quality\" CLAUDE.md"
findings.finding: The plan assumes the existence of a test harness at tests/run-all.sh without stating that it exists or what it does; an executor must verify this against the actual repo. Additionally, the second AC's verify command grep -q "test.quality" presumes the literal string "test quality" (the . matches any char) will appear in CLAUDE.md, but the documentation could legitimately phrase it as "test-quality gate", "quality of tests", etc., making the grep a brittle, self-imposed constraint rather than a verified premise. These claims need confirmation against the live codebase, which is not provided here.
findings.suggested_edit: State explicitly that tests/run-all.sh already exists (or add it to File Impact), and relax/clarify the grep target to match the exact heading/string the author intends to add to CLAUDE.md so the verify command reliably reflects the documentation change.

findings.location: File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit"; Description: "workflow docs" (title) vs single-file edit set
findings.finding: The title says "Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs" (plural) and the description references the "pre-commit gate" and "test quality enforcement overlay," but the declared edit set lists only CLAUDE.md. If the enforcement overlay/pre-commit hook has its own docs (e.g., a CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/ entry, or the hook script's comments/help text), those consumers may also need updating and are not named. Additionally, AC2 greps for the literal string "test.quality" but the edit set/description does not state CLAUDE.md will contain that exact token, so the verification may not match what is written. This needs the live repo to confirm whether other workflow-doc files exist.
findings.suggested_edit: Either narrow the title to "Update CLAUDE.md for test quality enforcement" if only that file changes, or explicitly add the additional workflow-doc files to the File Impact list. Also confirm the planned CLAUDE.md text includes a string matching the AC2 grep pattern "test.quality".

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)' and 'grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md'
findings.finding: The end-state is verifiable via two concrete commands, and the union of steps (edit CLAUDE.md) plausibly reaches them. However, there is a potential gap: the first AC requires `bash tests/run-all.sh` to pass, but the plan describes only a docs edit to CLAUDE.md — it does not name a test that exercises documentation, so whether run-all.sh is even affected by this change (and thus a meaningful gate here) cannot be confirmed without codebase access. Also, the description mentions 'workflow docs' (in the title) and a 'pre-commit gate', but File Impact lists only CLAUDE.md, leaving it unclear whether other docs are in scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify File Impact to reconcile the title's 'workflow docs' with the single CLAUDE.md edit, and state which test in run-all.sh validates the documentation change (or note that run-all.sh is a general regression gate).

findings.location: Testing Mode: GREEN; AC verify steps: `bash tests/run-all.sh` and `grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md`
findings.finding: Testing Mode is GREEN and the grep-based structural assertion is a legitimate verification pattern for a docs change, so the approach is reasonable. The minor risk is that `grep -q "test.quality"` is a very loose match (the dot is a regex wildcard and any single occurrence passes), which could pass even if the documentation is incomplete or off-topic; it does not assert that the actual gate behavior/workflow is documented. No behavioral test is expected for a pure docs edit, so this is acceptable but thin.
findings.suggested_edit: Tighten the grep to a literal anchored phrase (e.g. `grep -qF 'test quality gate'` or grep for the specific section heading) so the check confirms the intended content rather than any incidental match.

findings.location: Title: "Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs for test quality enforcement" vs. File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit" and AC list
findings.finding: The title promises updating both CLAUDE.md AND "workflow docs", but the body's File Impact and acceptance criteria only cover CLAUDE.md. No workflow doc files are named, edited, or verified. This is a potential scope mismatch (the title promises more than the body delivers) — either the title overstates scope or a workflow-docs edit step was omitted.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) narrow the title to "Update CLAUDE.md for test quality enforcement", or (b) add the specific workflow doc file(s) to File Impact with a corresponding acceptance criterion. Suggestion: confirm with the author whether workflow docs are in scope.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: "bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)" and "grep -q \"test.quality\" CLAUDE.md"
findings.finding: The plan assumes tests/run-all.sh exists and is runnable, and that a "test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate" already exist to be documented. These cannot be confirmed from the plan text and would need verification against the live codebase. Additionally, the verify grep pattern "test.quality" uses a regex where '.' matches any character; if the doc text uses "test quality" or "test-quality", the grep may pass or fail in ways that don't strictly confirm the intended documentation content.
findings.suggested_edit: Verify tests/run-all.sh exists before relying on it; describe what the test quality gate/overlay actually is so the documentation content can be reviewed. Consider tightening the grep (e.g., grep -qi "test quality gate") or escaping the dot, and note the assumed pre-existing overlay/gate.

findings.location: File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit" vs. title "Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs"
findings.finding: The title promises updating both CLAUDE.md AND "workflow docs", but the File Impact section lists only CLAUDE.md as an edit target. If other workflow documentation files (e.g., docs/ or a pre-commit/CONTRIBUTING file) are intended, they are unnamed; if not, the title overstates the scope. Additionally, the acceptance check `grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md` requires the literal token to be added but the edit content/section is not specified, and whether pre-commit gate config files need to exist for run-all.sh to pass cannot be confirmed without the live repo.
findings.suggested_edit: Either list the specific 'workflow docs' files under File Impact (or remove them from the title if only CLAUDE.md changes), and name the section/heading in CLAUDE.md where the test-quality-gate text will live so the grep target is unambiguous.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' / Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: The grep verification for the documentation criterion is brittle: 'test.quality' is a regex where '.' matches any character, and a single matching string does not confirm the gate is actually 'documented' in a meaningful, complete way. It is measurable in-session (greppable) but only weakly tied to the real intent of documenting the gate and pre-commit hook. The 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes' criterion is measurable and in-session-completable. I cannot fully judge whether run-all.sh requires any host-live ops without codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Suggestion: tighten the doc check to a literal/anchored grep (e.g., grep -qF 'test quality' or grep -qi 'test quality gate' CLAUDE.md) and add criteria asserting the pre-commit gate and overlay are described (e.g., grep for the hook name/command). Optionally assert run-all.sh runs without network/host-live dependencies.

findings.location: Description: 'Update project documentation for the test quality enforcement overlay and pre-commit gate.'
findings.finding: The plan is pure mechanism: it states WHAT to update (CLAUDE.md, workflow docs) but not WHO benefits or WHAT problem the lack of documentation causes. There is an implied consumer (developers/agents relying on CLAUDE.md to follow the test quality gate), but it is never stated.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a sentence naming the beneficiary and problem, e.g., 'So that contributors/agents reading CLAUDE.md understand and comply with the test quality gate and pre-commit hook, preventing low-quality tests from being committed.'

findings.location: Acceptance criterion: 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' / Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md; Description: 'Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs'
findings.finding: Two coherence gaps. (1) Title and Description reference 'workflow docs' ('Update CLAUDE.md and workflow docs for test quality enforcement') and a 'pre-commit gate', but the File Impact lists only CLAUDE.md and no acceptance criterion covers any workflow doc or pre-commit gate file — that described work has no corresponding criterion (orphan described work). (2) The Description mentions a 'pre-commit gate' but no criterion verifies it is documented; the only doc criterion checks the generic term 'test quality gate'. (3) The verify command grep pattern 'test.quality' (a regex matching 'test', any char, 'quality') does not necessarily match the criterion's prose 'test quality gate' nor the Description's 'test quality enforcement overlay' — terminology is inconsistent across body, criterion text, and verify command.
findings.suggested_edit: Either remove 'workflow docs' / 'pre-commit gate' from the title/description to match the CLAUDE.md-only scope, or add File Impact entries plus acceptance criteria covering those files. Align the documented term consistently (e.g. 'test quality enforcement') across the description, criterion text, and the grep pattern so the verify command actually matches the intended phrase.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: "bash tests/run-all.sh passes (exit 0)" and "grep -q \"test.quality\" CLAUDE.md"
Description / File Impact: "CLAUDE.md — Edit"
Acceptance Criteria: "grep -q \"test.quality\" CLAUDE.md"
findings.finding: The grep pattern "test.quality" uses a regex where '.' matches any character; it's a thin check that confirms a string exists but not that the gate is actually documented meaningfully. The first AC (run-all.sh exit 0) is measurable and in-session completable. The second is verifiable but a weak proxy for the stated goal of 'documents test quality gate' — a single matching line passes regardless of substance. Both criteria are completable in one session.
findings.suggested_edit: Consider strengthening the verify to grep for a more specific phrase (e.g. "pre-commit" plus "test quality") or require a named section heading, to ensure the documentation is substantive rather than a single incidental match.

findings.location: Description: "Update project documentation ... File Impact: CLAUDE.md — Edit"
Acceptance criterion: 'CLAUDE.md documents test quality gate' with Verify: grep -q "test.quality" CLAUDE.md
findings.finding: The grep pattern uses a regex where '.' matches any character, so it would match 'test quality', 'test-quality', 'test.quality', etc. — but the criterion's intent ('documents test quality gate') is broader than what the literal grep confirms. The grep only proves a single token sequence exists, not that the gate/pre-commit overlay is actually documented. The check is measurable and in-session-completable, but the AC and its verify command are weakly coupled: a doc that merely mentions 'test quality' in passing would pass without documenting the gate.

findings.location: Summary: "Add a PostToolUse hook to plugins/dso/hooks/dispatchers/post-bash.sh (or post-all.sh)" and AC "Hook registered in consolidated dispatcher"
findings.finding: The primary edit target is expressed as an unresolved either/or: post-bash.sh OR post-all.sh. This ambiguity matters because a SUGGESTION: sentinel comes from an Agent tool return, not a Bash tool return — post-bash.sh would likely be the wrong file, since Agent and Bash are distinct tools with distinct PostToolUse events. The plan does not name the consolidated dispatcher file it must register the hook in, nor the matcher/event that scopes the hook to Agent (vs Bash/all) tool returns. It also references suggestion-record.sh without giving its path, so its existence/signature cannot be confirmed. Whether post-bash.sh receives Agent returns, what the dispatcher's CONFIDENT/UNCERTAIN registration mechanism requires, and whether suggestion-record.sh exists all require live-codebase inspection.
findings.suggested_edit: Resolve the post-bash.sh vs post-all.sh choice explicitly by stating which dispatcher receives Agent (not Bash) tool returns; name the exact dispatcher file and the matcher/event used to scope to Agent returns; and give the full path and expected CLI signature of suggestion-record.sh so its existence can be verified before implementation.

findings.location: Summary: "Add a PostToolUse hook to plugins/dso/hooks/dispatchers/post-bash.sh (or post-all.sh)"
findings.finding: The plan defers a real design decision to the executor: which dispatcher file should host the hook (post-bash.sh OR post-all.sh). These are different files for different tool events; an Agent tool return is not a Bash event, so the choice materially affects whether the hook ever fires. The executor would have to guess or stop to clarify. Additionally, the acceptance criterion 'Hook registered in consolidated dispatcher' references the 'consolidated dispatcher architecture (CONFIDENT/UNCERTAIN pattern)' without specifying which dispatcher or what the registration mechanism is.
findings.suggested_edit: Resolve the file choice explicitly: name the single dispatcher file that handles PostToolUse on Agent tool returns (e.g., 'post-all.sh, because Agent returns are not Bash events'), and describe the concrete registration step (where in the dispatcher the hook is invoked).

findings.location: Summary: "records it via suggestion-record.sh" and "Integrates with consolidated dispatcher architecture (CONFIDENT/UNCERTAIN pattern)"
findings.finding: The plan assumes the existence and interface of suggestion-record.sh (its CLI/args for recording a suggestion) and the existence of a 'consolidated dispatcher' with a CONFIDENT/UNCERTAIN pattern that supports registering a PostToolUse hook on Agent tool returns. It also assumes PostToolUse hooks can intercept Agent tool returns and access the return payload. These claims can only be confirmed against the live codebase, which I cannot inspect. An executor would need to verify suggestion-record.sh's invocation contract and how the dispatcher exposes Agent-tool return content before relying on them.
findings.suggested_edit: Add verification steps and concrete interface details: cite the actual signature/usage of suggestion-record.sh, confirm post-* dispatcher reads Agent tool return payloads, and document how a PostToolUse hook is registered in the consolidated dispatcher (with a pointer to an existing example hook).

findings.location: ## Summary and ## Context: 'Add a PostToolUse hook ... that intercepts Agent tool returns, extracts the first SUGGESTION: sentinel per return, and records it via suggestion-record.sh'
findings.finding: The plan is described almost entirely as mechanism (which hook file, what sentinel, which script to call). It does not explicitly state WHO benefits or WHAT problem the SUGGESTION: extraction solves — e.g. why sub-agent suggestions need to be captured and recorded, and who consumes the recorded suggestions. The story reference (.claude/scripts/dso ticket show 95f8-7ec4) likely contains this, but it is not surfaced in the plan itself. An implied technical consumer (the suggestion-record.sh pipeline / a human reviewing recorded suggestions) is plausibly inferable, which keeps this from being a hard FAIL.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence in Context naming the beneficiary and problem, e.g. 'Sub-agents emit SUGGESTION: lines that are currently lost; this hook captures them so [maintainers/the dso workflow] can review/act on suggestions surfaced during Agent tool runs.'

findings.location: ## Summary and ## Context sections
findings.finding: The plan is largely mechanism-focused (extract SUGGESTION: sentinel, call suggestion-record.sh, register in dispatcher) and does not explicitly state WHO benefits or WHAT problem is solved. There is an implied technical consumer (the SUGGESTION sentinel feeds suggestion-record.sh, presumably surfacing sub-agent suggestions to a downstream consumer), which counts for a low-level task, but the user-facing value/why is deferred to an external story (95f8-7ec4) accessible only via `.claude/scripts/dso ticket show`. Cannot fully verify the problem statement without that external context.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence stating the beneficiary and problem, e.g. 'So that suggestions emitted by sub-agents are captured and surfaced to <consumer/user> instead of being lost in the Agent tool return.'

findings.location: AC: 'Tests cover sentinel extraction edge cases' and Testing Mode: RED
findings.finding: The plan declares RED mode (RED-before-GREEN intent is present) and names a test entry point (bash tests/run-all.sh), which is good. However, the testing approach is under-specified: 'edge cases' is named but not enumerated. The behavioral contract implies several boundary cases that should be explicitly listed — e.g., multiple SUGGESTION: lines in one return (only the FIRST should be extracted), zero SUGGESTION: lines (no record, no crash), malformed sentinel (warn path), SUGGESTION: embedded mid-text vs at line start, empty payload after SUGGESTION:, and non-Agent tool returns (hook should not fire). Without naming these, the test suite risks being a happy-path/change-detector rather than contractual coverage. Confirming the actual edge-case list would require reading the linked story (95f8-7ec4) which is not accessible here.
findings.suggested_edit: Enumerate the specific edge cases the tests must cover: (1) first-of-multiple SUGGESTION lines extracted, (2) no sentinel present -> no record + clean exit, (3) malformed sentinel -> warn, no crash, non-zero handling defined, (4) empty/whitespace payload, (5) non-Agent tool return ignored. Tie each to an assertion in run-all.sh.

findings.location: Summary: 'post-bash.sh (or post-all.sh)'; AC: 'Hook registered in consolidated dispatcher'; Verify: 'bash tests/run-all.sh'
findings.finding: Termination is clearly defined via 'bash tests/run-all.sh passes' with an exact reproduce command, so a stop condition exists. State that these tests should fail before implementation.

findings.location: Context: references story 95f8-7ec4; implied consumer is suggestion-record.sh / sub-agent suggestion workflow

findings.location: File Impact table + Implementation Notes (only edits plugins/dso/scripts/dso_ci_review/runner.py)

findings.finding: The plan asserts T1 tests should turn GREEN but does not specify the behavioral coverage those tests must assert, and several exit-code-contract branches from DD2 have no corresponding test. Specifically there is no described test for: rc==1 → schema_fail (stderr lines parsed into errors), TimeoutExpired → validator_error + CRITICAL + non-zero exit, FileNotFoundError(ENOENT)/PermissionError(EACCES)/other OSError → validator_error, and 'other non-zero exit' → validator_error. The validator_error AC verify block is a no-op (it ends in `pass # full main() integration tested in T1 tests` and only prints 'Structure check: OK'), so it does not actually exercise the CRITICAL-to-stderr/return-1 contract. RED-before-GREEN ordering is also not stated even though DD2 demands these failure paths 'never be silently skipped'.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit test cases (or enumerate them in the plan) covering each DD2 branch: (1) exit 0→pass, (2) exit 1→schema_fail with parsed stderr lines and NO CRITICAL/return-1, (3) exit N (other)→validator_error→CRITICAL+return 1, (4) TimeoutExpired→validator_error, (5) FileNotFoundError→validator_error, (6) PermissionError→validator_error, plus a tmpfile-cleanup-on-error assertion (try/finally). Replace the no-op validator_error AC with one that actually patches _validate_findings_schema to return validator_error, runs main(), and asserts return==1 and 'CRITICAL' in captured stderr. State that T1 tests are written RED first (Testing Mode UPDATE).

findings.finding: Most criteria are measurable and in-session (pytest, ruff, grep, importability). Two are weak as written. The 'schema_fail path continues' check `grep -A 10 "schema_fail" ... | grep -v "CRITICAL\|return 1" | grep -q "schema_fail"` only confirms a schema_fail-related line exists that itself lacks CRITICAL/return 1 on that line — it does not actually prove the control-flow path avoids returning 1 for schema_fail. The 'validator_error path emits CRITICAL and returns non-zero' check ends with `pass # full main() integration tested in T1 tests` and prints 'OK' regardless, so it asserts nothing. The real assurance is deferred to the 'All T1 tests now pass' criterion, which is measurable. Confirm T1 tests actually cover these two paths.

findings.location: Scope Boundary section (IN/OUT scope) and single-file File Impact
DD2 / 'NOTE on S-B integration' vs main() insertion snippet comment 'schema_fail: leave merged unchanged'
findings.finding: Minor cross-section tension around the schema_fail handoff. DD2 says exit 1 is a 'schema-fail signal handed off to the correction handoff point (S-B integration point)', and the NOTE says S-A 'should expose _schema_result so S-B can intercept it'. But the main() insertion snippet handles only the validator_error branch and then has a comment-only schema_fail path; `_schema_result` is a local variable that is not returned, stored, or otherwise exposed beyond the function scope, so there is no concrete mechanism for S-B to 'intercept' it. The plan's stated 'simplest contract' (continue to _write_output unchanged, 'S-B will wrap the call site') is internally consistent enough to not block, but the phrase 'expose _schema_result so S-B can intercept' is not realized by the provided code. Reconcile whether S-A must leave any structural seam or whether the local variable is sufficient.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) drop the 'expose _schema_result so S-B can intercept' language and state plainly that S-A only inserts the call and the validator_error/pass branches, with schema_fail falling through unchanged for S-B to later wrap; or (b) specify the concrete seam S-A leaves (e.g., a named block/marker comment or a helper return) so S-B has something defined to wrap.

The plan helpfully instructs the sub-agent to read both shell scripts to confirm args/stdin/env, which mitigates (c). These cannot be confirmed without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Keep the explicit instruction to read both shell scripts; additionally flag the exit-code-1==schema-fail contract and the schema-hash value as assumptions to confirm against validate-review-output.sh before relying on the routing.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria block vs Implementation Notes (exception routing) and Story DD Coverage (DD2)
findings.finding: Coverage gaps between criteria and description: (1) The implementation notes specify distinct exception routing for TimeoutExpired, FileNotFoundError, PermissionError, and OSError, but no acceptance criterion verifies any of these paths beyond the generic validator_error structure check. (2) DD2's requirement that signal-termination / other non-zero exits produce a non-zero CI exit is only partially covered — the validator_error criterion checks CRITICAL emission but the grep-based verify is a no-op. (3) The 'schema_fail path continues' verify (`grep -A 10 schema_fail ... | grep -v 'CRITICAL\|return 1' | grep -q schema_fail`) is fragile: it can pass even if a 'CRITICAL'/'return 1' appears within the schema_fail region as long as one matching line survives the inverse grep. Terminology is otherwise consistent (validator_error/schema_fail/pass used uniformly). No orphan criteria found.
findings.suggested_edit: Add criteria (or fold into T1 tests) covering the timeout/ENOENT/EACCES branches, and tighten the schema_fail grep to assert the absence of a return-1 in the schema_fail block rather than relying on line-filtering survival. Make the validator_error verify actually exercise main() rather than a structural no-op.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'validator_error path emits CRITICAL to stderr and returns non-zero' AC, and final AC 'All T1 tests now pass'
findings.finding: The plan's testing coverage for the core behavioral contract is thin and partly inert. The validator_error AC's verify snippet sets up mocks but ends in `pass # full main() integration tested in T1 tests` and only prints 'Structure check: OK' — it asserts nothing about the CRITICAL stderr emission or the non-zero return code it claims to verify. The actual behavioral coverage is deferred to 'T1 tests' (test_validate_findings_schema / test_main_validator_error / test_main_schema_fail) which are referenced but never described or specified in the plan body. Several DD-mandated failure paths (exit 1 schema_fail, other non-zero exit, ENOENT/EACCES, TimeoutExpired, tmpfile cleanup on error) have no corresponding test description, so an executor cannot tell whether these branches are covered. In UPDATE testing mode RED-before-GREEN is implied by 'All T1 tests now pass (GREEN after implementation)' but the RED state / test authorship is not described.
findings.suggested_edit: Either describe the T1 test cases (one per exit-code/exception branch: pass, schema_fail, other-nonzero, ENOENT, EACCES, TimeoutExpired, plus tmpfile cleanup) in the plan body, or make the validator_error AC actually assert behavior (e.g., invoke main() under mocks and assert returncode==1 and 'CRITICAL' in captured stderr) instead of `pass`/print('OK').

findings.location: Implementation Notes — 'IMPORTANT: Before finalizing the invocation, the sub-agent MUST read ... write-reviewer-findings.sh and validate-review-output.sh'; Pattern Reference lines 150-159; 'Schema hash: 214949ee476be6d0'; '5-dirname-levels from __file__'

The plan does flag (a)/(b) for verification (the 'MUST read' note), which is good, but the exit-code routing semantics (rc==1 specifically means schema_fail) is asserted as fact in the routing table and is the load-bearing premise of DD2.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit verification step/AC that the executor confirms validate-review-output.sh's actual exit codes (e.g., grep the script for `exit 1`/`exit 0` paths) and that 5-level dirname resolution yields plugins/dso before relying on the routing table; note these as assumptions-to-confirm rather than facts.

(2) The final AC references named tests 'test_validate_findings_schema or test_main_validator_error or test_main_schema_fail' that appear nowhere in the description body (no T1 test authoring is described), so this criterion is effectively an orphan with no corresponding described work. findings.location: Implementation Notes → 'IMPORTANT: Before finalizing the invocation, the sub-agent MUST read ... validate-review-output.sh to confirm: (a) exact args order ... (b) whether stdin vs file input ... (c) any required env vars'

On a benign reading this is a verification/audit step rather than an unresolved design decision: the plan already commits to the concrete invocation (['bash', validator_script, 'code-review-dispatch', tmp_path], file-path-as-arg, no stdin), grounded by the write-reviewer-findings.sh:150-159 reference and the AC grep check. findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'schema_fail path continues (does NOT emit CRITICAL, does NOT return 1)' with Verify: grep -A 10 "schema_fail" ... | grep -v "CRITICAL\|return 1" | grep -q "schema_fail"
findings.finding: The verify command for the schema_fail AC is effectively non-discriminating and will pass even on a wrong implementation. Because the inserted code contains multiple comment lines mentioning 'schema_fail' (e.g. '# schema_fail: leave merged unchanged ...'), the pipeline `grep -A 10 schema_fail | grep -v 'CRITICAL|return 1' | grep -q schema_fail` succeeds as long as ANY line in the window contains 'schema_fail' and is not itself a CRITICAL/return-1 line — which the comment lines guarantee. It does not actually verify that the schema_fail branch lacks a `return 1` or CRITICAL print. The end-state ('S-B integration point preserved') is therefore not reliably reachable via this check; the real guarantee rests on the T1 tests (test_main_schema_fail). Recommend tightening or relying on the T1 test as the authoritative gate.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace the grep heuristic with the existing T1 test (test_main_schema_fail) as the authoritative AC, e.g. assert main() returns 0 and stderr contains no 'CRITICAL' when _validate_findings_schema returns status=='schema_fail'.

Under a benign reading these line up, so this is a minor consistency note, not scope drift.

findings.location: Insertion Point code block (`return 1` only in validator_error branch) vs NOTE on S-B integration: 'after _validate_findings_schema(), if status == "schema_fail", continue to _write_output() with the original findings'
findings.finding: There is a coherence gap around how the schema_fail result is 'exposed' to S-B. DD1 says validation happens BEFORE _write_output(), and the NOTE says S-A 'should expose _schema_result so S-B can intercept it' / 'S-B will wrap the call site'. But the inserted code assigns _schema_result to a local variable in main() and does nothing with it on the schema_fail path — there is no described mechanism (return value, stored attribute, structured object) by which S-B can later 'intercept' it without re-editing main(). The plan's two statements ('expose so S-B can intercept' vs 'just continue, S-B will wrap') are mutually consistent only if S-B is expected to re-edit this exact spot, which contradicts 'expose'. This is internal tension that should be resolved so the executor knows whether to leave a TODO/hook or simply fall through.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the plan that S-A's contract is simply 'fall through to _write_output() on schema_fail with a documented code comment marking the S-B interception point' (no exposure mechanism), OR specify the concrete exposure mechanism (e.g., return the result, or set a named local consumed by a later block). Pick one and make both the NOTE and the code block agree.

findings.location: Insertion Point in main() code block vs. NOTE on S-B integration

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'schema_fail path continues ... Verify: grep -A 10 "schema_fail" ... | grep -v "CRITICAL\|return 1" | grep -q "schema_fail"'
findings.finding: This grep is a weak/unreliable measure of the intended behavior. The plan body itself contains the lines 'CRITICAL: schema validator failed' and 'return 1' in the validator_error branch, and the comment block mentions 'schema_fail' multiple times. The pipeline `grep -A 10 schema_fail | grep -v 'CRITICAL|return 1' | grep -q schema_fail` only checks that *some* line within 10 lines of a 'schema_fail' match survives the CRITICAL/return-1 filter and itself contains 'schema_fail' — it does not actually verify that the schema_fail code path avoids emitting CRITICAL or returning 1. It can pass even if the path is wrong. Other greps (timeout, code-review-dispatch, hash) are measurable and fine.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace the grep with a behavioral test: patch _validate_findings_schema to return _SchemaValidationResult('schema_fail', [...]), run main(), and assert no 'CRITICAL' on stderr and return code != 1 (i.e., proceeds to _write_output). This mirrors the validator_error structural test already present.

The contract 'expose _schema_result so S-B can intercept it' is not realized by the snippet (the variable is not returned, stored, or passed onward), creating a gap between the stated S-B integration contract and the actual code shape.
findings.suggested_edit: Either add an explicit `if _schema_result.status == "schema_fail":` branch with the documenting comment at the exact handoff site, or clarify in the plan that S-B's seam is simply the call site of _validate_findings_schema (the variable need not be persisted), so the 'expose _schema_result' wording matches the actual fall-through design.

findings.location: Acceptance criteria for schema_fail path: grep -A 10 "schema_fail" ... | grep -v "CRITICAL\|return 1" | grep -q "schema_fail"
findings.finding: The schema_fail AC uses a grep pipeline that is logically weak: `grep -A 10 schema_fail | grep -v 'CRITICAL|return 1' | grep -q schema_fail` will pass merely because the literal token 'schema_fail' appears somewhere in the 10-line window of any line containing 'schema_fail' (e.g. the comment block itself). Similarly the validator_error AC (criterion with `pass # full main() integration tested in T1`) only prints 'Structure check: OK' and asserts nothing about behavior. findings.location: Title vs Implementation Notes / Insertion Point in main()

findings.finding: Two internal coherence issues. (1) The schema_fail handling is described inconsistently: the Insertion Point code block does NOT contain any branch that writes output for schema_fail (it only handles validator_error then falls through), while the NOTE says 'continue to _write_output() with the original findings'. The fall-through happens to achieve that, but the code comment in the block ('schema_fail: leave merged unchanged; S-B story will intercept') and the AC grep both assume distinct schema_fail handling — an implementer could be confused about whether schema_fail must short-circuit or fall through. findings.location: Approach sections vs. Success Criteria / Closure Checks; specifically the bootstrap design ('banded bootstrap', 'risk tier low/high', 'per-class reviewer attestation', 'First-week tightening: ≤10 mutations/pass for 7 days'), Conflict policy (per-element provenance, jira-* mirroring), Snapshot-isolation/concurrency contracts, and the 'two consecutive passes before auto-merge' invariant vs SC5
findings.finding: Several substantial described work-items have no corresponding acceptance criterion (orphan description), and at least one criterion conflicts with the described design. (1) The detailed bootstrap workflow — per-class dry-run manifest, explicit per-class reviewer attestation, separate bulk pass for ~1,300 low-risk vs drip-feed ~300 high-risk, and the 'First-week tightening: ≤10 mutations/pass + per-pass operator review for 7 days' — has no Success Criterion or Closure Check verifying it; SC1 only checks the >=80% anomaly drop. (2) The rich conflict policy (per-element provenance tagging origin:local|jira, 'removed locally' vs 'never existed locally', local-wins-on-destructive) has no dedicated criterion. (3) The snapshot-isolation contract and tickets-branch rebase-then-retry strategy are unverified by any criterion. (4) Terminology/coherence conflict: the design states the at-most-one-dso_local_id invariant 'requires 2 consecutive passes before auto-merge fires (grace window)', but SC5 asserts the duplicate property 'is restored to single value by the pass-2 auto-merge' after a single injected violation in one pass — these are reconcilable only if pass-1 detects and pass-2 merges, which SC5 should state explicitly rather than triggering 'one reconciler pass' then asserting same-pass auto-file plus pass-2 merge.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit criteria (or fold into existing ones) for: the banded bootstrap with per-class attestation, the first-week ≤10-mutations/pass tightening, and the per-element provenance conflict policy. Reword SC5 to make the two-pass grace window explicit: 'pass-1 detects the dso_local_id invariant violation and files the bug (no merge, grace window); pass-2 auto-merges to single value.' Also confirm every named workflow file name is consistent — note the inconsistency below.

findings.location: Implementation Notes: 'mirror write-reviewer-findings.sh exact args' and 'mirror _classify_tier_via_bash() pattern exactly'; Pattern Reference citing write-reviewer-findings.sh:150-159 and runner.py:424-526

The plan commendably instructs the sub-agent to READ those files before finalizing, which mitigates the risk, but these premises themselves are unverified here.

It patches mocks and then `pass`es, deferring the real check to the T1 tests AC. This is a near-duplicate of the final 'All T1 tests now pass' AC and provides no independent verification, making it an orphan/no-op criterion.

schema_fail handling: Insertion Point comment 'leave merged unchanged; S-B story will intercept' / NOTE on S-B: 'continue to _write_output() with the original findings' vs DD2 'exit 1 → schema-fail signal handed off to the correction handoff point'

findings.suggested_edit: Make the handoff contract concrete: e.g. specify that _schema_result remains in scope at the _write_output() call (and is not shadowed), or add an explicit structured marker/return that S-B will hook, so 'integration point preserved' is verifiable rather than implied.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; Success Criterion 5 references `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property`; Closure Check references `dso_reconciler/invariants.py`; Dependencies cite 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions an executor must confirm before relying on them, and which I cannot verify from plan text alone: (1) that `acli-integration.AcliClient` exposes a `set_issue_property` method (used as the SC5 verification mechanism); (2) that label JQL `labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"` reliably matches given that Jira labels cannot contain spaces/colons unmodified — note SC3 verification uses a different label pattern `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` (hyphen, LIKE) than the design's `dso-id:<uuid>` (colon, exact match), an internal inconsistency suggesting the actual label format is unverified; (3) capability/forward-compat probes are described as pre-cutover steps but their outcomes (does old bridge crash on new fields?) are assumed-resolvable rather than verified. These should be confirmed against the live codebase/Jira instance.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile the label format: state the exact sanitized label string written and searched, and make SC3's JQL (`labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"`) use the identical token as the design's `dso-id:<uuid>`. Add an explicit pre-flight assertion that `AcliClient.set_issue_property` (and the property-read endpoint) exist in the current acli-integration module before SC5 can run.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' preamble and SC2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' replaced by 'three times back-to-back, not separated by 20min'); SC7 ('ALREADY SATISFIED — user explicit override'); Closure Checks ('5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations' replacing 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%'; 'deterministic absence-of-runs' replacing 7-day soak); heartbeat 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004'.
findings.finding: There is a genuine gap between several stated end-states and the verification that proves them, because the verification protocol substitutes weaker in-session analogs for the durability properties the criteria actually assert. Three concrete examples: (a) SC2 asserts parity that 'holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' but the analog runs passes back-to-back with a single injected update — this does not exercise the snapshot-isolation/HEAD-advanced-mid-pass behavior the design hinges on, so 'durable parity' is not actually reached. (b) The Closure Check replaces 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%' with '5 consecutive green runs' and claims this is 'stronger evidence'; 5 runs cannot establish a <1% failure rate (which needs ~100+ samples), so the done-condition is not met by the substitute. (c) SC7 and the heartbeat check are declared pre-satisfied by override/prior stories rather than verified within this plan's scope, leaving rollback-executability and heartbeat-firing unverified for the delivered system. The cron-cadence/schedule end-state ('runs on the configured ~20-min schedule') is also never verified — only workflow_dispatch is exercised, so scheduled triggering is asserted but untested.
findings.suggested_edit: For each substituted criterion, either (i) retain at least one verification that exercises the durability property (e.g., run SC2 across passes separated by real cadence with a scheduled trigger, and confirm at least one scheduled (non-dispatch) run fired), or (ii) explicitly downgrade the criterion's claimed strength to match the analog (e.g., reword to 'no failures observed across 5 consecutive runs' rather than implying a <1% rate). For SC7/heartbeat, link the closing evidence artifacts and state the residual unverified gap (e.g., DD3 'old bridges run 1 scheduled cycle post-revert') explicitly as accepted risk.

findings.location: Identity model: "JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning." Success Criterion 5: "REST-PUT a second `dso_local_id` value via `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property`". External Dependencies: "acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified".
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions an executor must confirm against the live system before relying on them: (1) that label JQL `labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"` works while entity-property JQL does not on this Jira Cloud instance — note Jira labels do not permit colons, so `dso-id:<uuid>` as a literal label value is questionable and the JQL example `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` in SC3 uses a hyphen form that is inconsistent with the colon form `dso-id:<uuid>` used in the Identity model; (2) the existence of helper `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property`; (3) that the capability probe will actually round-trip property read/write. I cannot verify these against the live Jira/codebase, but the label-format claim in particular is a load-bearing assumption for the entire identity model.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-implementation check that the chosen label encoding is JQL-searchable on Jira Cloud (labels reject spaces/colons), and reconcile the colon form `dso-id:<uuid>` against the hyphen form `dso-id-*` used in SC3. Confirm `AcliClient.set_issue_property` exists or name the module to add it.

findings.location: Success Criteria preamble "All Success Criteria are verified in-session...Time-based requirements have been replaced with equivalent in-session analogs"; SC2 "holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity" replaced by back-to-back dispatches; Closure Check "5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations" replacing "rolling 30-day failure rate <1%"; SC7 "ALREADY SATISFIED — ... user explicit override".
findings.finding: There are real gaps between 'what we'll do' and several stated end-states. (a) SC2's durability requirement ("durable", "spanning normal local activity", across the ~20-min cadence) is materially weakened by running three passes back-to-back via workflow_dispatch and a single injected update — this does not exercise the snapshot-isolation/HEAD-advanced-mid-pass path nor the 20-min scheduled cadence, so the convergence-under-real-activity end-state is not actually reached by the verification. (b) The Closure Check substituting 5 sequential green runs for a rolling 30-day <1% failure rate is asserted to be "stronger evidence," but 5 runs cannot detect a <1% failure rate; the stated end-state (durable reliability) is not demonstrably reached. (c) SC7 and parts of CC3/heartbeat are marked ALREADY SATISFIED / VERIFIED via prior-story override rather than by steps in this plan, and DD3 ("old bridges run 1 scheduled cycle post-revert") is explicitly conceded "structurally unverifiable" — meaning the rollback end-state is asserted, not verified. Net: the union of described steps does not fully reach the durability/reliability/rollback end-states as originally specified; termination is clear but several 'done' signals are surrogate evidence weaker than the criteria they replace.
findings.suggested_edit: For SC2, retain at least one scheduled-cadence run (or explicitly run a pass after a real local commit lands on the tickets branch HEAD mid-pass) so the snapshot-isolation path is exercised; state the residual risk explicitly. For the reliability Closure Check, keep the in-session 5-run check but add a post-cutover scheduled-run observation window (even short) and label the 5-run result as a smoke gate, not equivalent to <1% over 30d. For SC7/DD3, flag the rollback verification as carrying an accepted unverified residual rather than 'satisfied'.

findings.location: ## Context section: "Project maintainers (and any future DSO operators on Jira-mirrored projects ...) need a sync that converges from full snapshots"; "operators currently cannot trust the bridge's audit (bridge-fsck shows 1,482 anomalies with no remediation path)"
Whole plan; e.g. Identity model, 429/5xx/401 contracts, Bootstrap, Success Criteria with concrete verification commands

External Dependencies: "External heartbeat monitor ... ownership: user_setup_required; handling: user_manual; claude_has_access: depends on chosen service" and Out-of-band observability: "status-badge monitor or scheduled-canary GitHub Action". Also Success Criteria preamble: "All Success Criteria are verified in-session ... Time-based requirements have been replaced with equivalent in-session analogs."
findings.finding: The plan is overwhelmingly detailed and executable, but two areas leave a real design decision to the executor. (1) The heartbeat monitor mechanism is left as an open choice ("status-badge monitor or scheduled-canary GitHub Action", "depends on chosen service") and is user_setup_required, so the agent cannot fully complete the out-of-band observability portion without a clarification about which service/who configures it. (2) The Closure Check for the heartbeat is already marked "VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004" while the dependency is still user_setup_required — these two statements are in tension and an executor may be unsure whether the heartbeat work remains to be done. These are minor because the core reconciler work is fully specified and the heartbeat is an isolated, deferrable add-on with a plausible benign reading (already-configured canary).
findings.suggested_edit: Resolve the tension by stating explicitly whether the heartbeat monitor is already configured (and therefore out of scope for this ticket) or still needs setup; if the latter, name the specific service/mechanism (e.g., scheduled-canary GitHub Action committed in this repo) rather than leaving it as an open "or" choice, and clarify the user-vs-agent split.

findings.location: Success Criteria SC2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity'), SC4 ('2 consecutive passes on converged state'), Closure Check CC3 ('reconcile-bridge.yml runs on the configured ~20-min schedule with 5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations'), and the 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' header replacing all time-based requirements with in-session analogs.
findings.finding: Several criteria are originally written as durability/soak requirements that are inherently out-of-session, and the plan resolves them by unilaterally redefining the verification protocol to in-session analogs. Two specific issues: (1) The schedule/cron behaviour itself is never actually verified — CC3 substitutes 5 back-to-back workflow_dispatch runs for the '~20-min schedule' requirement, but workflow_dispatch does not exercise the cron trigger, so 'runs on the configured ~20-min schedule' remains unverified in-session. Similarly the Closure Check 'no scheduled runs occur after cutover' is asserted as 'structural impossibility' rather than observed. (2) SC2's 'spanning normal local activity' across passes separated by real 20-min cadence is replaced by a single injected update between passes 2 and 3 run back-to-back; this is a weaker analog and should be flagged as not equivalent to the stated durability requirement. Additionally SC7 and parts of CC3/heartbeat are marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED' / 'VERIFIED' by prior-story closure and a 'user explicit override', which means those acceptance criteria are not independently verifiable within this ticket's session and rely on out-of-session evidence and authorization.
findings.suggested_edit: For criteria that genuinely require cron/schedule behaviour ('runs on the configured ~20-min schedule', 'no scheduled runs after cutover'), either (a) add an explicit in-session test that validates the cron syntax and trigger wiring (e.g. assert the workflow's `on.schedule` block is present and parseable, plus a single real scheduled fire observed), or (b) restate the criterion as 'cron configured + N manual runs green' and acknowledge the residual gap. For SC2, state explicitly that the back-to-back+injection analog is an approximation and add a follow-up verification of at least one real cadence-separated pass. For SC7/heartbeat items marked ALREADY SATISFIED, keep them but flag that they depend on prior-story evidence and a user override rather than in-session execution.

findings.location: Success Criteria 1-6, Closure Checks (git ls-tree / gh api / grep assertions), External Dependencies capability probe, and the 'tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh' harness.
Title "Replace edge-triggered Jira bridge with level-triggered reconciler" vs. Approach "Replace both event-driven bridge workflows ... with a single level-triggered reconciler workflow"
findings.finding: The plan body faithfully serves the title: it deletes the two edge-triggered workflows and introduces a level-triggered reconciler that converges from full snapshots, directly addressing the four structural causes named in Context. Scope is large but coherent and self-consistent; no contradiction between headline intent and detailed steps. One minor scope-drift note (not a failure): Success Criteria 7 and several Closure Checks are marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED'/'VERIFIED' by prior-story override rather than verified by this plan, and the 2026-05-24 verification-protocol revision substitutes in-session analogs for the originally time-based durability/soak criteria — this materially weakens the empirical strength of 'durable parity', '30-day failure rate', and '7-day no-run' guarantees the title's reliability intent implies, but does not redirect scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line note acknowledging that in-session analogs (e.g., 3 back-to-back passes for 'durable parity', 5 sequential runs for '30-day failure rate') reduce wall-clock soak confidence, and flag which durability claims remain unproven until real cadence runs accumulate post-merge.

findings.location: Approach: "New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)"; Closure Checks reference "dso_reconciler/invariants.py"; deletion targets bridge-outbound.py/.yml, bridge-inbound.py, inbound-bridge.yml, _outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, _inbound_api

However two items need live-codebase confirmation and one is internally inconsistent. (1) Naming inconsistency: the Approach lists modules fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health, but Closure Checks/SC5 reference `dso_reconciler/invariants.py` (the at-most-one-dso_local_id invariant enforcement) which is not in the declared module list — either add `invariants` to the package module list or fold it into an existing module. (2) Workflow filename inconsistency: Approach/cutover names files as `bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py` and `reconcile-bridge.yml`, while the first Closure Check refers to `bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml` (outbound vs inbound naming asymmetry: bridge-outbound vs inbound-bridge) — confirm exact on-disk filenames so the deletion check matches. (3) Blast radius for grep-purge of `_outbound_cursor`/`_outbound_handlers`/`_inbound_api`/`BRIDGE_ENV_ID` consumers across plugins/dso, .github, .claude cannot be confirmed without the codebase; the plan does acknowledge BRIDGE_ENV_ID retention, which is good. Cannot assert any target is hallucinated without repo access.
