findings.location: Success Criteria header 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision): ... verified in-session via live execution against the deployed reconcile-bridge.yml workflow on origin/main' vs Approach '### Cutover and rollback — Hard cutover: single commit deletes both event-driven workflows and enables reconcile-bridge.yml' and SC7/CC entries marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED'/'VERIFIED 2026-05-24'
findings.finding: There is a temporal/coherence contradiction between the plan-as-implementation and the verification protocol. The Success Criteria and Closure Checks repeatedly assert that artifacts are 'deployed' on origin/main and that several criteria are ALREADY SATISFIED/VERIFIED (SC7 story 7339 closed; CC heartbeat VERIFIED via story 7004; CC3 canary). Yet the Approach describes the cutover as a future single-commit action and the whole package as new work. A pre-execution plan cannot coherently both propose to build/cutover the reconciler AND claim its deployed workflow has already been live-verified in-session. Either the work is partially done (in which case the Approach should state current state and remaining work) or the 'VERIFIED/ALREADY SATISFIED' annotations are premature. Additionally SC3's JQL uses `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` (hyphen) while the identity model defines the label as `dso-id:<uuid>` (colon) and elsewhere `labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"` — the verification query pattern is inconsistent with the defined label format.
findings.suggested_edit: Separate 'plan' from 'evidence': remove or quarantine ALREADY-SATISFIED/VERIFIED annotations until execution, OR add an explicit 'Current state' subsection stating which artifacts already exist on origin/main and which remain to be built, so reviewers/agents are not told to verify something the plan also says will be created. Fix SC3 JQL to match the defined label format (`dso-id:` colon, e.g. `labels NOT IN ('dso-id:...')` or the correct wildcard).

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' and SC2 ('spanning normal local activity component is replaced by injecting a deliberate local-side ticket update'); External Dependencies 'External heartbeat monitor ... handling: user_manual; claude_has_access: depends on chosen service'
findings.finding: Several decisions are deferred or left for the executor to resolve. (1) The heartbeat monitor dependency is explicitly 'user_setup_required' with 'claude_has_access: depends on chosen service' and the Approach offers 'status-badge monitor or scheduled-canary GitHub Action' as an unresolved either/or — yet a Closure Check claims it is already VERIFIED via a live canary. The executor cannot tell whether to set this up or treat it as done. (2) The wholesale replacement of every time-based Success Criterion and Closure Check with 'in-session analogs' is a significant verification-policy decision that materially weakens the original durability guarantees (e.g., '3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' → three back-to-back dispatches; '30-day <1% failure rate' → 5 sequential runs). An executing agent must accept these substitutions as authoritative, but they invert the intent of durability criteria and an agent could reasonably stop to ask whether this is sanctioned. (3) The pass-duration ceiling (15 min) vs cadence (~20 min) and the REST budget default (200) are concrete enough, but the auto-merge 'grace window for legitimate transient duplicates' relies on '2 consecutive passes' whose meaning under back-to-back workflow_dispatch (not 20-min-separated) is ambiguous in the SC5 verification.
findings.suggested_edit: Resolve the heartbeat monitor to a single concrete mechanism and reconcile its dependency status with the 'VERIFIED' claim. State explicitly who authorized the time→in-session substitution and whether the original durability semantics are waived. Clarify whether SC5's pass-2 auto-merge depends on real-time pass separation given back-to-back dispatch.

findings.location: Title 'Replace edge-triggered Jira bridge with level-triggered reconciler' vs Approach '### Identity model', '### Conflict policy', '### Out-of-band observability'
findings.finding: The body faithfully serves the title: it replaces both edge/event-driven workflows with a single level-triggered reconciler converging from full snapshots, addressing the four named structural causes (STATUS-before-CREATE, compaction stripping CREATE, fragile echo prevention, severed ID-space continuity). Scope additions (out-of-band heartbeat, weekly fsck audit, conflict-policy provenance model) are reasonable supporting infrastructure for the stated trust-signal goal rather than drift. Minor: the weekly fsck audit and external heartbeat extend somewhat beyond a strict 'replace the bridge' framing, but the Context explicitly frames this as a platform-level investment, so they are within stated intent.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' and SC2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' replaced by back-to-back workflow_dispatch + injected update), SC7 ('ALREADY SATISFIED ... user explicit override'), and Closure Checks ('5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations' replacing 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%').
findings.finding: Several criteria substitute durability/soak requirements with in-session analogs that materially weaken what is being measured rather than preserving it. SC2's 'durable parity spanning normal local activity' and the original 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%' / '7-day soak' / 'no scheduled runs for N days' are genuine durability and time-based properties; running 3-5 back-to-back workflow_dispatch passes does NOT yield 'the same level of empirical confidence' — it cannot observe behavior under real scheduled cron firing, compaction over time, or accumulated drift. The claim '5 consecutive 100% green is stronger evidence than a 30d historical rate' is asserted, not justified, and is generally false for failure-rate estimation (5 samples cannot bound a <1% rate). SC7 marks rollback 'ALREADY SATISFIED' via a user override with one sub-check 'structurally unverifiable post-cutover' — this is not in-session verifiable as written and effectively waives a criterion. These are measurable but the verification substitutions do not actually verify the stated property.
findings.suggested_edit: For SC2/CC failure-rate items, keep the in-session smoke check but explicitly label it as a smoke/sanity gate and retain a post-merge monitoring acceptance (e.g., first 7 scheduled cron runs green, tracked via heartbeat) rather than claiming equivalence. For SC7, restate the residual unverifiable sub-check (DD3 old-bridge resume) as an explicit accepted-risk with the runbook forward-fix path rather than 'ALREADY SATISFIED'.

findings.location: Approach: 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)' and 'Set-valued fields ... carry per-element provenance in bridge_state/mapping.json — each element tagged origin: local | jira'.
findings.finding: The reconciler introduces a fairly elaborate set of new abstractions (6+ modules, per-element provenance tracking, banded bootstrap manifests, drift-injection harness). For a stated 'currently single-project usage' this is a large surface; whether the per-element provenance model and module split are justified vs. over-engineered depends on existing bridge code already present (e.g., does acli-integration/AcliClient already provide property/label helpers being re-wrapped?). The plan does reference acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property, suggesting reuse, but whether the new package duplicates existing event-bridge logic cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line note on what existing helpers (AcliClient, fsck tooling) are reused vs. newly built, to make the NIH/over-engineering boundary explicit; confirm the 6-module split is warranted by present code rather than speculative.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning' and 'labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"'; SC3 uses JQL 'labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"'; External Dependencies: 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified', 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/API assumptions an executor must verify against the live Jira/codebase: (1) that label JQL `labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"` works given Jira label tokenization rules — Jira labels cannot contain spaces and colon handling/case-folding in JQL is nontrivial; SC3 even uses a DIFFERENT pattern `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` (hyphen, not colon, and LIKE on labels), an internal inconsistency suggesting the exact label/JQL form is unsettled. (2) that `AcliClient.set_issue_property` exists and that REST property read/write endpoints are reachable with the provided token. (3) per-pass label-search returns all matches without pagination surprises within the 200-call budget. These are claimed verified 'during planning' but cannot be confirmed from plan text and the SC3/identity-model mismatch is a concrete red flag.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile the label search syntax: the identity model uses `labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"` while SC3 uses `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` — pick one canonical form and confirm Jira label tokenization permits the colon/hyphen and exact-match semantics on a throwaway issue as part of the capability probe before relying on it. Confirm AcliClient.set_issue_property exists in the current codebase.

findings.location: Success Criteria header '(2026-05-24 revision)' and SC2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' → replaced by back-to-back workflow_dispatch + injected update), SC7 ('ALREADY SATISFIED — ... user explicit override'), Closure Check 3 ('5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations' replacing rolling 30-day failure rate), and Closure Check heartbeat ('VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004').
findings.finding: Several criteria were rewritten to substitute in-session analogs for genuinely time/soak-based guarantees, and some are declared already-satisfied via override rather than verifiable. SC2's durability requirement ('durable', 'spanning normal local activity') is materially weakened: three back-to-back dispatches with one injected update do not demonstrate durability across real cadence and concurrent activity. Closure Check 3's '5 consecutive green runs' is asserted to be 'stronger evidence than 30-day <1% failure rate' but it does not exercise the ~20-min cron scheduling, concurrency contention, or rate-limit accumulation that the 30-day window is meant to catch. The level-triggered convergence claim (the whole point of the ticket — that the system self-heals over repeated passes/time) is precisely the property that cannot be validated in a single back-to-back session. SC7 and the heartbeat check being pre-declared satisfied by override/prior-story closure means they are not re-verifiable as part of this plan's acceptance.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly mark the time-dependent guarantees (steady-state durability, cron firing, 30-day failure rate, multi-day soak) as deferred post-merge monitoring with a named follow-up, rather than claiming the in-session analog is equivalent. For SC2, keep the in-session smoke check but add an explicit post-cutover observation window AC. For SC7/heartbeat, cite the override evidence but flag them as out-of-session-validated so reviewers know they are not freshly verifiable here.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; SC5: 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; Closure Check: 'BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained — it is consumed by the post-cutover reconciler (dso_reconciler/invariants.py ...)'; External Dependencies: 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified'; '/rest/api/3/issue/{key}/properties/dso_local_id'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions that an executor must confirm against the live system before relying on them: (1) that label-JQL (`labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"`) reliably searches the colon-containing label value on Jira Cloud (note Closure Check 1's mismatching glob `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` uses a hyphen, not the colon used elsewhere — possible label-format inconsistency); (2) that `AcliClient.set_issue_property` exists with that signature; (3) that BRIDGE_ENV_ID is in fact consumed by a not-yet-written module `dso_reconciler/invariants.py`; (4) that the capability/forward-compat probes will actually exercise the claimed APIs. These cannot be confirmed from plan text alone and require live codebase/Jira access I do not have.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-execution verification step for the label value format (colon vs hyphen) and reconcile SC3/Closure-Check-1's `dso-id-*` glob against the `dso-id:<uuid>` label scheme used in the Identity model. Confirm `AcliClient.set_issue_property` signature and the capability-probe's coverage before cutover.

findings.location: Success Criteria 1-7 and Closure Checks vs Approach sections 'Conflict policy' (set-valued field per-element provenance), 'First-week tightening: ≤10 mutations/pass + per-pass operator review for 7 days', and 'Out-of-band observability' (Weekly periodic fsck-style audit).
findings.finding: There is a gap between several substantial design elements in the Approach and what the acceptance criteria actually verify, so 'done' does not cover the full stated end-state. Specifically: (a) the per-element provenance model for set-valued fields (labels/watchers/links tagged origin: local|jira, 'removed locally' vs 'never existed') is a complex, bug-prone design with NO success criterion or closure check exercising it — no AC injects a label/link add-on-both-sides or local-removal scenario; (b) the conflict policy 'local wins on destructive changes / additive merged inbound' is never tested by an AC beyond the duplicate/dual-create checks; (c) 'First-week tightening ≤10 mutations/pass for 7 days' is an operational end-state with no closure check (and is inherently multi-day, conflicting with the in-session protocol). The UNION of verification steps therefore does not reach the full end-state described in the Approach; an executor could pass all ACs while the provenance/conflict-merge logic is unbuilt or wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: Add success criteria that exercise (i) a Jira-side additive change (new comment/label/link added) being merged inbound, (ii) a local destructive change (status/title) winning outbound, and (iii) the set-valued provenance distinction (local removal propagates vs Jira addition merges). Either add a closure check for the first-week tightening window or move it to an explicit post-merge operational runbook item so it is not implied to be in-scope-verified.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' header, and SC2/SC4/CC3/CC4; plus SC7 'ALREADY SATISFIED' and CC heartbeat 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24'.
findings.finding: Several criteria's measurability has been weakened by substituting in-session analogs for the durability/time properties they were meant to prove, and two criteria are declared pre-satisfied rather than verifiable. SC2 requires parity 'durable across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' — the substitution runs the 3 passes 'back-to-back, not separated by 20min' with a single injected update, which no longer measures durability across real cadence/concurrency (the very failure mode the reconciler is meant to fix). SC4 'idempotent on converged state' depends on SC2's back-to-back runs actually reaching a converged state, which is not independently asserted. SC7 is marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED ... by user explicit override' with DD3 'structurally unverifiable post-cutover' — this is not a measurable in-session verification but an authorization waiver. Similarly the CC3 heartbeat line is asserted 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004' rather than verified by this ticket. CC '5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations' replacing 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%' is reasonable as an analog, but the claim it is 'stronger evidence' is asserted, not demonstrated.
findings.suggested_edit: For SC2, keep the back-to-back-pass analog but explicitly state which durability property is NOT covered and add a residual-risk note, or run at least one pass after a genuine HEAD advance to exercise the snapshot-isolation/defer path. For SC4, add an explicit pre-condition assertion that converged-state was reached (e.g., pass-1 manifest mutation_count returned to 0) before asserting pass-2/3 idempotency. For SC7 and the heartbeat CC, mark them as 'verified out-of-band / accepted via override' in a separate 'pre-satisfied' section rather than as in-session success criteria, so it is clear they are not re-verified by this ticket.

findings.location: Approach: new package 'plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/' (modules fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health), deletion of bridge-outbound.py/bridge-inbound.py and bridge-outbound.yml/inbound-bridge.yml; CC 'No references to bridge-outbound.py, bridge-inbound.py, _outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, _inbound_api remain in plugins/dso/, .github/, .claude/'.
findings.finding: The edit set is named with concrete files/modules and the blast radius (workflow deletion, cursor state, mapping.json on tickets branch) is explicitly acknowledged, including a dedicated dangling-reference sweep across plugins/dso/, .github/, .claude/. This is plausible. However, confirmation that bridge-outbound.py/_outbound_cursor/_inbound_api have no consumers beyond the listed directories (e.g., tests, docs, other plugins) and that 'dso_reconciler/invariants.py' (referenced in SC5/CC for BRIDGE_ENV_ID) is consistent with the listed module set requires the live codebase. Note invariants.py is referenced but is not in the enumerated module list (fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health) — verify that module is actually planned.
findings.suggested_edit: Add 'invariants' (and any 'health'/alerting submodules referenced in SC5/CC) to the enumerated dso_reconciler module list so the named symbols match, and widen the dangling-reference sweep to include the tests/ and docs/ trees if those exist.

findings.location: Approach 'Conflict policy': per-element provenance tagging ('origin: local | jira') for set-valued fields (labels, watchers, links) in mapping.json; and 'Out-of-band observability' weekly fsck audit plus heartbeat canary.
findings.finding: The level-triggered reconciler replacing two edge-triggered workflows is a justified architectural simplification, not over-engineering. The main candidate for gold-plating is the per-element provenance model with origin tagging and 'removed locally vs never existed locally' distinction across labels/watchers/links — this is a non-trivial CRDT-like layer whose necessity depends on whether all three set-valued field types actually exhibit bidirectional churn in this single-project usage; the plan itself notes 'currently single-project usage.' Whether this is justified or speculative generality cannot be decided from plan text alone. The reconciler package's six modules are a reasonable decomposition, not gratuitous.
findings.suggested_edit: Consider scoping the per-element provenance mechanism to the field type(s) that actually churn bidirectionally in DIG today, and document the others as last-writer-local until a second consumer materializes (Rule-of-Three), to avoid building a general provenance engine for one project.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; External Dependencies: 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified', 'capability probe round-trips ...'; SC5: 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; CC: 'gh api repos/navapbc/digital-service-orchestra/actions/workflows/{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml returns state=deleted'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions an executor must verify rather than trust. (1) It assumes the existing helper 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property' exists and supports setting/reading entity properties — this is a named symbol that must be confirmed against the actual integration module. (2) The label-vs-property design ('JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud') is asserted as 'verified during planning' but is exactly the kind of vendor-behavior claim the capability probe is supposed to confirm; if the probe is the verification, the criterion should run before cutover, not be assumed. (3) The CC uses `gh api ... actions/workflows/{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml returns state=deleted` — assuming the GitHub API exposes a 'deleted' state for deleted workflow files is an unverified API-behavior assumption (deleting a workflow file typically removes it from the workflows list rather than returning a state=deleted record). These should be flagged for verification before being relied upon.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-implementation verification step that confirms (a) AcliClient.set_issue_property and the property read endpoint exist with the assumed signatures, and (b) the exact `gh api` response for a deleted workflow file (and adjust the Closure Check to whatever the API actually returns, e.g., 404 or absence from the workflows list). Treat the Jira-Cloud property-JQL limitation as proven only by the capability-probe result, and gate cutover on that probe.

findings.location: Success Criteria 1-7, Closure Checks, and 'tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh' (SC6)
findings.finding: Testing coverage is broad and behavioral: bootstrap convergence (SC1), parity-under-injected-local-update (SC2), dual-creation invariant (SC3), idempotency/0-mutation (SC4), end-to-end anomaly alert path including the dedup_key and auto-merge restore (SC5), and a 3-mode synthetic drift-injection harness asserting bridge-fsck exit 0 (SC6). Structural greps (Closure Check on residual references) and command-output assertions are present and legitimate. The one gap worth noting: failure-path contracts (429 backoff, 5xx abort, 401 P1-file, REST-call-budget overrun) are richly specified in the Approach but only the 429-free/under-budget happy path is verified (CC analog '5-consecutive-pass 429-free window'); there is no test that deliberately exercises a 401-fatal-abort or a budget-exceeded clean-abort to confirm those handlers actually fire correctly. Consider adding a fault-injection assertion for at least the 401 and budget-ceiling paths.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Success Criterion or harness mode that injects a simulated 401 (or budget overrun) and asserts the pass aborts cleanly and files the expected P1/no-advance-state, mirroring the SC6 drift-injection approach.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' and SC2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes'), CC3 ('runs on the configured ~20-min schedule'), and the deleted-workflow Closure Check ('no scheduled runs ... occur after the cutover commit')
findings.finding: There is a gap between the stated durability end-state and the in-session verification analogs. SC2 requires parity 'durable... across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' but the analog runs three back-to-back workflow_dispatch invocations 'not separated by 20min' — back-to-back manual triggers do not exercise the snapshot-isolation / stale-HEAD deferral logic or the 20-min cadence behavior that the durability claim depends on. Similarly, CC3 verifies the '~20-min schedule' end-state via 5 manual dispatches, which does not actually demonstrate the cron fires. The deleted-workflow check asserts 'no scheduled runs occur after the cutover' as 'structural impossibility,' which is sound, but the converse (that reconcile-bridge.yml's cron actually fires on schedule) is never verified by any criterion — only manual dispatch is. So the union of steps reaches 'works when manually triggered' but not the stated end-state 'runs on the configured ~20-min schedule.' Verification is otherwise concrete and terminating.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit check that reconcile-bridge.yml's scheduled (cron) trigger fires at least once post-cutover (e.g., gh run list filtered to event=schedule shows >=1 run), and have SC2 exercise the snapshot-isolation deferral path explicitly rather than relying solely on back-to-back manual runs.

findings.location: Closure Checks file names ('inbound-bridge.yml', 'bridge-outbound.yml', and `gh api ...{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml`) vs Approach 'Cutover' ('deletes both event-driven workflows') and SC2/CC verify commands referencing 'reconcile-bridge.yml'
findings.finding: Workflow filename references are internally inconsistent: the first Closure Check says 'Both bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml workflow files are deleted' then verifies via `gh api ...{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml` — mixing 'bridge-outbound.yml' / 'inbound-bridge.yml' / 'outbound-bridge.yml' / 'inbound-bridge.yml' naming. An agent cannot determine the canonical filenames to delete/grep. This is a real ambiguity that will cause the deletion/verification grep to miss the actual files. Note SC5's auto-merge claim ('property restored to single value by the pass-2 auto-merge') is coherent with the 2-consecutive-pass grace window in 'Dedup + self-cleaning,' so no contradiction there.
findings.suggested_edit: Pin the exact on-disk filenames once (e.g., bridge-outbound.yml and bridge-inbound.yml) and use them verbatim in both the deletion statement and every gh/git verify command.

findings.location: Title 'Replace edge-triggered Jira bridge with level-triggered reconciler' vs Approach 'Replace both event-driven bridge workflows ... with a single level-triggered reconciler workflow'
Success Criteria 1-6 + Closure Checks; tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh; capability/forward-compat probes under 'Cutover and rollback'
findings.finding: Testing coverage is broad and uses legitimate live-execution + structural/command-output assertions: bootstrap convergence (SC1), parity with injected local update (SC2), dedup mismatch greps (SC3), idempotent 0-mutation manifests (SC4), end-to-end anomaly alert path with restore (SC5), synthetic drift harness covering 3 anomaly modes (SC6), and grep-based legacy-reference closure checks. Failure-path contracts (429/5xx/401) are specified. The only thin spot: there is no explicit RED-before-GREEN / negative assertion that the drift harness actually fails when the reconciler is absent or broken (i.e., proving the test can detect a regression, not just pass on a healed state).
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally add to SC6 a precondition assertion that bridge-fsck exits NON-zero immediately after injection and BEFORE reconcile-bridge.yml runs, to confirm the harness detects the anomaly rather than passing vacuously.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' header and SC2/SC7; Closure Checks revision header
findings.finding: Termination criteria exist for every Success Criterion and Closure Check, but several durability/soak requirements have been redefined into in-session analogs that do not clearly reach the originally stated end-state. SC2 requires parity 'durable... across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' but the analog runs three back-to-back workflow_dispatch passes (not separated by 20min) with a single injected update between passes 2 and 3 — this weakens the 'spanning normal local activity' guarantee and may not demonstrate durability. SC7 is declared 'ALREADY SATISFIED' via user override despite DD3 ('old bridges run 1 scheduled cycle post-revert') being 'structurally unverifiable' — a genuine gap between 'what done looks like' (rollback proven executable) and the evidence captured. The Closure Check substitution of '5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations' for a 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%' is asserted to be 'stronger evidence' but this is not self-evidently equivalent. Whether these analogs truly reach the stated end-state depends on facts not verifiable here.
findings.suggested_edit: For SC2, either retain a true time-separated pass or justify why back-to-back passes plus one injected update is empirically equivalent to durability across normal activity. For SC7, explicitly mark DD3 as an accepted residual risk in the rollback runbook rather than implying full satisfaction. For the 5-run analog, state the assumption (transient/flaky failures are equally likely per-run) under which 5 green runs substitutes for a 30-day rate.

findings.location: Closure Check 'No references to ... BRIDGE_ENV_ID...' vs Context 'fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)' listed as a structural cause to be fixed
findings.finding: The Context lists 'fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)' as one of the four structural causes of failure that the level-triggered architecture is meant to eliminate, yet a Closure Check states 'BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained — it is consumed by the post-cutover reconciler.' This is a potential contradiction: the named root-cause mechanism is being kept. The benign reading is that the reconciler reuses the *identifier* for a more robust label/property-based echo prevention while abandoning the fragile single-token scheme, but the plan does not explicitly reconcile these two statements, which could confuse the executing agent during the legacy-reference grep.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence clarifying that the *fragile single-token echo-prevention design* is replaced by the label-primary identity model, while the BRIDGE_ENV_ID variable itself is repurposed by dso_reconciler/invariants.py — so retention is not a regression of the named root cause.

findings.location: Title 'Replace edge-triggered Jira bridge with level-triggered reconciler' vs Approach 'Replace both event-driven bridge workflows ... with a single level-triggered reconciler workflow'
Success Criteria 7 ('Rollback dry-run') and Closure Check on heartbeat ('VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004') and CC3 heartbeat clause
findings.finding: Several acceptance items are pre-marked SATISFIED/VERIFIED inside the plan-under-review rather than stated as criteria to be met: SC7 says 'ALREADY SATISFIED — story 7339... closed with user explicit override', and the heartbeat Closure Check says 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004 closure'. A criterion that is asserted-as-already-done in the plan body is not a verifiable acceptance criterion for THIS ticket and creates ambiguity about what is actually in scope. Separately, terminology for the workflow filenames is inconsistent: the Approach/cutover section refers to 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py' and 'bridge-inbound.py' while the Closure Checks reference workflow files as 'bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml' and 'weekly-bridge-fsck.yml' — the inbound workflow is named both 'bridge-inbound' and 'inbound-bridge', and outbound similarly; this naming drift will confuse the executor doing the deletion/grep closure checks.
findings.suggested_edit: Pin one canonical filename per workflow (e.g., always 'inbound-bridge.yml'/'outbound-bridge.yml') and use it consistently in Approach and Closure Checks. For SC7 and the heartbeat check, either move pre-satisfied items to a 'Prerequisites already met' note with the evidence link, or restate them as criteria this ticket must re-verify; do not embed PASS verdicts inside the criteria.

findings.location: Approach section as a whole; 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)' plus identity, dedup, conflict, concurrency, 429/5xx/401, bootstrap, alerting, cutover/rollback, observability subsystems
findings.finding: This is labeled EPIC_CLASS class:architectural and replaces two production workflows with an entirely new multi-module reconciler package spanning a fetcher/differ/applier engine, per-element provenance merge model, snapshot-isolation concurrency contract, rate-limit/error-handling policy, a banded bootstrap migration of ~1,300+~300 items, an auto-bug-filing alert subsystem, hard cutover + rollback playbook, and out-of-band heartbeat/weekly-audit observability. That is far too large for a single unit of work and there is no decomposition into child tickets. An architectural epic of this scope should be split into vertical children (e.g., identity/mapping foundation; reconcile engine; bootstrap migration; cutover+rollback; observability/alerting), each independently reviewable and executable.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Children' decomposition breaking the epic into vertically sliced child tickets (identity+mapping, diff/apply engine, bootstrap migration, cutover/rollback, observability/alerting), each with its own success criteria, rather than executing the whole reconciler in one session.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' header and per-criterion replacements; e.g. SC2 'spanning normal local activity ... replaced by injecting a deliberate local-side ticket update', CC3 '5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations ... replaces rolling 30-day failure rate', heartbeat/SC7 items asserted as already-VERIFIED
findings.finding: The plan systematically substitutes in-session analogs for the originally specified durability/soak requirements (3 passes back-to-back instead of 'spanning normal local activity', 5 sequential green runs instead of rolling 30-day <1% failure rate, deterministic absence-of-runs instead of 7-day soak). These substitutions encode a real, contestable design/acceptance decision (whether burst evidence is equivalent to durability over time) that the author has pre-decided; the durability properties (drift holds under real concurrent activity, cron actually stops firing over days, 429-free under sustained real load) are explicitly NOT equivalent to their burst analogs and an executor cannot resolve whether 'good enough' has been met without clarification. This is decision-deferral-by-substitution rather than ambiguous wording, but it leaves the executor unable to know the true bar. Also SC7/heartbeat being marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED'/'VERIFIED' via user override means the executor cannot independently verify them in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) keep the time-based criteria as the real bar and schedule the soak, or (b) explicitly state that the in-session analogs are the agreed acceptance bar AND have the author/owner sign off that burst evidence is accepted in lieu of durability, noting the residual risk per criterion. Make clear which evidence the executor must produce vs. which is inherited from prior stories.

findings.location: Success Criteria #7 (Rollback dry-run) and Closure Checks bullet on heartbeat (CC3 analog) — both marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED' / 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24'
findings.finding: Several criteria are pre-marked satisfied with external story references (SC7 'story 7339-...', CC3 heartbeat 'story 7004 closure', bug ticket c03a-2e77') that are not described anywhere in the plan body — the Approach section describes the rollback playbook and heartbeat as work-to-build, but the success/closure criteria treat them as already-done. This is a coherence gap: a criterion that is 'already satisfied' has no corresponding described work remaining, and conversely the described rollback/heartbeat work has no live verification criterion left. Also terminology drift on workflow filenames: the body uses 'bridge-outbound.yml'/'bridge-outbound.py' while the Closure Checks use both 'bridge-outbound.yml' and 'inbound-bridge.yml' / 'outbound-bridge.yml' inconsistently (e.g. 'inbound-bridge.yml and bridge-outbound.yml are deleted', then `gh api ...{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml`). The actual deployed filenames must be named one way consistently or the git/gh verification commands will not match real files.
findings.suggested_edit: Pick and use one canonical filename per workflow (e.g. always 'bridge-inbound.yml'/'bridge-outbound.yml') across Approach, Success Criteria, and Closure Checks. For pre-satisfied criteria, either move them out of the active criteria list into a 'Prior evidence' note, or add the described work for the items still being built so each described workstream maps to a live criterion.

findings.location: Approach section as a whole; spans identity model, dedup/self-cleaning, conflict policy (set-valued per-element provenance), concurrency/snapshot-isolation, 429/5xx/401 contracts, banded bootstrap, anomaly alerting, cutover+rollback, out-of-band observability — plus a new 6-module Python package
findings.finding: This is an architectural epic (EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural) but it is written as a single executable ticket with no decomposition into children. It bundles at least seven independently-shippable workstreams: (1) reconciler core (fetcher/differ/applier), (2) identity model label+property stamping, (3) per-element provenance set-field merge, (4) rate-limit/error contracts, (5) banded bootstrap + dry-run manifest, (6) cutover + rollback playbook, (7) out-of-band heartbeat + weekly audit. Each carries its own risk tier and the bootstrap is explicitly a high-stakes one-shot migration of ~1,300 items. Executing this as one unit is too big for a single coherent session and the hard-cutover deletion of both live bridges in one commit concentrates risk. The conflict-policy set-valued provenance work alone is a sizable sub-feature.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets: build+land the reconciler and identity model behind a non-cutover dry-run mode first; separate children for provenance-tagged set-field merge, bootstrap/manifest, cutover+rollback, and observability. Keep the hard cutover as its own gated child that depends on the others passing.

findings.location: Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision) headers on both Success Criteria and Closure Checks; SC2 'spanning normal local activity' replaced by 'injecting a deliberate local-side ticket update'; SC7 'evidence sufficient by user authorization'; CC 'observe ... per-pass REST call counts under the cap'
findings.finding: The plan mostly avoids open-ended placeholders, but it embeds several design decisions and risk-tradeoffs that an executor will have to interpret without clear authority. (1) The blanket 'in-session analog' substitution of all time-based criteria (durability across passes, 30-day failure rate, multi-day soak, 7-day no-cron soak) is asserted as 'equivalent... same level of empirical confidence' — this is a judgment the executor must accept or contest; collapsing 3 passes 'back-to-back, not separated by 20min' specifically removes the temporal-convergence property the original criterion was testing, which is a real design decision deferred under the guise of verification. (2) Risk tiers ('low/high per pre-cutover analysis') for the ~1,300 vs ~300 split are referenced but the analysis/threshold defining the split is not specified, so the executor must invent the banding rule. (3) The heartbeat monitor is 'status-badge monitor OR scheduled-canary GitHub Action OR other' with user_setup_required and 'claude_has_access: depends on chosen service' — an unmade choice that blocks the heartbeat closure check. These force the executor to either stop and ask or silently make a call.
findings.suggested_edit: Specify the risk-tier banding rule explicitly (what makes an item high-risk). Pin the heartbeat mechanism to one concrete option. Either retain at least one genuinely time-spanning verification for durability/idempotence or explicitly flag that the in-session analogs are weaker and require sign-off, rather than asserting equivalence.

findings.location: Success Criteria 1-7, Closure Checks, tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh

Closure Checks header 'inbound-bridge.yml' vs Approach/Cutover 'bridge-inbound.py'; and the Approach describes a 'Weekly periodic fsck-style audit' / 'weekly-bridge-fsck.yml' while SC list does not include it as a numbered Success Criterion (only appears in Closure Checks).
findings.finding: Terminology inconsistency: the two legacy workflows are referred to inconsistently — Approach says 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py' and Cutover says 'delete both event-driven workflows', but the Closure Check names the YAML files 'bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml' (outbound named subject-first, inbound named subject-last). The deletion verify command uses `{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml` which implies BOTH are named `<dir>-bridge.yml`, contradicting 'bridge-outbound.yml'. An agent cannot deterministically know the actual filenames to delete/grep. Also several richly-described mechanisms (conflict policy / set-valued provenance in mapping.json, snapshot-isolation contract, 429/5xx/401 contracts) have no corresponding Success Criterion verifying them.
findings.suggested_edit: Pin the exact legacy workflow filenames once and reuse them verbatim everywhere (e.g. 'bridge-outbound.yml' and 'bridge-inbound.yml'), and fix the glob accordingly. Consider adding explicit success criteria for the conflict/provenance policy and the snapshot-isolation/local-wins-on-staleness behavior, which are central design claims currently unverified.

findings.location: Approach: new package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health/invariants); set-valued per-element provenance in mapping.json; BRIDGE_ENV_ID retained.
findings.finding: The single-project-today scope ('currently single-project usage') combined with a 7-module package, per-element set-valued provenance tracking, drip-feed risk tiers, and a separate weekly audit workflow could be over-built for one project — but the plan explicitly frames this as a deliberate platform-level architectural investment and reuses existing infra (acli-integration.AcliClient, /dso:fix-bug, BRIDGE_ENV_ID), so NIH/over-engineering cannot be confirmed from plan text. Whether the module split is gratuitous abstraction needs codebase access.

EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural; Approach spanning identity model, dedup, conflict policy, concurrency, rate-limit contracts, banded bootstrap, alerting, cutover/rollback, observability; Dependencies: None; no child tickets listed.
findings.finding: This is labeled an architectural epic and bundles at least eight independently substantial workstreams (new reconciler package, identity/dedup model, conflict+provenance policy, concurrency/snapshot isolation, 429/5xx/401 contracts, banded bootstrap migration of ~1,300 items, anomaly auto-filing, hard cutover+rollback, out-of-band heartbeat). For an epic of class:architectural the plan presents no decomposition into child tickets (Dependencies: None) — it reads as one monolithic unit. The risky one-commit hard cutover plus a live bulk migration of ~1,300 items is too large to execute coherently as a single unit and should be split into vertical slices (e.g. reconciler-core+capability-probe, dedup/identity, conflict-provenance, bootstrap-migration, cutover+rollback, observability).
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets with explicit ordering/dependencies — minimally separate (1) reconciler core + probes, (2) bootstrap/migration pass, (3) hard cutover + rollback playbook, (4) observability/heartbeat — so each is independently reviewable and the live cutover is isolated from build-out.

findings.location: Success Criteria 1-7 and Closure Checks; e.g. SC5 'Anomaly alert path end-to-end', SC6 'tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh', and structural greps in Closure Checks
findings.finding: Testing coverage is broad and behavioral: convergence (SC1), parity (SC2), dedup (SC3), idempotency (SC4), an explicit fault-injection/alert path (SC5), a 3-mode synthetic drift-and-heal harness (SC6), plus structural grep closure checks. Failure paths (429/5xx/401) are designed in the Approach but note: there is no Success Criterion or Closure Check that actively exercises the 429/5xx/401 contracts (the REST-budget check only observes the *absence* of 429s, it never forces one). Consider adding a fault-injected 429/5xx/401 assertion to confirm the abort-clean and P1-file behavior, since these are named structural causes/contracts.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Success Criterion that injects a forced 401 (e.g., revoked token) and asserts a fatal pass abort + P1 bug filed with no in-pass retry, mirroring SC5's fault-injection style.

findings.location: Cutover/rollback Approach vs Success Criteria; SC7 'Rollback dry-run ... ALREADY SATISFIED'; Closure Check 'reconcile-bridge.yml runs on the configured ~20-min schedule ... VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004'; heartbeat dependency 'user_setup_required'
findings.finding: Several described work items have their verification pre-declared satisfied or shifted to other stories, creating orphan/duplicate-status coherence problems: (1) SC7 rollback is marked ALREADY SATISFIED via an external story override, so the cutover/rollback design in the body has no live criterion in THIS ticket. (2) The heartbeat is 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004' in a Closure Check yet still listed as 'user_setup_required' in External Dependencies — contradictory status. (3) The 2026-05-24 verification-protocol revision replaces every time-based criterion with in-session analogs, which weakens the 'open-count parity durable ... across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' criterion (SC2) to back-to-back runs — the 'durable/spanning normal activity' concept described in body is no longer matched by the criterion as written.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile the heartbeat status (either it is verified or user-setup-required, not both); either retain SC7/heartbeat as live criteria in this ticket or explicitly move them to named child tickets; and restore a durability check for SC2 (e.g., assert parity across passes that each contain a distinct injected local mutation).

findings.location: New package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health) and per-element provenance in bridge_state/mapping.json
findings.finding: The 6-7 module decomposition (fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health/invariants) is reasonable for a reconciler, not obviously gratuitous. However, two items warrant a NIH check against the live codebase: (a) the plan reuses acli-integration.AcliClient (good, reuses existing), but (b) the per-element set-valued field provenance model ('origin: local|jira', 'removed locally vs never existed') is a non-trivial CRDT-like abstraction whose necessity for a single-project, single-flight bridge isn't justified against simpler last-writer-wins. Cannot confirm whether existing bridge code already has reusable diff/mapping primitives.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm no reusable diff/mapping/state primitives exist in the old bridge before building dso_reconciler from scratch; and justify the per-element provenance model versus a simpler local-wins-set-union given single-project scope, or defer it to a follow-up.

EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural; Dependencies: None; Approach spans new package, identity model, dedup/self-cleaning, conflict policy, concurrency, rate-limit contracts, banded bootstrap, alerting, cutover/rollback, and out-of-band observability
findings.finding: This is tagged architectural/epic-scale but presented as a single unit of work with 'Dependencies: None' and no decomposition into children. The scope spans at least eight independently-testable subsystems (reconciler core diff/apply, identity/dedup invariants, conflict-merge with per-element provenance, rate-limit/error contracts, banded bootstrap migration of ~1,300+~300 items, anomaly auto-filing, hard cutover+rollback, out-of-band heartbeat). Delivering all of this plus a hard cutover in one commit is too large for one coherent session and risks the very mid-migration irreversibility the plan itself flags. For an epic, the absence of child tickets is a decomposition gap; for a leaf it is oversized. Note SC7 and the heartbeat are already implicitly split into other stories (7339, 7004, 7004 again), which signals the work already lives across tickets but the parent doesn't declare that structure.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into vertical-slice children (e.g., 1: reconciler core + identity/dedup on read-only/dry-run; 2: conflict-merge; 3: error/rate-limit contracts; 4: banded bootstrap migration; 5: cutover+rollback; 6: heartbeat/weekly-audit) with explicit parent/child dependency edges, rather than carrying it as one unit with 'Dependencies: None'.

findings.location: Success Criteria #2 ("holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity") and the global "Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)" preamble; Closure Checks CC3/heartbeat/CC weekly; SC7 marked "ALREADY SATISFIED".
findings.finding: Several criteria as originally stated require out-of-session validation (durable parity "across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity", rolling 30-day <1% failure rate, "no scheduled runs for N days", ≥2 weekly cycles, first-week 7-day tightening). The plan recognizes this and substitutes in-session analogs, which is the right instinct, but two substitutions weaken the criterion to the point of not verifying the original property: (a) SC2's "durable across normal local activity" is replaced by a single injected update between passes 2 and 3 run back-to-back — this does not exercise convergence under genuine concurrent activity or over time; (b) Closure-Check 30-day <1% failure rate replaced by 5 sequential green runs is asserted as "stronger evidence" but 5 runs cannot surface intermittent/scheduled-cadence failure modes. Additionally SC7 is declared pre-satisfied by user override with DD3 "structurally unverifiable" — that is an out-of-session acceptance, not an in-session verification.
findings.suggested_edit: Keep the in-session analogs but explicitly scope down the claims they verify (e.g., SC2: 'verifies immediate convergence under a single concurrent edit; durability under sustained activity is out of scope for in-session verification'). For the 30d-failure-rate analog, state it verifies 'no failure across 5 runs' rather than equating it to a 30d rate. Flag SC7 as accepted-by-override rather than verified.

findings.location: "REST-PUT a second `dso_local_id` value via `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property`" (SC5); "JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning"; "`/rest/api/3/issue/{key}/properties/dso_local_id`"; references to `_outbound_cursor`, `_inbound_api`, BRIDGE_ENV_ID consumers.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions an executor must confirm before relying on: (1) existence and signature of `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property`; (2) that BRIDGE_ENV_ID is actually consumed by the new reconciler (`dso_reconciler/invariants.py`) rather than legacy code — this is asserted but the module is new; (3) that label JQL (`labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"`) reliably returns issues given Jira label tokenization/case rules (the fsck cross-ref in SC3 uses `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` with a hyphen, while the identity model uses `dso-id:<uuid>` with a colon — an inconsistency that may indicate an unverified label-format assumption); (4) the Jira-Cloud 'no JQL on entity properties' claim. I lack the live codebase/Jira instance to confirm or refute these.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-execution checklist that confirms AcliClient.set_issue_property exists with the assumed signature, that BRIDGE_ENV_ID's only consumer is the reconciler, and the exact label token format (colon vs hyphen) that JQL must match.

findings.location: Identity model: label `dso-id:<uuid>` (colon) vs SC3 verification JQL `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` (hyphen). Also Closure Check "No references to ... remain" vs "BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained".
findings.finding: Cross-section inconsistency in the identity label format: the Identity model and dedup path use the label `dso-id:<uuid>` (colon separator), but the SC3 verification command searches `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` (hyphen separator). If the actual label uses a colon, the SC3 query will never match the real labels and the duplicate check is invalid; if it uses a hyphen, the design section is wrong. These must be reconciled. (The BRIDGE_ENV_ID retention note is internally consistent — the plan corrects the original over-broad closure check — so that is not a contradiction.)
findings.suggested_edit: Pick one label format and make the Identity model, dedup path, and SC3 JQL all use the identical token (e.g., `dso-id:<uuid>` and `labels = "dso-id:<uuid>"` / appropriate negation), or document why the fsck query pattern differs from the stored label.

findings.location: "New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)"; cutover deletes bridge-outbound.py/bridge-inbound.py and bridge-outbound.yml/inbound-bridge.yml; Closure Check scans plugins/dso/, .github/, .claude/.
findings.finding: Edit targets are named and plausible (new package, two workflow YAMLs deleted, two scripts deleted, mapping.json/bridge_state/ on tickets branch). However blast radius is only partially enumerated: the plan lists symbols to purge (`_outbound_cursor`, `_outbound_handlers`, `_inbound_api`) but does not enumerate the current callers/importers of bridge-outbound.py / bridge-inbound.py that may exist elsewhere (e.g., shared helper modules, /dso command wiring, scheduled cron references). The SC5 dependency on `acli-integration.AcliClient` and SC6 on `tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh` imply pre-existing files whose presence I cannot confirm. Whether the named edit set is complete needs live-codebase confirmation.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit caller/importer sweep step before deletion (grep for imports of the two scripts and shared bridge helpers) and confirm `tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh` and AcliClient.set_issue_property either exist or are part of this ticket's edit set.

findings.location: "Each band carries a risk tier (low/high) per pre-cutover analysis"; "~1,300 low-risk items" vs "~300 high-risk items"; "Per-pass REST call budget capped (default 200)"; "Hard pass-duration ceiling (15 min default)"; heartbeat monitor "status-badge monitor or scheduled-canary GitHub Action".
findings.finding: Two genuine design decisions are deferred onto the executor: (1) The low/high risk-tier partition driving the bulk-vs-drip bootstrap split ('~1,300 low-risk' vs '~300 high-risk') is attributed to a 'pre-cutover analysis' that is not specified — the executor must invent the classification rule before it can produce the per-class manifest. (2) The heartbeat monitor is left as an open choice between a status-badge monitor or a scheduled-canary action ('or other' in External Dependencies), and is marked user_setup_required — an executor cannot complete it without picking and wiring one. Most numeric defaults (200 calls, 15 min, ≤10 mutations/pass) are concrete and fine.
findings.suggested_edit: Define the risk-tier classification rule (which anomaly classes/conditions map to low vs high) explicitly, and either pick the heartbeat mechanism or scope it out of this ticket as a separate user-owned task with an acceptance gate.

findings.location: Success Criteria #2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity'), #4, and Closure Checks: 'reconcile-bridge.yml runs on the configured ~20-min schedule with 5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations'; 'no scheduled runs occur after the cutover commit'; SC7 'ALREADY SATISFIED ... user explicit override'.
findings.finding: Several criteria substitute in-session analogs for genuinely temporal/durability guarantees, and the substitution materially weakens what is measured rather than preserving 'the same level of empirical confidence' as claimed. (a) 'Spanning normal local activity / 3 consecutive passes' is replaced by 'back-to-back workflow_dispatch with one injected update' — this does NOT exercise the ~20-min cadence, scheduled-trigger path, or real concurrent local activity, so cadence/scheduling regressions and snapshot-isolation races under real timing are unverified. (b) The Closure Check 'no scheduled runs after cutover' is asserted as 'structural impossibility — deleted YAMLs cannot fire crons'; that is reasonable for the deleted workflows but is not actually executed/observed. (c) SC7 and the heartbeat closure check are marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED / VERIFIED' by prior-story closure and user override rather than verified within this plan's session — these are out-of-session validations being imported, and DD3 ('old bridges run 1 scheduled cycle post-revert') is explicitly conceded 'structurally unverifiable.' Net: the rollback resume-path and real-schedule behavior are NOT in-session measurable here.
findings.suggested_edit: For each replaced criterion, state explicitly what confidence is lost vs. the original and add a minimal scheduled-trigger smoke check (e.g., let one real cron tick fire and assert it runs/exits 0) where feasible; for SC7/heartbeat, either re-run the verification in this session or label them as inherited-evidence (not session-verifiable) and flag the residual risk rather than marking SATISFIED.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; SC5 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; '/rest/api/3/issue/{key}/properties/dso_local_id'; Closure Check 'BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained ... consumed by dso_reconciler/invariants.py'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions an executor must confirm before relying on them: (1) that label-JQL search is reliable while property-JQL is unsupported on this Jira Cloud instance ('verified during planning' is unattributed); (2) that a helper 'AcliClient.set_issue_property' exists with that exact signature; (3) that the REST property endpoint shape is correct for the deployed Jira API version; (4) that BRIDGE_ENV_ID is genuinely consumed by the (as-yet-unwritten) dso_reconciler/invariants.py — this references a module the plan also says will be newly created, so the claim is forward-looking, not an existing fact. These can only be confirmed against the live codebase/Jira, which I cannot access.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-implementation verification step listing each asserted helper/endpoint/Jira capability and confirming it exists (the capability probe partially covers Jira; add codebase grep for AcliClient.set_issue_property and BRIDGE_ENV_ID consumers).

findings.location: Approach 'Single-flight via concurrency: { group: reconcile-bridge ...}' vs Closure Check 'gh api ... actions/workflows/{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml'; and SC #1 '1,482 -> <=300' (>=80% drop) vs SC #6 'bridge-fsck exits 0' / Context 'every bridge-fsck run produces either zero anomalies or an actionable bug ticket'.
findings.finding: Two coherence frictions: (1) Workflow filename references are inconsistent across the plan — Approach says it deletes 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py' and Closure Checks reference both 'bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml' AND 'gh api ... {inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml'. The .yml naming ('bridge-outbound.yml', 'inbound-bridge.yml', 'inbound-bridge.yml/outbound-bridge.yml') is internally inconsistent and an executor cannot be sure of the canonical filenames. (2) SC#1 only requires anomalies drop to <=300 after bootstrap (the ~300 high-risk drip-fed items remain), but the Context promises 'every bridge-fsck run produces either zero anomalies or an actionable bug ticket' and SC#6 asserts 'bridge-fsck exits 0' — fsck exit 0 on a state with up to 300 residual anomalies is not reconciled with the 'zero anomalies' trust signal.
findings.suggested_edit: Pin the exact pre/post workflow filenames once and reuse them verbatim everywhere; clarify whether bridge-fsck exit 0 means 'no UNCLASSIFIED anomalies' (the ~300 high-risk drip items being expected/tracked) and align SC#1, SC#6 and the Context statement on that definition.

findings.location: Approach: new package 'plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)' but later references 'dso_reconciler/invariants.py'; deletion targets 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py'; Closure Check grep targets '_outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, _inbound_api'.
findings.finding: Edit targets are mostly named and plausible (new package, deleted workflows, grep'd legacy symbols), satisfying the spirit of scope accuracy. One inconsistency: the module list enumerates fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health, but SC#5 and a Closure Check reference 'dso_reconciler/invariants.py', a module NOT in the declared list — either the list is incomplete or invariants logic is meant to live in one of the named modules. Whether the legacy symbols (_outbound_cursor etc.) and the AcliClient helper actually exist where claimed needs live-codebase confirmation.

findings.location: Out-of-band observability: 'status-badge monitor or scheduled-canary GitHub Action'; External Dependencies heartbeat: 'status badge / scheduled-canary action / other' with 'claude_has_access: depends on chosen service'; Conflict policy set-valued provenance.
findings.finding: The heartbeat/observability mechanism is left as an unresolved choice ('status-badge monitor OR scheduled-canary OR other', 'depends on chosen service') — this defers a real design+access decision to the executor and is marked user_setup_required, so an agent could stall on which mechanism to wire up. Most other sections are decisive (conflict policy, 429/401 contracts, dedup grace window are all concretely specified), so this is the main executable-without-clarification gap. The heartbeat is also partly claimed already VERIFIED via story 7004, creating ambiguity about whether the executor must build it or not.
findings.suggested_edit: Pick one heartbeat mechanism (or state the decision criterion and default) and clarify whether the executor must (re)configure it this session or inherit the story-7004 canary as-is.

findings.location: Approach > 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)'; also 'External Dependencies' references existing acli-integration.AcliClient
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 6-module package and a custom per-element provenance set-merge engine, custom backoff/budget contracts, and a synthetic drift harness. Some of this may duplicate existing helpers (e.g. acli-integration.AcliClient already exists and is referenced for set_issue_property). Whether the new modules over-build vs. reuse existing bridge utilities cannot be confirmed without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Note which existing helpers (acli-integration, current bridge mapping/state code) are reused vs. rewritten, so the executor doesn't reinvent existing JQL/property plumbing.

findings.location: Success Criteria header '2026-05-24 revision' and SC7/CC items marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED' / 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24' before implementation exists
findings.finding: Verification/termination is undermined: several Success Criteria and Closure Checks are pre-declared satisfied (SC7 'ALREADY SATISFIED', heartbeat CC 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24', BRIDGE_ENV_ID carve-out) before the reconciler is built, and durability criteria (open-count parity 'durable', <1% 30-day failure rate, 7-day soak) are swapped for back-to-back workflow_dispatch runs. Back-to-back triggers explicitly bypass the ~20-min cadence and time-spanning activity the criteria were designed to test, so the 'done' signal no longer demonstrates the convergence/durability the end-state requires. The union of steps does not clearly reach the stated durable end-state.
findings.suggested_edit: Retain at least one cadence-spanning soak (or scheduled-run) verification for parity/idempotence/failure-rate, and re-verify the pre-satisfied items against the actually-deployed reconciler rather than carrying prior-story closures forward.

findings.location: Closure Checks: 'No references to ... remain' lists removed symbols but then 'BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained'; vs Context which names 'fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)' as one of the four structural root causes to eliminate. Also SC5(c) vs Dedup grace-window.
findings.finding: Two cross-section contradictions. (1) Context lists BRIDGE_ENV_ID echo-prevention as a structural cause being fixed, but Closure Checks retain BRIDGE_ENV_ID and route it into dso_reconciler/invariants.py — unclear whether the root cause is actually removed or merely relabeled. (2) SC5(c) asserts the second dso_local_id 'is restored to single value by the pass-2 auto-merge', but the Dedup section requires '2 consecutive passes before auto-merge fires'; a single triggered pass (SC5 triggers reconcile-bridge.yml once) plus 'pass-2' wording is internally inconsistent about whether one or two passes run, and whether auto-merge would even fire given the grace window.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify how BRIDGE_ENV_ID's retained use differs from the deprecated echo-prevention root cause; and reconcile SC5's single workflow_dispatch trigger with the 2-consecutive-pass auto-merge grace window (either trigger twice or state pass-1 only files the alert).

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; External Dependencies: 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified'; SC5 references 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; SC3 JQL 'labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"'
findings.finding: Several environment/codebase claims an executor must trust: (a) Jira Cloud JQL-on-property limitation (plausible but version-dependent); (b) existence of acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property; (c) capability-probe APIs (label JQL, property REST endpoints) round-trip on the live tenant. Additionally SC3's JQL uses `labels NOT LIKE "dso-id-*"` (hyphen) whereas the label scheme is `dso-id:<uuid>` (colon) — the asserted match pattern may not match the asserted label format. These cannot be confirmed without the live codebase/tenant.
findings.suggested_edit: Mark the JQL/property and AcliClient claims as 'verify in capability probe', and reconcile SC3's `dso-id-*` pattern with the `dso-id:<uuid>` label format.

findings.location: Bootstrap 'risk tier (low/high) per pre-cutover analysis' and 'the ~1,300 low-risk items ... ~300 high-risk items'; Cutover 'If either crashes, patch the old bridges to ignore the new fields'; Conflict policy set-valued provenance
findings.finding: Multiple decisions are deferred to the executor without a rule. (1) The low/high risk-tier classification is the gate between a single bulk pass and drip-feed, but the criteria for assigning a tier are 'per pre-cutover analysis' — undefined, so the 1,300/300 split is an unjustified placeholder. (2) The forward-compat fallback 'patch the old bridges to ignore the new fields' is an open-ended code change with no spec. (3) Per-element provenance tagging 'origin: local | jira' and 'removed locally vs never existed' implies a migration/backfill for existing mapping entries with no origin tag — unspecified default. An agent would likely need to stop and ask on the risk-tier rule.
findings.suggested_edit: Define the concrete risk-tier classification rule (which anomaly attributes map to low vs high), specify the default `origin` for pre-existing mapping elements, and scope the old-bridge patch (which fields, which functions).

findings.location: Approach → 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)'; also 'currently single-project usage, treated as a platform-level investment'
findings.finding: The plan builds a fairly elaborate reconciler (6+ modules, per-element provenance tracking, banded bootstrap, anomaly auto-filing with dedup windows) and self-justifies platform-level generality for what it admits is 'currently single-project usage.' This is a plausible over-engineering risk (building multi-tenant/platform machinery for one consumer), but whether the modular split and provenance model are gratuitous vs. warranted by the four root causes cannot be confirmed without seeing the existing bridge code. Per-element provenance in mapping.json in particular is a new abstraction whose payoff (set-valued conflict resolution) should be confirmed to have >1 real use site.
findings.suggested_edit: State explicitly which complexity (provenance tracking, platform generality) is required by the four root causes vs. speculative, and defer any single-call-site abstraction until a second consumer exists.

findings.location: Success Criteria header '2026-05-24 revision' and SC2/SC4/CC 'reconcile-bridge.yml runs on the configured ~20-min schedule with 5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations'
findings.finding: Several termination/verification criteria have been redefined to in-session analogs that no longer test the stated end-state. SC2 requires durability 'across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' but the verification fires three workflow_dispatch runs 'back-to-back, not separated by 20min' — this does not exercise the ~20-min cadence or real concurrent activity that the durability claim depends on. The CC for the ~20-min schedule is verified by manual dispatch, never by an actual scheduled cron firing, so 'runs on the configured schedule' is not actually verified. The deleted-workflow 'absence-of-runs' check is sound, but the positive schedule-firing of the new workflow is not. Net: the union of verification steps leaves a gap between 'what done looks like' (durable, scheduled, time-spanning convergence) and 'what is verified' (back-to-back manual triggers).
findings.suggested_edit: Retain at least one verification that a real scheduled (cron) run of reconcile-bridge.yml fires and exits 0, and that drift holds across passes separated by genuine intervening activity, rather than collapsing all temporal checks into back-to-back manual dispatch.

findings.location: SC5(c) 'the chosen issue's property is restored to single value by the pass-2 auto-merge' vs. Dedup section 'Violations require 2 consecutive passes before auto-merge fires' and SC5 verification which triggers reconcile-bridge.yml only once
findings.finding: Internal contradiction between SC5's single-trigger verification and the auto-merge grace-window design. The design states the at-most-one invariant requires 2 consecutive passes before auto-merge fires; SC5(a)/(b) assert the alert and bug ticket happen 'within that pass' (one trigger), yet SC5(c) asserts the property is restored 'by the pass-2 auto-merge' — but the verification only triggers the workflow once. The verification as written cannot observe a pass-2 auto-merge. Either SC5 must trigger at least two passes, or the design/criterion disagree on when merge fires. Separately, CC5 says BRIDGE_ENV_ID is retained and consumed by the reconciler, while the Context lists 'fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)' as one of the four root causes being eliminated — these two statements about BRIDGE_ENV_ID's fate should be reconciled.
findings.suggested_edit: Make SC5 trigger two consecutive passes (or document why one suffices) and clarify in Context whether BRIDGE_ENV_ID is removed, repurposed, or retained, consistent with CC5.

findings.location: Identity model 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; External Dependencies 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; SC5 'REST-PUT ... via acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions an executor must trust: that Jira Cloud disallows property JQL (stated as verified, but no evidence link), that label JQL is reliable for identity search at scale, and that helper methods like AcliClient.set_issue_property exist with the assumed signature. These cannot be confirmed from plan text alone and would need checking against the live Jira instance and acli-integration module before relying on them.
findings.suggested_edit: Cite the probe/evidence for the property-JQL limitation, and confirm AcliClient.set_issue_property and the REST property endpoints exist as referenced (the capability probe partially covers this — ensure it asserts the helper API surface too).

findings.location: Out-of-band observability 'status-badge monitor or scheduled-canary GitHub Action'; External Dependencies heartbeat 'status badge / scheduled-canary action / other ... claude_has_access: depends on chosen service'; Conflict policy 'low/high risk tier per pre-cutover analysis'
findings.finding: A few unresolved design choices are pushed onto the executor. The heartbeat mechanism is left as 'status-badge monitor OR scheduled-canary OR other', with access marked 'depends on chosen service' — a real decision deferred; however CC3 claims it was already VERIFIED via story 7004's live canary, which partially resolves it (benign reading: the canary path is the chosen one). The risk-tier banding ('per pre-cutover analysis') and which ~1,300 vs ~300 items fall in each band are not enumerated, leaving the bootstrap split to be determined by an analysis not yet performed. These are choices an agent might pause on, though most have a plausible default.
findings.suggested_edit: Lock the heartbeat mechanism to the canary approach already cited in CC3, and reference where the per-class risk-tier analysis output (the ~1,300/~300 split criteria) is defined so the bootstrap bands are not improvised at execution time.

findings.location: Approach → 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)' and 'Set-valued fields ... carry per-element provenance in bridge_state/mapping.json'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a 6+ module package plus a bespoke per-element provenance model and a manifest/attestation subsystem. For a stated single-project usage this could be over-built, but it is explicitly framed as a 'platform-level investment' and the architectural switch from edge- to level-triggered genuinely needs fetch/diff/apply separation, so the modular split is plausibly justified (Rule-of-Three is arguably met by the four structural failure causes). Whether existing tracker/bridge utilities (e.g. acli-integration.AcliClient already referenced) already provide fetch/property helpers that this package would re-implement cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Note explicitly which existing helpers (e.g. acli-integration.AcliClient) the new package reuses vs. re-implements, to avoid NIH duplication of Jira REST/JQL plumbing.

findings.location: Approach, Cutover, Closure Checks — concrete file/symbol names: bridge-outbound.py/.yml, bridge-inbound.py/inbound-bridge.yml, reconcile-bridge.yml, bridge_state/mapping.json, _outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, _inbound_api, BRIDGE_ENV_ID, dso_reconciler/invariants.py

Context: 'operators currently cannot trust the bridge's audit (bridge-fsck shows 1,482 anomalies with no remediation path)' and 'Project maintainers (and any future DSO operators on Jira-mirrored projects) need a sync that converges from full snapshots'

Whole plan; EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural, yet 'Dependencies: None' and no child tickets. Approach spans a new 6-module package, identity model, dedup/self-cleaning, conflict policy, concurrency/snapshot isolation, 429/5xx/401 contracts, banded bootstrap, alerting, hard cutover+rollback, and out-of-band observability.
findings.finding: The ticket is labeled architectural/epic-class but is presented as a single monolithic unit with no decomposition into children. The scope (replace two workflows with a new package implementing differ/applier/provenance + bootstrap migration of ~1,300+ items + cutover + rollback + heartbeat monitor) is far too large for one coherent execution unit and bundles independently-shippable slices (e.g. capability probe, reconciler core, bootstrap migration, cutover, observability) under one hard cutover. Several Success Criteria are also marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED' via separate stories (7339, 7004, 7004 canary), implying child work already exists but is not represented in the decomposition.
findings.suggested_edit: Split into child tickets along vertical slices: (1) capability/forward-compat probes, (2) reconciler core package (fetch/diff/apply/identity), (3) banded bootstrap migration, (4) hard cutover + rollback playbook, (5) out-of-band heartbeat + weekly fsck audit. Reference the already-closed stories (7339, 7004) as completed children rather than embedding their results as 'already satisfied' criteria.

findings.location: Approach > New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health); and 'Set-valued fields ... carry per-element provenance in bridge_state/mapping.json'
findings.finding: The plan replaces two existing event-driven scripts (bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py) with a fresh 6-module package plus a new mapping.json provenance model and a new dedup/auto-merge invariant engine. The architectural-class epic and full-snapshot reconcile design plausibly justify a rewrite, so this is not clearly over-engineering. But two areas merit a plan-text-only flag for possible reinvention/proliferation: (1) the bespoke per-element provenance tagging (origin: local|jira, 'removed locally' vs 'never existed locally') is a sophisticated three-valued merge model used across labels/watchers/links — confirm this is needed at >1 call-site rather than a single field, per Rule-of-Three; (2) the existing acli-integration.AcliClient is referenced for property writes — confirm the new fetcher/applier do not re-implement Jira REST plumbing that AcliClient already provides. Needs codebase access to confirm NIH.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line note confirming dso_reconciler reuses acli-integration.AcliClient for all Jira REST calls (no new HTTP layer), and confirm provenance-tagging is applied to ≥2 set-valued field types before building the generic abstraction.

findings.location: Approach (entire) + Success Criteria 1-7; EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural but 'Dependencies: None' and no child tickets listed
findings.finding: This is declared an architectural epic yet is presented as a single undecomposed unit of work with no children. The scope is very large: a 6-module reconciler package, a three-valued set-field provenance merge model, dedup auto-merge with grace windows, 429/5xx/401 contracts, snapshot-isolation with rebase-then-retry, a banded bootstrap over ~1,300+~300 items, anomaly auto-filing with dedup windows, hard cutover + rollback playbook, out-of-band heartbeat, and a weekly audit pass. These are naturally separable vertical slices (e.g., identity/dedup core; reconcile loop + conflict policy; bootstrap/migration; cutover+rollback; observability/heartbeat) that should be children of the epic. As one leaf ticket this is too big to execute coherently in a single session and invites partial/incoherent delivery.
