Note also the success-criteria 'in-session analog' substitutions and pre-satisfied SC7/CC items (story 7339, story 7004) suggest the work is in fact being done in pieces — which argues for explicit decomposition rather than a single ticket.
findings.suggested_edit: Either re-scope as an epic with explicit child tickets (suggested seams: (1) reconciler core: fetcher/differ/applier/mapping + identity model; (2) conflict policy + set-field provenance; (3) banded bootstrap/migration; (4) cutover + rollback playbook; (5) observability: heartbeat + weekly audit), each with its own ACs; or, if this is intentionally a leaf, document why it cannot be split and which already-closed children (7339, 7004) it depends on.

findings.location: Whole plan — 'Approach' spans Identity model, Dedup, Conflict policy, Concurrency, 429/5xx/401 contracts, Bootstrap, Anomaly alerting, Cutover/rollback, Out-of-band observability; EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural
findings.finding: This is labeled an architectural epic (EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural) but is presented as a single executable plan with one cutover commit. It bundles at least 8 substantial, separable workstreams — a brand-new Python package (fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health), a label-primary identity model, set-valued field provenance tracking, a banded bootstrap, an anomaly auto-filing path, a cutover+rollback playbook, an out-of-band heartbeat monitor, and a weekly fsck audit. Each is plausibly its own ticket. For an epic, the plan does not present a decomposition into child tickets; it reads as one monolithic unit of work that cannot be coherently executed/reviewed in one session.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into children along vertical seams, e.g.: (1) reconciler package skeleton + fetcher/differ/applier with identity model; (2) dedup + at-most-one invariant + self-cleaning; (3) conflict policy + set-valued provenance; (4) banded bootstrap + dry-run manifest; (5) anomaly auto-filing; (6) cutover + rollback playbook; (7) out-of-band heartbeat + weekly audit. Keep each child independently shippable.

findings.location: Approach sections 'Conflict policy' (Local-wins, set-valued provenance, jira-* mirroring) and 'Concurrency + isolation' (rebase-then-retry, snapshot-isolation contract) vs Success Criteria 1-7 and Closure Checks
findings.finding: Several substantial described behaviors have no acceptance criterion: (a) the conflict policy (local-wins on destructive changes, additive inbound merge, per-element provenance with 'removed locally' vs 'never existed locally') is richly specified but no Success Criterion or Closure Check verifies merge/provenance behavior; (b) the snapshot-isolation contract and rebase-then-retry write strategy are unverified; (c) the per-pass duration ceiling (15 min abort) has no criterion. Conversely, terminology drifts: the workflow file is named 'bridge-outbound.yml'/'inbound-bridge.yml' in Closure Checks but the python scripts are 'bridge-outbound.py'/'bridge-inbound.py' (outbound/inbound ordering flips), and the Closure Check 'gh api ...{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml' assumes filenames not consistently stated. These naming inconsistencies risk the agent verifying the wrong artifact.
findings.suggested_edit: Add criteria covering conflict policy / provenance merge, snapshot-staleness deferral, and the duration ceiling. Standardize workflow filenames (pick e.g. bridge-outbound.yml / bridge-inbound.yml) and use them identically in Approach, Success Criteria, and Closure Checks.

findings.location: Context section
Success Criteria 1-7 and 'tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh' (SC6)
findings.finding: Testing leans heavily on live-execution against the real DIG Jira project and a deployed workflow rather than unit/contract tests of the new modules (differ, applier, mapping provenance, invariants). There is no RED-before-GREEN ordering, no unit coverage of failure-path contracts (429 backoff budget, 5xx abort, 401 fatal/P1-file, duration-ceiling abort, snapshot-staleness deferral), and no boundary tests for the at-most-one-dso_local_id invariant grace window (2-consecutive-pass requirement). SC6's drift harness covers 3 happy-path heal modes but not the rate-limit/auth abort paths. The conflict-merge provenance logic ('removed locally' vs 'never existed locally') has no described test at all.
findings.suggested_edit: Add unit/contract tests for differ/applier/invariants modules, explicitly covering 429/5xx/401 contracts, duration-ceiling abort, snapshot-staleness deferral, the 2-pass grace window before auto-merge, and provenance distinction cases. State RED-before-GREEN where new logic is introduced.

findings.location: Success Criteria 2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity'), SC7 ('ALREADY SATISFIED — user explicit override'), Closure Checks heartbeat ('VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004'), and the 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' preamble replacing time-based requirements
findings.finding: Multiple criteria substitute weaker in-session analogs for the durability/soak properties they are meant to prove, and two are pre-declared satisfied rather than verifiable by the executing agent. SC2's 'spanning normal local activity' and '3 consecutive passes' is the core durability guarantee — running three back-to-back workflow_dispatch invocations does NOT demonstrate convergence across real 20-min cadence with intervening activity; this is a measurability gap, not just a time saving. The Closure Check replacing 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%' with '5 consecutive green runs' is asserted as 'stronger evidence' but is not equivalent — it cannot surface intermittent/cadence-triggered failures. SC7 and the heartbeat check are marked ALREADY SATISFIED via prior-story closure / user override, so the agent cannot independently verify them this session. The out-of-band heartbeat dependency is itself user_setup_required (External Dependencies), making in-session completion dependent on a manual external action.
findings.suggested_edit: Either retain a genuine (even if short) scheduled-cadence soak for SC2 and the failure-rate check, or explicitly mark those as out-of-session/post-deploy verification rather than relabeling them as equivalent. For SC7/heartbeat marked ALREADY SATISFIED, move them out of this ticket's Success Criteria or cite re-verifiable evidence the executing agent can reproduce.

findings.location: Approach 'plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/', Cutover 'deletes both event-driven workflows', Closure Check 'No references to bridge-outbound.py, bridge-inbound.py, _outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, _inbound_api remain in plugins/dso/, .github/, .claude/'
findings.finding: Edit targets are named and largely plausible (new package modules, workflow files, bridge_state/mapping.json, acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property). However the blast radius of deleting bridge-outbound.py/bridge-inbound.py and the cursor/handler symbols cannot be confirmed without the live codebase — the plan's own Closure Check greps for residual references but does not enumerate the current consumers/callers of those modules (anything importing _outbound_handlers/_inbound_api, scheduled-workflow YAML cross-refs, docs). The retained BRIDGE_ENV_ID is said to be consumed by dso_reconciler/invariants.py, a module not listed in the package's stated modules (fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health), an internal inconsistency in named symbols. Confirmation of full consumer set requires codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Enumerate known importers/callers of the deleted modules and cursor symbols up front, and reconcile the module list: add 'invariants' to the dso_reconciler module enumeration since Closure Checks/SC5 reference dso_reconciler/invariants.py.

findings.location: Entire Approach section — 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)' plus identity model, dedup, conflict policy, concurrency, rate-limit contracts, bootstrap, alerting, cutover/rollback, observability; EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural
findings.finding: This is a single ticket of architectural scope that bundles at least eight independently-substantial workstreams: a 6-module reconciler package, a label+property identity model, set-valued provenance merge logic, concurrency/snapshot-isolation contracts, 429/5xx/401 retry policy, a banded bootstrap migration of ~1,300 items, anomaly-driven auto-bug-filing, and a hard cutover+rollback playbook. It is marked class:architectural ('platform-level investment') yet has no decomposition into children. This is far too big to execute coherently in one session and should be split into a parent epic with children (e.g. identity/dedup core, conflict/provenance merge, rate-limit & concurrency contracts, bootstrap migration, alerting, cutover/rollback).
findings.suggested_edit: Convert this into a parent epic and decompose into vertical-slice children: (1) reconciler skeleton + fetcher/differ/applier with idempotent no-op pass; (2) label-primary identity + dedup invariant; (3) conflict/provenance merge; (4) rate-limit/concurrency/snapshot-isolation contracts; (5) banded bootstrap migration; (6) anomaly alerting; (7) cutover + rollback playbook + heartbeat. Each child carries its own ACs.

findings.location: Approach 'Conflict policy' (set-valued provenance, jira-* mirroring), 'Out-of-band observability' weekly fsck, vs Success Criteria 1-7 and Closure Checks; also terminology 'bridge-outbound.yml/inbound-bridge.yml' vs 'bridge-inbound.py/bridge-outbound.py'
findings.finding: Several substantial described mechanisms have no dedicated success criterion: the per-element provenance model for set-valued fields ('removed locally' vs 'never existed locally') is a complex feature with no verifying AC; the conflict policy (local-wins on destructive, additive merge inbound) is unverified; the 'jira-* wholly-new mirror becomes local-authoritative' flow is only incidentally touched in SC3. Conversely terminology is inconsistent for the deleted workflow files: the Approach says 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py' and 'reconcile-bridge.yml', the Cutover says 'deletes both event-driven workflows', but the Closure Check names 'bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml' (outbound vs inbound ordering and the inbound-bridge vs bridge-inbound naming flip create ambiguity about the actual filenames).
findings.suggested_edit: Add ACs covering conflict-policy and set-valued provenance behavior (e.g. inject a local label-removal + a Jira label-add and assert both resolve correctly). Standardize the exact workflow filenames in one place and reference them consistently (bridge-outbound.yml/bridge-inbound.yml).

findings.location: Context section
Success Criteria 5,6 and absence of unit-test/RED-before-GREEN description; 'tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh'
findings.finding: Testing is almost entirely live-integration end-to-end (workflow_dispatch against the live DIG project). There is no described unit-test coverage for the high-risk pure-logic components: the differ closure-check (reconciler-owned fields excluded from diff input), the at-most-one dedup invariant + 2-pass grace window, set-valued provenance reconciliation, and the 429/backoff/budget logic. These are exactly the failure-path/boundary behaviors that need deterministic tests rather than live probes. No RED-before-GREEN ordering is described for any new module. The drift_injection harness covers only 3 happy-path heal modes; failure paths (401 fatal abort, budget-exceeded clean abort, snapshot-staleness defer) have no test.
findings.suggested_edit: Add unit-test ACs for differ closure (assert reconciler-owned fields never appear as diffable mutations), dedup-invariant grace window (1-pass=no merge, 2-pass=merge), and rate-limit contracts (401→fatal+P1, budget-exceeded→clean abort) using mocked Jira responses, ordered RED-before-GREEN.

findings.location: Success Criteria 2 ('holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity'), SC7 ('ALREADY SATISFIED ... user explicit override'), Closure Check '~20-min schedule' and heartbeat 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004', and the '2026-05-24 verification protocol revision' replacing all time-based requirements
findings.finding: Multiple criteria have been redefined to dodge their own measurability. SC2's durability requirement ('spanning normal local activity', implicitly over time) is replaced by back-to-back dispatches with one injected update — a weaker analog that no longer measures durability under real cadence. SC7 is declared ALREADY SATISFIED by user override with DD3 explicitly 'structurally unverifiable post-cutover' — i.e. an unverified criterion marked done. The external heartbeat (External Dependencies: ownership user_setup_required, claude_has_access depends) is asserted VERIFIED via a prior story rather than verifiable in this session. The blanket 'time-based requirements replaced with in-session analogs' note risks masking genuinely out-of-session behavior (steady-state convergence over real 20-min cadence, 30-day failure rate). These should be flagged as not in-session completable in their true form, not silently substituted.
findings.suggested_edit: Keep the in-session analogs but explicitly mark SC2-durability, SC7-DD3, and the heartbeat-on-real-disable as out-of-session/operator-verified follow-ups rather than 'satisfied'. State the residual risk each analog does not cover.

findings.location: 'Replace both event-driven bridge workflows (bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py)'; Closure Check 'No references to bridge-outbound.py, bridge-inbound.py, _outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, or _inbound_api remain in plugins/dso/, .github/, .claude/'; 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'
findings.finding: Named edit targets (dso_reconciler package, bridge-*.py/.yml, _outbound_cursor/_inbound_api symbols, AcliClient.set_issue_property) are plausible but cannot be confirmed without the live codebase. The blast radius of deleting both bridges and BRIDGE_ENV_ID semantics (retained but repurposed into dso_reconciler/invariants.py) implies downstream consumers — anything that reads _outbound_cursor restore-point artifacts, /dso:fix-bug invocation contract, ticket-bridge-fsck.py output schema — that are not enumerated as edit targets. The forward-compat probe acknowledges old-bridge consumers, which is good, but the full consumer list for the deleted symbols is not provided.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm against the codebase that the grep targets and AcliClient.set_issue_property/get_issue_property exist as named, and enumerate consumers of _outbound_cursor and the fsck output schema that the reconciler must satisfy.

findings.location: Conflict policy section (set-valued field provenance, local-wins-on-destructive, jira-* mirroring) and Success Criteria 1-7
findings.finding: The plan defines a rich conflict-resolution contract (per-element provenance, 'removed locally' vs 'never existed locally', local-wins-on-destructive-changes, additive-merge inbound) and a snapshot-isolation contract (re-read HEAD, defer on staleness), but NO Success Criterion or test exercises these behavioral paths. SC1-7 cover convergence count, open-count parity, dedup, idempotency, the dual-property alert path, synthetic drift (orphan/mislabel/missing-prop), and rollback — none asserts e.g. that a label removed locally propagates outbound while a label added in Jira merges inbound, nor that a mid-pass HEAD advance defers inbound state. These are the most error-prone parts of the design and lack failure-path/boundary coverage.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Success Criterion (or extend the drift-injection harness in SC6) that injects a set-valued conflict (local removal + concurrent Jira addition of the same label/watcher/link) and asserts the documented provenance outcome, plus a snapshot-staleness test that advances tickets-branch HEAD mid-pass and asserts inbound deferral.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; Dependencies: 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified'; SC5 references 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'
findings.finding: Several environment/codebase assertions are asserted-as-verified but can only be confirmed against the live Jira instance and existing code: (a) JQL-on-entity-properties unsupported on Cloud; (b) label JQL reliably finds issues immediately after create (Jira label/JQL indexing is eventually consistent, which could undermine the 'search-by-label before create' dedup path); (c) the helper `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property` exists and supports per-issue property PUT; (d) `_outbound_cursor` / inbound cursor artifacts exist and are capturable. An executor must verify these against the live Jira and the current codebase before relying on them. I lack codebase/Jira access to confirm or refute.
findings.suggested_edit: Mark the label-JQL eventual-consistency risk explicitly and add a probe step asserting a freshly-created issue's label is findable by JQL within the pass window; cite the file path where `AcliClient.set_issue_property` lives and confirm the cursor artifact names against the current bridge code.

findings.location: Bootstrap: 'Each band carries a risk tier (low/high) per pre-cutover analysis'; '~1,300 low-risk items'; '~300 high-risk items'; Conflict policy: 'become local-authoritative'
findings.finding: The low/high risk-tier split that drives the bootstrap (one bulk pass for ~1,300 low-risk vs drip-feed for ~300 high-risk) is deferred to an unspecified 'pre-cutover analysis' with no stated classification rule. An executor cannot determine which anomaly falls in which tier without making a design decision. Similarly, the per-class reviewer attestation ('explicit per-class reviewer attestation') requires a human reviewer mid-execution, which an autonomous agent cannot self-satisfy without clarification. Both push real decisions onto the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Specify the low/high risk-tier classification predicate per anomaly class (e.g. orphans with intact dso-id label = low; duplicates needing merge = high), and state who provides the per-class attestation and how the agent obtains/records it within session.

findings.location: Success Criteria header 'verified in-session via live execution' and SC2 'trigger ... back-to-back, not separated by 20min' vs Closure Check 'reconcile-bridge.yml runs on the configured ~20-min schedule'
findings.finding: Internal tension between the durability/soak intent and the in-session analogs. SC2 asserts 'durable parity ... across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' but the verification runs three back-to-back workflow_dispatch passes NOT separated by the 20-min cadence and substitutes a single injected update for 'normal local activity' — the analog does not establish the durability the criterion names. Likewise the Closure Check claims the schedule is verified while only running manual dispatches, and the 'deterministic absence-of-runs' check asserts deleted YAMLs cannot fire but does not verify the new ~20-min cron actually fires on schedule. The headline criteria and their substituted verifications make materially weaker claims than stated.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) retain at least one verification that the ~20-min cron actually fires unattended (e.g. wait for one scheduled run), or (b) reword the criteria to drop 'durable'/'spanning normal local activity'/'runs on schedule' so the criterion text matches what the in-session analog actually proves.

findings.location: Approach sections 'Conflict policy', 'Out-of-band observability' (weekly fsck + P2 unclassified drift), 'Anomaly alerting' (max 5 tickets/pass + 24h dedup) vs Success Criteria / Closure Checks
findings.finding: Several described work items have no corresponding acceptance criterion: (1) the entire conflict policy / per-element provenance model is described but unverified by any SC (orphan described-work); (2) the anomaly-alert per-pass cap (max 5 new tickets/pass) and 24h identical-anomaly dedup are described but no criterion exercises the cap or dedup window (SC5 verifies a single alert only). Conversely, SC7 and several Closure Checks are marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED / VERIFIED' by prior story closures (7339-ce88, 7004, c03a-2e77) rather than mapping to work in this plan — these read as orphan criteria relative to the described work. Terminology is also inconsistent for the deleted workflow files: 'bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml' (Closure Check) vs 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py' (Approach) vs '{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml' (gh api) — three different naming orders/extensions for the same artifacts.
findings.suggested_edit: Add criteria for the conflict-policy provenance behavior and the alert-cap/dedup window; normalize the workflow filenames to a single canonical form everywhere; and move pre-satisfied items (SC7) into a clearly-labeled 'carried evidence' section rather than mixing them with this ticket's verifications.

findings.location: External Dependencies: 'External heartbeat monitor ... ownership: user_setup_required; handling: user_manual'; Closure Check 'out-of-band heartbeat alert configured and verified firing'; Bootstrap 'per-class reviewer attestation'; SC2 '7 days' first-week tightening
findings.finding: Some criteria are not in-session completable by the agent: (a) the out-of-band heartbeat monitor is explicitly 'user_setup_required / user_manual' with 'claude_has_access: depends on chosen service' — its Closure Check is marked verified via a prior story but a fresh run cannot self-satisfy it; (b) per-class reviewer attestation requires a human; (c) 'First-week tightening: ≤10 mutations/pass + per-pass operator review for 7 days' is an inherently multi-day operational requirement with no in-session analog provided (unlike the other time-based items which were given analogs). These need either a documented in-session substitute or explicit out-of-session designation.
findings.suggested_edit: Provide an in-session analog for the 7-day first-week tightening and the operator-review/attestation steps (or mark them explicitly as post-merge operational items not gating this ticket's completion), and confirm whether the heartbeat monitor can be configured with available auth or must be a user_manual gate.

findings.location: Approach > 'Conflict policy' and 'Set-valued fields carry per-element provenance' vs Success Criteria (which only test bootstrap convergence, parity, dedup, idempotency, one alert path, and synthetic drift).
findings.finding: The most complex and risk-bearing behaviors of the plan have no test coverage in the Success Criteria. Specifically: (1) the conflict policy (local-wins on destructive changes vs additive inbound merge) is never exercised by any SC; (2) per-element set-valued provenance ('removed locally' vs 'never existed locally', origin tagging, local removals propagating outbound vs Jira additions merging inbound) — the hardest correctness surface — has no boundary/failure-path test; (3) snapshot-isolation contract (HEAD-advanced-mid-pass defer) is untested; (4) 429/5xx/401 contracts and the per-pass REST budget abort path are not verified by any SC (only the no-429 happy path is observed in a Closure Check). SC6 ('synthetic drift heals') covers only orphan/mislabel/missing-prop modes, not conflict or set-merge semantics. There is also no RED-before-GREEN or failure-injection ordering for these paths beyond SC5/SC6.
findings.suggested_edit: Add Success Criteria (or expand SC6 drift modes) that inject: a local destructive change concurrent with a Jira additive change and assert local-wins + additive-merge; a label removed-locally vs Jira-added-label scenario asserting correct provenance resolution; a mid-pass HEAD advance asserting inbound deferral; and a forced 429/401 to assert clean abort / P1 file. Specify expected (RED) state before reconcile and asserted (GREEN) state after.

findings.location: Approach > Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; SC5: 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; Closure Checks: 'gh api repos/navapbc/digital-service-orchestra/actions/workflows/{inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml returns state=deleted'.
findings.finding: Several premises rest on environment/codebase facts an executor must confirm against the live system, which this reviewer cannot inspect: (a) existence and exact signature of 'AcliClient.set_issue_property' and 'AcliClient' label/property round-trip support; (b) whether 'gh api .../workflows/{name}.yml returns state=deleted' is a real API response shape (the GitHub API typically 404s or omits deleted workflow files rather than returning state=deleted); (c) the asserted-as-verified claim that label JQL is reliable while property JQL is unsupported on Jira Cloud. Each should be confirmed against the live codebase/API before relying on it.
findings.suggested_edit: Before cutover, have the capability probe explicitly assert each helper/API behavior (set_issue_property signature, label JQL latency/eventual-consistency, the exact gh api response for a deleted workflow) and record the observed responses, rather than asserting them as already-verified.

findings.location: Approach > Bootstrap: 'Each band carries a risk tier (low/high) per pre-cutover analysis'; '~1,300 low-risk items ... ~300 high-risk items'; 'drip-fed steady state'. Also External Dependencies > heartbeat monitor: 'status badge / scheduled-canary action / other ... claude_has_access: depends on chosen service'.
findings.finding: Two unresolved design decisions are pushed onto the executor. (1) The risk-tiering rule that splits ~1,300 low-risk from ~300 high-risk items is referenced as 'per pre-cutover analysis' but the classification criteria are never stated — the executor must invent which anomalies are low vs high risk, and the '~300 high-risk' / 'drip-fed' cadence is undefined ('drip-fed' rate unspecified beyond the first-week ≤10/pass rule). (2) The heartbeat monitor service is left as 'status badge / scheduled-canary / other' with 'depends on chosen service' — a real design choice deferred. An agent would need to stop and ask, or guess.
findings.suggested_edit: State the explicit low/high risk classification predicate per anomaly class, define the drip-fed cadence (items/pass) for high-risk items, and pick one concrete heartbeat mechanism (e.g., a scheduled-canary GitHub Action) rather than an open menu.

findings.location: Success Criteria preamble: 'verified in-session via live execution against the deployed reconcile-bridge.yml workflow on origin/main' vs Approach > Cutover: 'Hard cutover: single commit deletes both event-driven workflows and enables reconcile-bridge.yml' and SC7 'ALREADY SATISFIED'.
findings.finding: There is a sequencing contradiction: nearly all Success Criteria and Closure Checks require executing the *deployed* reconcile-bridge.yml on origin/main (post-cutover), yet the plan's own cutover step is a hard single-commit cutover gated on pre-cutover probes. Verifying SC1-SC6 'on origin/main' presupposes cutover has already landed, but cutover safety depends on bootstrap/parity results — a circular gate. Additionally, SC7 and several Closure Checks are pre-marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED / VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story ...' inside a plan still 'under review', which contradicts the framing that these are criteria to be met by this work. The Context says 'currently single-project usage' but also frames it as a platform investment for 'future DSO operators' — a milder tension but worth reconciling.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify the ordering: which SCs are verified on a staging/throwaway branch pre-cutover vs which require post-cutover origin/main, and resolve the circular dependency (e.g., bootstrap+parity verified on a non-main deployment first). Move 'already satisfied' attestations into an evidence appendix rather than embedding them as criteria.

findings.location: Approach sections 'Conflict policy', 'Set-valued field provenance', 'Snapshot-isolation contract', '429/5xx/401 contracts' vs Success Criteria 1-7.
findings.finding: Described work without any criterion (orphan description): the conflict policy, per-element set-valued provenance, snapshot-isolation contract, and the 429/5xx/401/REST-budget contracts are substantial described behaviors with no corresponding Success Criterion. Conversely the 'Anomaly alerting' per-pass cap (max 5 new tickets/pass) and 24h identical-anomaly dedup are described but only partially probed by SC5. Terminology is mostly consistent, but the workflow filename is referred to inconsistently: 'bridge-outbound.yml'/'bridge-inbound.py' vs the Closure Check 'inbound-bridge.yml'/'outbound-bridge.yml' (order flipped between sections), which could confuse deletion-target matching.
findings.suggested_edit: Add criteria for conflict policy, set-valued provenance, and the rate-limit/error contracts; standardize the workflow file names (pick either 'bridge-inbound.yml' or 'inbound-bridge.yml' and use it everywhere) so the deletion Closure Check targets the real filenames.

findings.location: Success Criteria preamble '(2026-05-24 revision)' replacing time-based requirements; SC2 'holds across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' replaced by 'three times ... back-to-back'; Closure Check '5 consecutive workflow_dispatch invocations' replacing 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%'; SC7 'ALREADY SATISFIED ... user explicit override'.
findings.finding: Several criteria substitute in-session analogs for requirements that are inherently durability/soak claims, and the substitutions are not equivalent in what they measure. '3 back-to-back passes' does not establish 'durable parity spanning normal local activity'; '5 consecutive green workflow_dispatch runs' does not establish a 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%' (it cannot observe the scheduled-cron failure modes or rare-condition flakiness the original guarded against). SC7 is marked satisfied via 'user explicit override' with DD3 ('old bridges run 1 scheduled cycle post-revert') admitted as 'structurally unverifiable' — i.e., a real verification gap waved through. These are measurable as written but the measurement does not verify the stated property; some require out-of-session/scheduled observation that an in-session analog genuinely cannot reproduce.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) keep at least one scheduled-cadence soak gate before declaring the cron-failure-rate criterion met, or (b) explicitly downgrade the original durability claims so the plan does not assert a property its verification cannot demonstrate. Flag SC7's DD3 as an accepted-known-gap rather than satisfied.

findings.location: Success Criteria #1-7 and Closure Checks, plus the '2026-05-24 revision' verification protocol note
findings.finding: The testing plan is broad on behavioral/contractual coverage (idempotency SC4, dedup SC3, conflict/alert SC5, synthetic drift heal SC6) but systematically replaces every durability/time-based requirement with same-session analogs that do NOT exercise the conditions they replace. The plan's stated core value is a sync that 'converges' and 'heals itself' over time across cadenced passes, yet: SC2's '3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' is run 'back-to-back, not separated by 20min', which defeats the snapshot-isolation/HEAD-advance contract that only manifests across real time gaps. CC3 replaces a 30-day <1% failure rate with 5 sequential green runs but provides no negative/failure-path test (429 budget exceedance, 5xx abort, 401 fatal, pass-duration-ceiling abort) actually being triggered and asserted — these contracts are designed but never RED-tested. SC7 (rollback) is marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED' by override with DD3 explicitly 'structurally unverifiable', so the rollback path is unverified. Boundary cases (the 2-consecutive-pass grace window before auto-merge, the per-pass cap of 5 tickets, the 24h dedup window) have no test.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit failure-path tests that force each error contract: inject a synthetic 429/5xx/401 and assert the documented backoff/abort/P1-file behavior; assert pass aborts on the 15-min ceiling. Add a boundary test for the 2-pass auto-merge grace window (assert NO merge after 1 pass, merge after 2). Keep at least one cadence-spanning assertion for SC2 (or explicitly justify why back-to-back is equivalent for the snapshot-isolation contract, which it is not).

findings.location: Approach: 'New Python package plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/ (modules: fetcher / differ / applier / mapping / manifest / health)' and per-element provenance in mapping.json
findings.finding: The 6-7 module decomposition and per-element provenance tagging (origin: local|jira on every label/watcher/link) for a currently single-project bridge is a sizeable abstraction surface; whether it is justified or over-engineered depends on existing helpers in plugins/dso (e.g. acli-integration.AcliClient is referenced, so a Jira client layer already exists). Cannot confirm NIH/duplication or whether the provenance machinery is exercised at more than one call-site without the live codebase. The 'platform-level investment / future DSO operators' framing for current single-project usage is a mild over-scope signal but is explicitly acknowledged by the author.

findings.location: Closure Check 'No references to ... BRIDGE_ENV_ID ...' vs Context 'fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)' listed as one of the four structural root causes
findings.finding: Cross-section contradiction: the Context names BRIDGE_ENV_ID as one of the four structural causes of the failure ('fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)') that the level-triggered redesign is meant to eliminate, but a Closure Check states 'BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained — it is consumed by the post-cutover reconciler (dso_reconciler/invariants.py, bridge_alerts dedup)'. The plan does not explain how the retained BRIDGE_ENV_ID in the new reconciler avoids the fragility that made it a root cause — it reads as the same token surviving the redesign that was supposed to remove it. Separately, SC2 claims to verify 'spanning normal local activity' but the verification injects a single deliberate update between passes 2 and 3, contradicting the snapshot-isolation/HEAD-advance contract in 'Concurrency + isolation' which only triggers when HEAD advances mid-pass (back-to-back dispatches give no real mid-pass window).
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile the BRIDGE_ENV_ID treatment: either explain in Approach how the reconciler's continued use of BRIDGE_ENV_ID is no longer fragile (e.g. it is now confirmatory not authoritative), or update the Context so BRIDGE_ENV_ID is not listed as a root cause being removed. Align SC2's verification text with the snapshot-isolation contract.

findings.location: Success Criteria 1-6 and Closure Checks; e.g. SC6 `tests/drift_injection/inject-and-heal.sh` and SC5 anomaly alert path
findings.finding: Testing plan is broadly strong: it covers behavioral/contractual paths (idempotent 0-mutation manifests, anomaly alert end-to-end, synthetic drift heal-back-to-fsck-0), failure paths (429/5xx/401 contracts, dedup-invariant violation), boundaries (per-pass REST budget cap, ±5 drift, 2-consecutive-pass grace window), and legitimate structural greps (Closure Check 5 reference-removal). One thin spot: there is no explicit RED-before-GREEN/negative assertion that the drift-injection harness actually FAILS fsck (exits non-zero) immediately after injection and BEFORE the reconcile pass — SC6 only asserts exit 0 after healing, so a no-op harness could pass vacuously.
findings.suggested_edit: In SC6, add an intermediate assertion: after each mode injects its anomaly and BEFORE calling reconcile-bridge.yml, assert bridge-fsck exits non-zero (RED), then assert exit 0 after the pass (GREEN).

findings.location: Success Criteria header '(2026-05-24 revision)' and SC7/Closure Checks marked 'ALREADY SATISFIED' / 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24' (e.g. SC7 story 7339, CC heartbeat story 7004, CC3 5-run analog)
findings.finding: The title/goal ('Replace edge-triggered bridge with level-triggered reconciler') is faithfully served by the Approach. However the verification-protocol revision systematically substitutes the title's durability intent (the reconciler must be trustworthy over time — 'durable parity', 'rolling 30-day failure rate <1%', '7-day soak', 'no scheduled runs for 7 days') with same-session back-to-back analogs, and pre-marks SC7 and two Closure Checks as already satisfied via user override. This is a potential dilution of the stated goal rather than scope drift in the build itself; whether the in-session analogs preserve 'the same level of empirical confidence' is a judgement that cannot be confirmed from plan text alone. Flagging as a fidelity risk to escalate, not asserting the substitution is wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: Have the author/reviewer explicitly confirm acceptance that durability criteria are being demonstrated by N-consecutive-run analogs rather than wall-clock soak, and that pre-satisfied items (SC7, heartbeat CC) retain valid evidence; document the residual risk (e.g. SC7 DD3 'old bridges run 1 scheduled cycle' is structurally unverifiable post-cutover).

findings.location: Approach: new package `plugins/dso/scripts/dso_reconciler/` with modules fetcher/differ/applier/mapping/manifest/health/invariants; and 'currently single-project usage, treated as a platform-level investment'
findings.finding: Per-element provenance tracking (`origin: local | jira` on every label/watcher/link) and the multi-module package are non-trivial abstraction for a stated single-project (DIG-only) deployment, which risks over-engineering against a single call-site. That said, the plan replaces TWO existing event-driven workflows with one reconciler (a consolidation, not pure NIH) and explicitly frames the breadth as a deliberate platform investment, so a benign reading applies. Cannot confirm whether equivalent reconciler/diff machinery already exists in the codebase without repo access.
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm no existing reconcile/diff utility in plugins/dso can be reused; consider whether per-element provenance is needed for the v1 single-project scope or can be deferred until a second mirrored project actually exists (Rule-of-Three).

findings.location: Closure Check 'No references to ... remain' vs Closure Check 'BRIDGE_ENV_ID is intentionally retained'; and Context lists BRIDGE_ENV_ID as a 'structural cause' (fragile single-token echo prevention)
findings.finding: Cross-section tension on BRIDGE_ENV_ID. The Context names 'fragile single-token echo prevention (BRIDGE_ENV_ID)' as one of the four structural causes being eliminated, yet a Closure Check explicitly retains BRIDGE_ENV_ID for the new reconciler (invariants.py / bridge_alerts dedup). It is plausible the token is reused for a different, non-fragile purpose, but the plan never states how the new use avoids the 'fragile single-token echo prevention' failure mode it was blamed for — leaving a reader unable to reconcile 'cause to remove' with 'intentionally retained'. Secondary coherence note: the Closure Check reference-removal grep also lists `_outbound_cursor`, while the rollback playbook and SC pre-cutover step require capturing `_outbound_cursor` to a restore-point artifact — these can coexist (capture before deletion) but should be sequenced explicitly to avoid contradiction.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence explaining how the reconciler's reuse of BRIDGE_ENV_ID (echo/loop prevention via label+property identity, not the single-token mechanism) avoids the original fragility, reconciling the Context 'cause' with the retention. Also clarify ordering: _outbound_cursor is captured to the restore-point artifact during cutover, then removed, so the reference-removal grep applies to the final post-cutover state.

findings.location: Identity model: 'JQL on entity properties is NOT supported on Jira Cloud — design verified during planning'; SC5: 'acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property'; CC: 'plugins/dso/scripts/rollback-bridge-cutover.sh STEP 1 merge-commit detection'; External Dependencies: 'acli + JIRA_API_TOKEN verified'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several environment/codebase assertions that an executor must confirm against live code/Jira before relying on them: (a) that JQL entity-property search is unsupported on Jira Cloud (drives the entire label-primary identity model); (b) that helper `acli-integration.AcliClient.set_issue_property` exists and supports PUT of a second property value; (c) that `ticket-bridge-fsck.py`, `.claude/scripts/dso ticket list`, and `rollback-bridge-cutover.sh STEP 1 merge-commit detection` exist with the described behavior. I lack the live codebase/Jira to confirm these.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit pre-execution verification step that re-confirms the JQL-property limitation and the existence/signature of AcliClient.set_issue_property and ticket-bridge-fsck.py before depending on them; cite the planning evidence inline.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'Verification protocol (2026-05-24 revision)' and SC7 ('ALREADY SATISFIED — ... user explicit override'); CC3 heartbeat ('VERIFIED 2026-05-24 via story 7004')
findings.finding: Several criteria are not independently measurable in-session as written. SC2 originally required holding 'across 3 consecutive passes spanning normal local activity' and CC require multi-day soak / rolling-30d failure rates — the revision substitutes back-to-back workflow_dispatch runs and asserts these are 'stronger evidence', but back-to-back runs do not exercise the durability/scheduling/time-separation property the criteria target, so the in-session analog measures something weaker than the stated requirement. SC7 and the heartbeat CC are marked pre-satisfied by override/another story rather than verified by this plan, so they are not measurable within this ticket's execution.
findings.suggested_edit: For durability/soak criteria, keep the original time-based requirement and explicitly schedule a follow-up verification rather than asserting back-to-back runs are equivalent; or justify why the scheduled-cron path is exercised. Re-derive heartbeat/rollback evidence within this ticket or link the override authority explicitly.

findings.location: Approach 'Out-of-band observability' (heartbeat + weekly fsck) vs Closure Checks; 'First-week tightening: ≤10 mutations/pass + per-pass operator review for 7 days'
findings.finding: Two near-orphan mappings: (1) the 'First-week tightening: ≤10 mutations/pass for 7 days' operational requirement in the Bootstrap section has no corresponding Success Criterion or Closure Check verifying it. (2) Terminology drift on workflow filenames: the body says 'bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py' and 'enables reconcile-bridge.yml', but the Closure Check refers to 'bridge-outbound.yml and inbound-bridge.yml' (note the inverted order/naming 'inbound-bridge.yml' vs 'bridge-inbound') and `gh api ... {inbound,outbound}-bridge.yml` — inconsistent workflow filenames could cause the deletion/verification step to target the wrong file.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a criterion (or explicitly defer) for the 7-day tightening window. Pin one canonical name per workflow file (e.g. bridge-inbound.yml vs inbound-bridge.yml) and use it everywhere in the deletion Closure Check.

findings.location: Approach: 'Replace both event-driven bridge workflows (bridge-outbound.py + bridge-inbound.py)'; CC: 'No references to bridge-outbound.py, bridge-inbound.py, _outbound_cursor, _outbound_handlers, or _inbound_api remain in plugins/dso/, .github/, or .claude/'
findings.finding: Edit set is named and the blast radius (deleting two workflows + scripts, new package dso_reconciler/, bridge_state/ on tickets branch) is acknowledged. However, the plan does not enumerate the consumers/callers of bridge-outbound.py/bridge-inbound.py beyond a grep-style reference sweep — any code or workflow that imports _outbound_handlers/_inbound_api or triggers these scripts (e.g. cron schedules, composite actions, docs) is only verifiable against the live repo. Cannot confirm completeness of the caller list from plan text.
findings.suggested_edit: List the known importers/callers of the deleted modules (or state 'grep-verified at execution') so the executor knows the expected blast radius rather than discovering it during the reference sweep.

findings.location: Context section: '1,482 fsck anomalies ... operators currently cannot trust the bridge's audit'; 'Project maintainers (and any future DSO operators on Jira-mirrored projects)'
Success Criteria revision banner; SC7 'ALREADY SATISFIED'; CC heartbeat 'VERIFIED 2026-05-24'; SC2 back-to-back substitution
findings.finding: There is a gap between the stated end-state and the verification path. The end-state requires durable parity across scheduled passes and a working out-of-band heartbeat under real workflow-disable conditions; the verification substitutes back-to-back manual dispatches and cross-ticket/override evidence. The deterministic 'deleted YAMLs cannot fire crons' argument is sound for the deletion check, but the 'durable across passes' and '30d failure rate' criteria are declared satisfied by weaker in-session analogs, leaving a real risk that the reconciler is termination-verified on triggered runs but never proven on its actual ~20-min schedule. Termination signal for the 7-day tightening window is also undefined.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly separate 'in-session acceptance' from 'post-deploy soak verification' as a tracked follow-up, rather than redefining soak criteria as already met; define the stop condition for the first-week tightening window.

findings.location: EPIC_CLASS: class:architectural; Approach spans identity model, dedup, conflict policy, concurrency, rate-limit contracts, bootstrap, alerting, cutover/rollback, observability
findings.finding: This is tagged as an architectural epic but is written as a single monolithic ticket with one combined Success Criteria/Closure Checks list rather than decomposed into children. The scope (new multi-module package, conflict/provenance model, bootstrap migration of ~1,300 items, rate-limit/concurrency contracts, hard cutover, rollback playbook, out-of-band observability) is far too large to execute coherently in one session and contains independently shippable slices (e.g. reconciler core, bootstrap migration, cutover/rollback, observability). No child decomposition is present.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into vertical child tickets (e.g. (1) reconciler package + identity/dedup, (2) conflict/provenance merge, (3) bootstrap migration, (4) cutover + rollback playbook, (5) out-of-band observability) each with its own acceptance criteria, leaving this as the parent epic.

findings.location: AC: 'Criteria registry ... reusing rebar.llm extension points', 'extends the close-gate signing', '`claim` integration', 'reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan references specific rebar subsystems it intends to touch/extend (rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, the close-gate signing path, the `claim` command/code path, the event reducer, solidlsp/ast-grep integrations) but names no concrete files, modules, or symbols. Blast radius is acknowledged conceptually (reuse extension points, reducer-ignored sidecar to stay 'out of hot paths'), and consumers like the reducer and CI checker are explicitly called out, so this is not a hallucinated-target failure. However, the actual edit targets and the full set of callers that touch `claim`/the state-transition path cannot be confirmed without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Optional: enumerate the concrete files/modules for each integration point (claim command, status-transition reducer, signing module reused from the close-gate, rebar.llm orchestrator entry points) and the known callers of the open->in_progress transition, so the executing agent can confirm blast radius rather than rediscover it.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Attestation: ... extends the close-gate signing'; Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'ast-grep'
findings.finding: As an epic-level plan the edit set is described by capability area (Layer-1/Layer-2 criteria, registry+orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT event, config, docs) rather than concrete files/symbols. Several named touch-points are plausibly grounded — it explicitly reuses existing rebar.llm extension points, extends the existing close-gate signing, leverages solidlsp 'already in repo', and adds a new claim-path check and a reducer-ignored event. Blast radius IS acknowledged in a couple of places: the claim hot-path is called out ('claim is a FAST check ... heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND') and the reducer is explicitly told to skip the REVIEW_RESULT type. However, the principal consumer/caller that this kind of change requires updating — the claim/state-transition command and the reducer that processes ticket events — is named only abstractly, and confirming the actual file/symbol targets (claim command module, reducer dispatch, signing module, llm catalog/contracts) requires the live codebase. No edit target appears hallucinated, and high blast radius is acknowledged, so this is not a failure; it needs codebase confirmation of the specific integration points.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally enumerate the concrete modules to be modified (claim/state-transition command, the ticket-event reducer that must skip REVIEW_RESULT, the close-gate signing module being extended, the rebar.llm catalog/contracts entry points) so the agent can confirm blast radius against the live code before executing.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list vs. Design summary; e.g. AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' vs. design 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Coherence is strong overall: each AC maps to a design-summary section and vice-versa (Layer-1, Layer-2, registry/orchestrator, attestation, claim, routing/budget, feedback spec, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion config, bugs-exempt, defaults, docs). Terminology is consistent (Layer-1/Layer-2, advisory/blocking, INDETERMINATE, material-event-commit binding, fail-open). Two very minor near-overlaps worth noting (not true duplicates): (a) 'criteria↔description coherence' appears as a Layer-2 advisory criterion AC and is conceptually adjacent to the broader 'intent fidelity'/'coherence' items — fine as distinct checks but worth confirming they are not the same check named twice; (b) the 'Feedback-authoring conforms to coaching spec' AC and the Layer-2 'anti-slop' / coaching-spec design text restate the same coaching requirements, which is acceptable since one is the spec and one is the conformance gate.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally add a one-line note clarifying that the Layer-2 'criteria↔description coherence' criterion (a check the gate runs on client tickets) is distinct from E1-style internal review, to avoid the reader conflating the gate's own coherence check with general intent fidelity.

findings.location: ## Context paragraph: 'when an agent claims a ticket... nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound' and 'early errors compound over autonomous agent trajectories (~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects)'; plus 'a rebar FEATURE used by a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE'

Acceptance Criteria list vs. Design summary; e.g. AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' vs. Success Criteria + Design summary 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Coherence is strong overall: each AC maps to a described mechanism (Layer-1/Layer-2 to the 'Two layers' section, attestation AC to 'Attestation', REVIEW_RESULT AC to 'Observability / FP substrate', claim AC to 'Trigger / enforcement', per-criterion config AC to 'Verdict model', bugs-exempt AC to 'Bugs are SKIPPED'). Terminology is used consistently (Layer-1/Layer-2, advisory/blocking-with-override, INDETERMINATE, material event, fail-open, attestation/certification). One thin spot: 'certification' (Context) and 'attestation' (Design/AC) appear to denote the same signed signal but are never explicitly equated, which could mildly confuse the implementer. Also minor near-overlap exists between AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec' and the Success Criterion on Layer-2 'coaching findings' — these are intentionally complementary (spec vs. behavior) rather than true duplicates.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one clause stating 'certification == the signed attestation' (or pick one term), e.g. in the Attestation section: 'the signed attestation (a.k.a. certification) is the composable CI rigor signal.' Optional: cross-reference the Layer-2 coaching AC and the feedback-authoring AC so it is clear one defines the format and the other the runtime behavior.

findings.location: ## Context (first paragraph) and 'a rebar FEATURE used by a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE'

Design summary: 'Criteria registry ... a generic orchestrator runs them (reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)'; Attestation AC: 'extends the close-gate signing'; Polyglot tooling: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'
findings.finding: The plan is mostly disciplined about reuse rather than reinvention: it explicitly extends the existing close-gate signing, reuses rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, and reuses solidlsp/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard already in repo. So the dominant NIH risk is low and self-mitigated. The residual concern is configuration-surface proliferation: the plan introduces several project knobs simultaneously (per-criterion advisory|blocking-with-override, chunking granularity, per-plan budget cap, risk posture, INDETERMINATE blocking behavior) plus a large criteria registry (Layer-1 ~7 checks, Layer-2 ~12 criteria + overlays + broad pass). Whether this is gratuitous vs. justified depends on the live config system and whether these knobs collapse into a smaller set; that needs codebase access to confirm. The 'generic orchestrator' abstraction is justified by many criteria (well past Rule-of-Three), so it is not gratuitous single-call-site abstraction.
findings.suggested_edit: Consider stating in the plan which knobs ship with a single sensible default vs. which are genuinely per-project, and whether the per-criterion blocking knob and the global risk-posture knob can be unified to reduce config surface. Confirm against the existing rebar config module that no equivalent knobs already exist before adding new ones.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list and Success Criteria — e.g. AC 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python...', AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented...', Success Criterion 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: The plan specifies WHAT must be built in detail but does not describe HOW any of it will be tested. There is no testing-plan: no behavioral/contractual test coverage named for the gate, no failure-path/boundary cases enumerated (e.g. missing signature, stale/material-edit-invalidated signature, --force override path, INDETERMINATE budget-exhaustion outcome, fail-open on unsupported language / no LSP server / tool crash / timeout — all of which are core correctness claims), and no RED-before-GREEN ordering. The fail-open 'IRONCLAD' claim and the invalidation-on-material-edit binding are exactly the kinds of behaviors that need explicit boundary tests, yet none are described. The 'measurably help an agent revise' success criterion implies an evaluation method that is also unspecified. Note the empirical-defaults experiments are referenced for tuning, but tuning experiments are not a substitute for behavioral/contract tests of the gate logic.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing-plan section / AC enumerating: (1) claim-gate tests for absent / stale / valid / material-edit-invalidated signatures and the recorded --force override; (2) fail-open tests for unsupported-language, no-server, crash, and timeout each asserting skipped=PASS; (3) material-edit-invalidation tests distinguishing material (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition) vs non-material (tags/comments/links/assignee) events; (4) INDETERMINATE budget-cap + chunk-atomic checkpoint resume; (5) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar reducer-ignored behavior; (6) the external CI checkability of the signature. State RED-before-GREEN where applicable. Structural greps / command-output assertions are acceptable for the polyglot/cross-language paths.

findings.location: Design summary → 'Criteria registry' + 'Polyglot tooling'; AC 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points.' and 'Attestation: ... (extends the close-gate signing).'
findings.finding: The plan repeatedly commits to reuse rather than reinvention — it explicitly extends the existing completion-verifier close gate's signing, reuses rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, and uses already-in-repo tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard). This is the opposite of NIH on its face. However, two areas warrant a codebase check: (1) the 'criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator' — confirm rebar.llm doesn't already provide a criterion catalog/orchestrator that this would duplicate, and that the registry is genuinely reused vs. a parallel new layer; (2) configuration surface is broad (per-criterion advisory/blocking, chunking, budget cap, risk posture, INDETERMINATE-blocking) which risks config proliferation. The plan mitigates this with shipped sensible defaults and by deferring the config UX (F5) and several knobs (F2–F4) to follow-ons, which is a benign reading. I cannot confirm the registry/orchestrator isn't a redundant new abstraction without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add one sentence to the 'Criteria registry' AC clarifying whether rebar.llm already exposes an orchestrator/criterion-catalog to extend (reuse) vs. requiring a new generic orchestrator, so the agent doesn't build a parallel layer.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (all 12 items) and Success Criteria; e.g. 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open' and 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'.
findings.finding: The plan does not describe how the change itself will be tested. There is no acceptance criterion or section specifying behavioral/contractual tests for the gate's own logic: e.g. claim is blocked when no/stale attestation exists and allowed with --force; fail-open paths (unsupported language, missing LSP server, tool crash, timeout) actually yield skipped=PASS not FAIL; material-edit invalidation fires on description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits but NOT on tags/comments/links/assignee; the reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT; INDETERMINATE non-blocking default; per-criterion blocking-with-override. Several criteria assert subjective outcomes ('measurably help an agent revise the plan', 'sufficient to reconstruct per-criterion FP/remediation analysis offline') with no test or validation method named. The experiment-grounded defaults AC references linked experiment work but no test confirms the shipped defaults match it. Fail-open is called 'IRONCLAD' yet has no failure-path test coverage specified — this is the highest-risk untested area for a broad client base.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC for a test suite covering: (a) claim gate happy/blocked/--force paths; (b) explicit fail-open cases per tool (unsupported lang, no server, crash, timeout) asserting skipped=PASS; (c) material vs non-material edit invalidation matrix; (d) reducer-ignores-REVIEW_RESULT and head-state signature event; (e) per-criterion advisory/blocking-override behavior. Prefer behavioral/contract tests over change-detectors, and assert failure-path/boundary cases (RED-before-GREEN for the gate logic). Structural greps over emitted events/certs are an acceptable cross-language pattern here.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria ... measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria) and AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'.
findings.finding: Two criteria are not in-session measurable/verifiable as written. (1) The Success Criterion 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' encodes an empirical efficacy claim that cannot be verified within a single implementation session — it requires labeled trajectories or an A/B over agent runs, with no stated measurement procedure or threshold. (2) 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' makes acceptance depend on a SEPARATE experiment ticket completing; if that work is out-of-session, this criterion cannot be satisfied here. Also several criteria lean on terms whose pass/fail bar is unstated (e.g. 'sensible defaults shipped', 'anti-slop', 'AC/DD quality').
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the efficacy criterion to an in-session-verifiable proxy, e.g. 'Layer-2 emits findings conforming to the coaching schema (grounded location + criterion ref + severity + checklist item), validated on a fixture set of N seeded-flaw plans where each seeded flaw produces a matching finding.' For the defaults criterion, either scope the experiment into this epic or restate as 'defaults are config-overridable and documented; calibration to experiment results is tracked in linked ticket <id>' so claiming this epic does not block on external work.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria lists vs. 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'.
findings.finding: Verification/termination is largely well-specified: claim is a 'FAST check for a valid signed attestation', the review is 'a REVIEW PROCESS ... revises and re-runs to earn a fresh signature' (clear stop condition), criteria are 'Frozen within a review loop (convergence)' giving termination, and INDETERMINATE/budget-cap provide bounded end-states — so most steps do reach the stated end-state. The residual gap: the epic's end-state depends on experiment-derived defaults and an external CI-check pattern (AC 'checkable by an external CI process'), but the plan does not state who owns the CI integration or whether a runnable CI check exists within this epic vs. follow-on F11 ('rigor-signature chain / CI integration helpers'). This creates a potential what-we'll-do vs what-done-looks-like gap between 'cert is checkable by CI' (AC) and the helper being deferred to F11. Resolving requires knowing the boundary between this epic and F11, which needs the linked design/session log not available here.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the AC whether 'checkable by an external CI process' means shipping a documented verification command/pattern in this epic (and add an AC like 'a documented `rebar verify-cert` command exits non-zero on missing/stale attestation') versus relying on F11; explicitly note the seam so the agent knows the in-epic end-state.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' and Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two acceptance/success items are not completable or measurable within one working session. (1) 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' relies on a downstream agent-revision outcome that has no in-session measurable threshold and reads like 'works well' — there is no defined metric, dataset, or pass bar. (2) 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' makes acceptance depend on a SEPARATE linked experiment ticket completing, which is out-of-session for this epic. Also note the AC explicitly references offline analysis (REVIEW_RESULT replay, FP/FN rates), which is correctly scoped as 'capture only / analysis offline' but the success criterion wording ('measurably help') still implies an in-session measurement that cannot be verified here.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the Layer-2 success criterion to an in-session-checkable form, e.g. 'Layer-2 emits findings that conform to the coaching spec (each grounded in a criterion/file/section, specific, severity-ranked+capped, checklist-form, affirms passing items)' — which is structurally verifiable. Move the 'measurably helps revision' efficacy claim to follow-on/offline (F1). For the defaults AC, either ship hardcoded provisional defaults verifiable now and mark experiment-tuning as follow-on, or explicitly state acceptance = 'defaults are wired and configurable' rather than 'grounded in experiment results'.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list vs Success Criteria; '`claim` integration: fast signed-attestation check' and 'the heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND'
findings.finding: Termination/verification is mostly well-specified: per-criterion PASS/AMBIGUOUS/FAIL/INDETERMINATE verdict model, signature freshness bound to material-event commit, and a fast claim-time check give clear 'done' and 'when to stop' semantics. However the end-state has a reachability gap: the plan says the heavy Layer-2 review runs OUT-OF-BAND and the client 'revises and re-runs to earn a fresh signature', but no acceptance criterion describes the mechanism that PRODUCES the signed attestation in the first place (the command/entrypoint that runs the review and emits the signature out-of-band). The ACs cover the claim-time consumer check, signing primitives, and REVIEW_RESULT capture, but the producing 'run the review' step that ties Layer-1+Layer-2 into an emitted attestation is implied rather than stated, so the union of described steps may not visibly reach the 'a valid signed attestation exists to be checked' end-state. This needs confirmation against the orchestrator/claim design rather than assertion of a defect.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC for the out-of-band review entrypoint, e.g. 'A `review`/verify command runs Layer-1+Layer-2 over a ticket plan and, on no-block, emits the signed attestation + REVIEW_RESULT sidecar', so the producer→consumer (claim) path is end-to-end and the stated end-state is reachable from the listed steps.

findings.location: Design summary / Polyglot tooling: "solidlsp (already in repo)"; "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner"; Acceptance Criteria: "extends the close-gate signing", "reusing rebar.llm extension points".
findings.finding: The plan rests on several claims about existing rebar infrastructure that an executor would need to verify against the actual codebase before relying on them: (1) "solidlsp (already in repo)"; (2) the completion-verifier / "close-gate signing" exists and can be extended for attestation signing + material-event-commit binding; (3) rebar.llm exposes a reusable "catalog/contracts/prompt/runner" with usable extension points; (4) the reducer can be made to ignore a sidecar event type ("reducer skips the type"). These are plausible given the epic context but are unverified assertions about the codebase. I lack codebase access and cannot confirm any of them, so I name them as assumptions rather than asserting they are wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short "Assumptions to verify before execution" note listing: solidlsp presence/version, the exact close-gate signing module to extend, the concrete rebar.llm extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner) and their interfaces, and the reducer mechanism for ignoring a new event type. Have the executor confirm each against the repo before building on it.

findings.location: Execution section: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments"; Acceptance Criteria: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)"; and "escalation to a stronger model by size + complexity + centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)."
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred to the executor or to unresolved external work, so an agent could not execute without stopping to clarify. (1) Chunking/budget/depth defaults are explicitly deferred to "experiment results (see linked experiment work)" — the AC depends on results that may not exist yet, leaving the executor unable to pick concrete defaults. (2) Routing thresholds for "complexity", "size", "centrality" and the escalation tiers are named but never given concrete cutoffs or a computation method ("centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time" specifies neither algorithm nor thresholds). (3) The Layer-1/Layer-2 split lists many criteria but does not define pass/fail thresholds for items like "oversize signals", "AC/DD quality", or "anti-slop", deferring those judgments to the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) make the experiment-derived defaults a hard prerequisite by marking the experiment ticket as a blocking dependency and stating placeholder defaults to use until results land, or (b) specify concrete initial thresholds/cutoffs for complexity/size/centrality routing and for the structural Layer-1 checks. Define how centrality/blast-radius is computed (which graph, which metric) rather than leaving it as a label.

findings.location: Title declares this an 'epic'; Context: 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate'. Acceptance Criteria list (11 distinct AC bullets) + Success Criteria (6) — but no decomposition into child tickets is present in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly self-identifies as an EPIC ('This epic builds...', 'NOT part of this epic') yet contains no child decomposition. The Acceptance Criteria enumerate at least 11 large, independently-substantial workstreams — Layer-1 deterministic criteria (7+ distinct checks each needing different tooling: LSP/ast-grep, syft+registry, scc/lizard), Layer-2 advisory criteria (~12 criteria + overlays + broad pass), a criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing+binding+invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budgeting, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar+reducer changes, per-criterion config, docs. This is far too big for one unit of work and, by the plan's own framing, must be split into children. The 'Follow-on work' section (F1–F12) defers adjacent scope but does NOT decompose the epic's own in-scope ACs into executable child tickets. For an epic, the criterion requires decomposition into children to be present and sensible; it is absent.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit child-ticket decomposition for the in-epic scope, e.g. split along vertical slices that each deliver a claimable signal: (C1) attestation signing + material-event binding + invalidation (extends close-gate); (C2) claim fast-check + --force override; (C3) criteria registry + generic orchestrator; (C4) Layer-1 deterministic floor + fail-open tooling; (C5) Layer-2 advisory criteria + coaching authoring; (C6) routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing + INDETERMINATE; (C7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer-skip + offline-ready; (C8) config surface + per-criterion blocking + defaults from experiments; (C9) docs. Confirm children collectively cover all 11 ACs (no parent AC left uncovered).

findings.location: Design summary — 'Polyglot tooling' bullet: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback'; Attestation AC: 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase claims an executor would need to verify before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' and 'ast-grep' availability/integration; (2) that rebar.llm exposes reusable 'catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' extension points; (3) that a 'completion-verifier close gate' signing mechanism exists and can be EXTENDED ('extends the close-gate signing'); (4) that a centrality/blast-radius computation is available 'at plan time'; (5) that the reducer can be made to IGNORE a new REVIEW_RESULT event type. None of these can be confirmed from plan text alone; they require the live rebar codebase I do not have access to.
findings.suggested_edit: List these as explicit pre-implementation verification points (e.g., 'Confirm solidlsp is wired and callable; confirm rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner are the intended reuse seams; confirm close-gate signing module is extensible; confirm reducer supports ignored sidecar event types') so the executor validates them against the code before building on them.

findings.location: Execution bullet & AC: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Layer-2 AC: 'a bounded broad pass' / 'severity-ranked + capped'; routing 'pivots on has_children + complexity'.
findings.finding: Multiple real design decisions are deferred to the executor or to unspecified external work. (1) Chunking, budget, and depth defaults are 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' / 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — but the experiment is not specified here and no fallback default is given, so an executor cannot proceed without it. (2) 'complexity' is the routing pivot yet is never defined (what computes it, thresholds). (3) 'bounded broad pass' and severity 'cap' give no concrete bound/number. (4) The full per-criterion BLOCKING-vs-advisory default for each Layer-1 vs Layer-2 criterion is described as a posture but not enumerated. An executing agent would need to stop and ask how to define complexity, what the caps/bounds are, and whether to block on the experiment.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline interim defaults (e.g., a named complexity metric + threshold, a numeric finding cap, a default chunk size) that experiments later tune, or mark the linked experiment as a hard dependency/blocker for the defaults AC and state the placeholder defaults to use until then. Define 'complexity' concretely.

findings.location: Top-level item is titled an 'epic' ('This epic builds a plan-review verification gate...') with 13 Acceptance Criteria spanning Layer-1 (8 distinct checks), Layer-2 (~12 advisory criteria + overlays + broad pass), a criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer changes, per-criterion config, docs, and experiment-grounded defaults.
findings.finding: The plan repeatedly self-identifies as an 'epic' but contains no decomposition into children, while clearly bundling many independently-shippable units of work (Layer-1 deterministic floor, Layer-2 advisory reviewer, registry/orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim gate, routing/budget engine, REVIEW_RESULT observability, docs). This is far too large to one-shot in a single session and an epic without children cannot be coherently executed. There is also a horizontal-layer-seam risk: 'Layer-1' and 'Layer-2' are described as separate layers, and slicing the work by layer (build all of L1, then all of L2) rather than by a thin vertical slice (one end-to-end criterion through claim->review->attestation->sidecar) risks a non-shippable intermediate state.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose this epic into child tickets before execution — e.g. (1) attestation signing + claim fast-check gate + material-event binding, (2) criteria registry + generic orchestrator, (3) Layer-1 deterministic floor (possibly per tool: solidlsp/ast-grep, syft/registry, scc/lizard), (4) Layer-2 advisory reviewer + coaching-feedback spec, (5) routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, (6) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer-ignore, (7) docs. Prefer a thin vertical slice (one criterion end-to-end) as the first child to de-risk the seam, then fan out. State which children are in-scope for this epic.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)" vs Design summary: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments"
findings.finding: The plan makes shipped defaults a prerequisite ("grounded in the experiment results") but neither the steps nor the success criteria define a termination/verification path for the experiments themselves: there is no stated way to know when the experiment work is 'done enough' to fix defaults, and the experiment work is referenced as external ("see linked experiment work") without being listed as a dependency or sequencing precondition. This leaves a gap between 'what we'll do' (run experiments to set defaults) and a verifiable end-state — the epic could be declared complete with placeholder defaults and no check that they were empirically grounded.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit dependency/sequencing note stating the experiment ticket must complete before defaults are frozen, and add a verifiable check to the AC (e.g. 'default chunking/budget/depth values are traceable to a named experiment result artifact') so the end-state is checkable rather than aspirational.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding: Several acceptance criteria are stated as end-states without a described verification method or termination signal, leaving a gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like'. Most concretely: the AC 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria) and 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results' assert measurable/empirical outcomes, but the plan defers the actual measurement and FP/remediation analysis to follow-on F1 ('FP/remediation-rate tracking tooling (offline analysis)') and to 'linked experiment work'. As written, there is no in-epic way to verify the 'measurably help' claim or to confirm the experiment-grounded defaults are reached before the epic is considered done — the union of described steps (capture-only sidecar, no in-epic analysis) does not reach those stated end-states.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the affected ACs to in-epic-verifiable end-states, e.g. 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar captures the fields required for the offline FP/remediation analysis (verified by a schema/replay test)' rather than 'measurably help', and explicitly scope the 'experiment-grounded defaults' AC as either (a) shipping placeholder defaults with a TODO to retune, or (b) a hard dependency on the linked experiment ticket that must close first. State the verification step (test, replay fixture, or manual check) for each AC.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)"; Design summary: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments"
findings.finding: Multiple concrete design decisions are deferred to unscoped/external experiments without specifying a fallback an executor can ship.
