The plan repeatedly says defaults are set 'EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and points to '(see linked experiment work)', but no experiment ticket is named, no provisional default is given, and there is no instruction for what to do if the experiment results are unavailable. An agent executing this epic would have to stop and ask which defaults to ship, or invent them. Additionally 'chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB ... default set EMPIRICALLY' leaves the actual shipped default value undefined.
findings.suggested_edit: Either name the specific linked experiment ticket as a hard dependency and state that this epic is blocked until it lands, or specify provisional defaults (e.g. an initial chunk size, budget cap, and depth-by-complexity table) that ship now and are tuned later. State the fallback if experiment data is absent.

findings.location: Title labels this an 'epic'; Context: "This epic builds a plan-review verification gate"; the entire Acceptance Criteria list (12 ACs spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, registry/orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/escalation/budgeting, sidecar events, config, docs)
findings.finding: The plan self-identifies as an EPIC but contains NO decomposition into child tickets. The scope is enormous: a polyglot deterministic Layer-1 with ~7 distinct check families and multiple external tools (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard), a Layer-2 with ~12 advisory criteria plus overlays and a broad pass, a declarative criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing/binding/invalidation, claim-gate integration, complexity/centrality routing and escalation, budget caps with chunk-atomic checkpointing, a sidecar event with reducer changes and offline-analysis substrate, per-criterion config, and docs. This is far too large to execute as one unit and must be split into vertical child slices. As an epic/parent, decomposition is required and is absent.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into linked child tickets, e.g.: (1) attestation signing + material-event binding + invalidation; (2) claim fast-check + --force override; (3) Layer-1 floor incl. polyglot tooling+fail-open; (4) criteria registry + orchestrator; (5) Layer-2 advisory criteria; (6) routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing; (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer skip; (8) per-criterion config; (9) docs. Prefer vertical slices (smallest claim->Layer-1->attestation end-to-end path first), and reference the experiment ticket as a dependency.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Multiple concrete design values are explicitly deferred to unspecified empirical experiments that are not part of this epic. The default chunking size, budget cap, and depth posture are all said to be 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' / 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)', but no experiment is defined, linked, or scoped here. An executing agent cannot ship 'sensible defaults' (required by Success Criteria) without either the experiment results or a stop-to-ask. This is decision-deferral pushing a real design decision onto the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline provisional placeholder defaults (e.g. 'ship chunk=N, budget=$X, depth=Y as initial defaults pending experiment refinement') and mark them explicitly as revisable, or link the specific experiment ticket and make this epic depend on it. State what the agent should do if experiment results are unavailable at execution time.

findings.location: Title/Context label this as an 'epic' and 'This epic builds...', but the Acceptance Criteria list 13 distinct deliverables (Layer-1 criteria suite, Layer-2 criteria suite, registry+orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, routing/budget, feedback spec, sidecar event, per-criterion config, bug exemption, defaults, docs)
findings.finding: The plan self-identifies as an epic but provides NO decomposition into child tickets for the in-scope work. The work spans many independent, individually-large subsystems (deterministic Layer-1 polyglot tooling, the LLM Layer-2 advisory suite, signed attestation, claim integration, observability sidecar, config surface, docs) that cannot be executed coherently in one session and have clear seams. findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Attestation: ... (extends the close-gate signing)'; 'claim integration'; 'Criteria registry ... reusing rebar.llm extension points'; Polyglot tooling: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'.

The claim path and the reducer are shared hot paths whose consumers (any code reading status transitions or replaying events) may need updating; these consumers are not enumerated. Whether the named targets exist and what callers depend on them can only be confirmed against the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: As a suggestion: add a 'File/symbol impact' note enumerating the concrete modules to be edited (claim command, the close-gate signing module being extended, the event reducer) and explicitly list the consumers of the claim transition and event reducer that the change must preserve or update. findings.location: Design summary / Polyglot tooling: 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'ast-grep (resolution/callers)', 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; Attestation: 'extends the close-gate signing'; Observability: 'the reducer skips the type'.

findings.location: ## Context and Design summary
Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; AC: 'Configurable chunking ... per-criterion blocking via config'; '_a reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR REVIEW_RESULT event_'
findings.finding: The plan is largely NIH-aware (it explicitly reuses rebar.llm extension points, the close-gate signing infrastructure, solidlsp/ast-grep already in repo, and reuses the completion-verifier pattern), which is good. The residual A1 risk is configuration-surface proliferation: the plan introduces a sizeable config matrix (per-criterion advisory|blocking-with-override, chunking knob, per-plan budget cap, risk posture, INDETERMINATE non-blocking default, escalation thresholds). For a single epic this is a broad config surface and several knobs (e.g. promote-to-blocking, FP-ceiling auto-demote) are already correctly deferred to follow-ons F2/F4. Whether the in-epic config surface is genuinely warranted by current consumers vs. speculative flexibility cannot be confirmed from plan text alone — it depends on the live rebar config conventions and whether these knobs have real near-term callers. Recommend validating that each shipped knob has a concrete default + a near-term need, otherwise narrow the initial surface.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a note justifying each in-epic config knob with a concrete near-term consumer/default, and consider deferring any knob without an immediate caller to the follow-on list (alongside F2/F4/F5) to keep the initial config surface minimal.

findings.location: ## Context paragraph and Design summary
Design summary: "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner"; "extends the close-gate signing"; and the per-criterion config knob
findings.finding: The plan broadly demonstrates anti-NIH discipline: it explicitly reuses rebar.llm extension points, extends the existing close-gate signing rather than building new signing, reuses solidlsp/ast-grep already in the repo, and mirrors the completion-verifier pattern. These are the right reuse moves. Two areas warrant a benign flag rather than a FAIL: (1) configuration surface is broad (risk posture, chunking, budget caps, per-criterion advisory|blocking, INDETERMINATE policy) — justified by the stated diverse client base, but worth confirming that defaults ship so most projects touch zero knobs (the plan does say "sensible defaults shipped", which mitigates this). (2) Whether the 'generic orchestrator' and 'criteria registry' duplicate existing rebar.llm catalog/runner machinery vs. genuinely extending it cannot be confirmed without the live codebase. No clear over-engineering or NIH defect is evident from plan text; confirmation of the orchestrator/registry boundary requires codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally add one sentence clarifying that the 'generic orchestrator + criteria registry' is a thin descriptor layer over the existing rebar.llm catalog/runner (not a parallel framework), and confirm the shipped-defaults path requires no config for a typical project.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list and 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results' AC; no dedicated testing/verification AC for the gate's own implementation
findings.finding: The plan describes the FEATURE'S verification behavior extensively (Layer-1 checks, fail-open, attestation invalidation, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar) but never states how the implementation of the gate itself will be tested. There is no acceptance criterion requiring tests for the new code paths: e.g. fail-open behavior on an unsupported language/missing LSP server/timeout (the 'IRONCLAD' guarantee), material-edit invalidation vs. non-material edits (tags/comments/links/assignee must NOT invalidate), the `claim` block-on-missing/stale-signature path plus `--force` override recording, and the reducer correctly IGNORING the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar type. These are the highest-risk, contract-defining behaviors of the epic and are asserted as guarantees yet have no testing plan, no failure-path/boundary coverage, and no RED-before-GREEN ordering. Without an explicit testing AC the agent may ship the gate with the safety-critical fail-open and invalidation logic unverified.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an acceptance criterion such as: 'Automated tests cover: (a) fail-open on unsupported lang / missing-server / crash / timeout -> skipped=PASS for each Layer-1 tool; (b) material-edit invalidates signature while tag/comment/link/assignee edits do not; (c) claim blocks on absent/stale attestation and records --force+justification override; (d) reducer ignores the REVIEW_RESULT event type. Include failure-path and boundary cases.' Structural greps / command-output assertions are acceptable for polyglot fixtures.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' vs Success Criteria / Design summary 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'

One coherence gap worth confirming: the AC 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on an external 'linked experiment work' ticket that is not described in the plan body; the body says defaults are set empirically 'via experiments' but does not identify those experiments as a separate dependency. This is plausibly intentional (the experiment is a separate linked ticket), so it is not clearly an orphan, but the dependency should be made explicit to avoid the epic asserting a criterion whose precondition lives entirely outside it.
findings.suggested_edit: In the description, add a one-line note under Design summary or a 'Dependencies' subsection naming the linked experiment ticket that produces the empirical defaults, so the 'grounded in the experiment results' AC has an in-plan referent. (Suggestion — verify the linked ticket exists.)

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' and Acceptance Criteria: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented: measurability/agent-completable ... a bounded broad pass.'
findings.finding: Two F1 problems. (1) The success criterion 'findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' is not in-session measurable as stated: 'measurably help' implies an evaluation/experiment over agent trajectories (with a metric and threshold) that is not defined here and likely cannot be validated within a single implementation session. (2) Several ACs are stated as 'implemented' lists rather than verifiable outcomes — e.g. the AC enumerating ~12 Layer-2 criteria and 'a bounded broad pass' has no per-item acceptance test, so 'done' is undefined for each criterion. The 'grounded in the experiment results' AC also depends on out-of-session experiment completion. Without measurable pass conditions an implementing agent cannot self-verify these criteria in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' criterion to an in-session-checkable form (e.g. 'each advisory finding includes criterion-id, location, severity, and a checklist item; verified on a fixture set of N seeded-defect plans where the gate surfaces the expected finding') and split the experiment-dependent defaults into a clearly-marked dependency rather than an AC of this epic. findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list vs. Design summary; AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Two acceptance items are not verifiable within a single working session as written. (1) 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' specifies an outcome (measured improvement in agent revision) with no concrete in-session metric, threshold, or fixture — 'measurably help' is unmeasurable without a defined benchmark and would require a multi-trajectory evaluation that cannot be confirmed in one session. By contrast, most other ACs are concretely checkable (claim blocks without signature, reducer skips REVIEW_RESULT type, fail-open skipped=pass, per-criterion config), so they pass.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (Layer-1, Layer-2, registry/orchestrator, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar) and 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results' AC.
findings.finding: The plan builds a non-trivial verification gate (Layer-1 deterministic checks, Layer-2 advisory reviewer, claim gating with --force, signed attestation invalidation, a reducer-ignored sidecar event) but the Acceptance Criteria describe only WHAT to build, not HOW any of it will be tested. There is no behavioral/contractual coverage called out: e.g. no test that a missing/stale signature actually blocks claim while --force overrides it, no fail-open test (unsupported language/tool => skipped=pass, never FAIL), no test that material edits invalidate the attestation while tags/comments/links/assignee do NOT, no test that the reducer ignores the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar type, and no failure-path/boundary cases (budget cap exceeded => INDETERMINATE non-blocking; tool crash/timeout). The fail-open behavior is described as 'IRONCLAD' yet no AC requires tests asserting it. Without explicit test ACs the agent may ship the feature with no verification of these safety-critical invariants, causing rework.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit testing acceptance criteria, e.g.: 'Tests cover (a) claim blocks on absent/stale/material-edit-invalidated signature and succeeds with recorded --force; (b) fail-open paths: unsupported language, no LSP server, tool crash, and timeout each yield skipped=pass and never FAIL; (c) material vs non-material edit invalidation matrix (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition invalidate; tags/comments/links/assignee do not); (d) reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT sidecar; (e) budget-cap-exceeded yields INDETERMINATE non-blocking by default. Specify RED-before-GREEN where feasible.' Structural greps / command-output assertions for the polyglot tooling are acceptable here.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria
Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)' vs Context/Design summary/AC

Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; and the broad Layer-1/Layer-2 criteria lists
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred onto the executor or to unresolved external work. (1) Default chunking/budget/depth values are 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' / 'see linked experiment work' — the experiment work is referenced but the values do not exist yet, so an agent cannot implement the defaults without that input, a clarification/blocking dependency. findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (12 AC bullets) vs. Success Criteria; e.g. 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'
findings.finding: The plan describes a rich union of mechanisms (Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim integration, sidecar) and each maps to a Success Criterion, so the end-state is largely reachable from the described steps. However, there is no explicit verification step stating HOW each AC will be demonstrated 'done' (e.g., tests, a demo command, a CI check run) and no termination/stopping criterion for the review loop itself beyond 'per-plan budget cap' and 'INDETERMINATE'. The plan asserts convergence ('frozen within a review loop (convergence)', 'convergent by construction') but does not state how an executing agent verifies that convergence/termination actually holds. As an epic-level plan this may be intentionally deferred to child tickets — needs confirmation of whether per-AC verification is expected here or in decomposition.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Verification / Done-definition' line per major AC (or one section) naming the artifact that proves it — e.g., 'claim gate: integration test asserting open->in_progress blocks without a valid signature and succeeds with --force', and state the review-loop termination condition explicitly (frozen criteria + budget cap => INDETERMINATE).

findings.location: ## Context paragraph ('when an agent claims a ticket ... nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound') and Design summary ('used by a BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE')

Success Criterion 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' and AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented ... measurability/agent-completable' and AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Several criteria assert measurable outcomes without a defined, in-session measure. 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no metric or threshold and likely requires offline replay/empirical comparison (the plan itself routes FP/remediation analysis to OFFLINE work and to follow-on F1), so it is not verifiable within the implementation session. Similarly 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on out-of-session experiment work being complete, making that AC unverifiable on its own. These read as unmeasurable/out-of-session as written.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword to in-session-testable form: replace 'measurably help' with a checkable behavioral assertion (e.g., 'each Layer-2 finding includes criterion-id + location + suggested-fix fields, asserted by unit tests'), and make the defaults AC conditional/explicit about its dependency (e.g., 'defaults wired to config keys; values populated from experiment ticket X, which must be closed first').

findings.location: Entire Acceptance Criteria list — no testing AC; closest is 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results' and 'Docs'
findings.finding: The plan does not describe how the change itself will be tested. There is no AC for unit/integration tests of the Layer-1 deterministic checks (which are described as 'deterministic' and thus highly testable), the fail-open behavior ('skipped = PASS' on unsupported lang/crash/timeout — a key boundary/failure path), the claim gate block/override behavior, attestation invalidation-on-material-edit, or reducer-ignores-REVIEW_RESULT. Given the plan emphasizes fail-open as 'IRONCLAD', the absence of any test/RED-before-GREEN or failure-path coverage requirement is a notable gap likely to cause rework.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing AC, e.g.: 'Tests: deterministic Layer-1 checks have unit tests including fail-open paths (unsupported lang, no LSP server, tool crash, timeout => PASS); claim gate has integration tests for block/valid/--force/stale-signature; attestation invalidation on material vs non-material edits is tested; reducer-ignores-REVIEW_RESULT is asserted.' Structural/command-output assertions are acceptable for polyglot cases.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (e.g. "`claim` integration: fast signed-attestation check", "Attestation: ... (extends the close-gate signing)", "REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event ... reducer skips the type")
findings.finding: The plan names broad subsystems to touch (claim path, signing/attestation that 'extends the close-gate signing', the event reducer, rebar.llm extension points) but never names concrete files/modules/symbols. The blast radius is real: a new event type ('REVIEW_RESULT') plus a new reducer-skip rule plus a state-transition gate at open->in_progress touches the event reducer, the claim command, and the CI/cert verification path — all consumers that 'a change of this kind would require updating' are implied but not enumerated. Edit targets are therefore vague rather than plausible-named, and consumer updates (e.g., the close-gate signer being extended, any CI consumer of the cert) are not concretely identified.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'File/symbol impact' subsection enumerating the concrete modules to modify (the claim command handler, the event reducer + its known-type set, the close-gate signing module being extended, the cert verification entry point used by CI) and the new module(s) for the criteria registry/orchestrator. Mark each as new vs. modified.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)' vs. the breadth of the Acceptance Criteria (Layer-1 + Layer-2 + registry + orchestrator + attestation + claim integration + routing/escalation + budgeting + sidecar event + config + docs)
findings.finding: The title frames a single 'verification gate', but the body is effectively an entire epic spanning ~12 acceptance criteria including a full criteria registry, generic orchestrator, polyglot tooling integrations, routing/escalation/budget machinery, and observability sidecar. This is plausibly intentional (the description repeatedly calls it an 'epic'), so it is not a contradiction — but whether this is meant to be executed as one ticket or decomposed into children cannot be decided from plan text and bears on intent-vs-scope fidelity. Confirming requires knowing whether children exist.

findings.location: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments" / AC: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)"; also "chunking of criteria-per-LLM-call is a PROJECT KNOB ... default set EMPIRICALLY"
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred to out-of-band 'experiment work' that an executing agent cannot resolve without stopping: the default chunking size, budget cap, and depth values are explicitly 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work),' yet no concrete defaults or the experiment's outputs are provided in-plan. An agent reaching these ACs must either pause for the experiment results or invent values — i.e., a deferred design decision pushed onto the executor. Similarly 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' names a mechanism without specifying the algorithm.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline placeholder safe defaults (with a note 'tune from experiment ticket X') so execution can proceed unblocked, or make the experiment a hard dependency/blocking link and state that this ticket's defaults AC is gated on it. Specify (or link a precise spec for) how centrality/blast-radius is computed.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (12+ checkboxes) and the framing 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate' with 'Follow-on work F1..F12'.
findings.finding: This is explicitly an EPIC ('This epic builds...') but no decomposition into child tickets is present. The AC list bundles Layer-1 polyglot tooling (LSP/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard), ~12 Layer-2 judgment criteria, a criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing/invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget machinery, the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer change, per-criterion config, and docs — each of which is a multi-session unit. As written this parent cannot be executed coherently in one session and there is no children-coverage map. The plan itself states routing pivots on 'has_children' yet declares no children.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit decomposition section listing the intended child tickets (e.g. C1 criteria registry+orchestrator, C2 Layer-1 floor, C3 each tooling integration, C4 attestation/signing, C5 claim integration, C6 routing/budget, C7 REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, C8 config surface, C9 docs) with a coverage statement that the children fully cover the parent AC, OR re-scope this ticket to a single child and link siblings.

findings.location: AC 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check', AC 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event ... reducer skips the type', AC 'Attestation ... (extends the close-gate signing)', and 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'.
findings.finding: The plan names systems to touch (claim command, the reducer, close-gate signing, rebar.llm extension points) and acknowledges blast radius for the reducer ('out of hot paths', 'reducer skips the type'). However NO concrete files/symbols are named — it references the `claim` path, the reducer, and the signing module only by role. The consumers that a new STATUS-gating check and a new event type would require updating (the claim code path, the event reducer's type dispatch, the attestation/signing module, head-state event handling, and any CI consumer of the cert) are gestured at but not enumerated as concrete edit targets. Confirming the named targets are plausible and complete requires the live rebar codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: List the concrete files/modules each AC will touch (claim command handler, reducer type-dispatch, completion-verifier signing module being extended, event-store schema) and explicitly call out the consumers that must be updated when adding the REVIEW_RESULT type and the open->in_progress gate.

findings.location: AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and design summary 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' vs. Context 'This description is the self-contained epic summary.'
findings.finding: A coherence gap exists between the self-contained-epic claim and the dependency on external experiment results. Multiple AC ('Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results') and routing/budget defaults are declared as in-scope deliverables of this epic, yet they depend on 'linked experiment work' whose existence/completion is not asserted. If the experiments are a prerequisite not yet done, shipping 'sensible defaults' (Success Criterion 4) and the experiment-grounded defaults AC cannot both be satisfied within this epic. This is a sequencing/dependency contradiction between sections.
findings.suggested_edit: State the dependency explicitly: either mark the experiment-grounded-defaults AC as blocked-on a linked prerequisite ticket, or specify provisional defaults to ship now with experiment-grounding deferred to a follow-on, and reconcile with Success Criterion 4's 'sensible defaults shipped'.

findings.location: Design summary 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC 'Configurable chunking; ... per-plan budget cap'; AC 'oversize signals', 'AC/DD quality', 'destructive sniff'; reference 'Full design detail lives in session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b'.
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred onto the executor or live in an external session log rather than the plan. Concrete thresholds are unstated: what 'oversize signals' threshold (scc/lizard) triggers a flag, what 'destructive sniff' patterns count, what the 'per-plan budget cap' value is, and the chunking default ('default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments') — all are 'decide/measure later' placeholders. Crucially the self-contained-summary defers full design to session log 63cc-ec94-4792-464b, so an executing agent would need to retrieve that log to act, i.e. it cannot proceed without clarification on the unspecified numerics and the ~12 Layer-2 criteria definitions.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline the concrete default thresholds/values (oversize cutoffs, budget cap, default chunk size, destructive-pattern list) or explicitly scope them to a prerequisite experiment ticket and mark this epic's executor as not needing them; do not leave 'set empirically' / 'see session log' as the only specification an executor has.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)' is repeatedly called 'This epic' / 'epic summary' (Context: 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate'; Design summary; Follow-on 'NOT part of this epic'), yet the Acceptance Criteria list ~13 large implementation deliverables with no decomposition into children.
findings.finding: This is self-described as an EPIC but the plan presents a flat list of ~13 heavyweight acceptance criteria (Layer-1 polyglot deterministic suite, Layer-2 advisory suite of ~12 judgment criteria, criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer changes, per-criterion config, docs) with no child-ticket decomposition shown. Each of Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, claim-integration, sidecar/observability, and routing is plausibly a multi-session unit of work on its own. An epic of this scope cannot be executed coherently in one session and the plan does not enumerate or even sketch the children it should be split into. The plan ironically concerns a gate that flags 'too big to one-shot' and 'children that don't cover the parent', so its own decomposition gap is notable.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Decomposition section listing the child tickets (e.g. C1 Layer-1 deterministic floor + fail-open tooling, C2 criteria registry/orchestrator, C3 Layer-2 advisory criteria, C4 attestation/signing + invalidation, C5 claim integration + --force, C6 routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, C7 REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer-skip, C8 config surface, C9 docs) and state which children cover which acceptance criteria, so the parent is decomposed and child-coverage is auditable.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria throughout: 'extends the close-gate signing', 'reusing rebar.llm extension points', 'reduce rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'reducer skips the type', and Polyglot tooling 'solidlsp (already in repo)'. No concrete files, modules, or symbols are named anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan repeatedly names integration points abstractly ('the close-gate signing', 'rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'the reducer', 'claim integration', 'REVIEW_RESULT event on the ticket') but never identifies a single concrete file path, module, class, or function it intends to touch. For an epic that modifies signing, the claim command, the event reducer, and adds a new event type, the absence of named edit targets and an acknowledged blast radius (which existing call sites/consumers of claim, signing, and the reducer must change) is a scope-accuracy gap that will cause rework. Blast radius is asserted as a concept ('centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time') for the FEATURE but the plan never acknowledges its OWN blast radius into rebar's claim path, signing, and reducer.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the concrete modules/symbols each child will touch (the completion-verifier/close-gate signing module, the claim command handler, the event reducer + event-type registry, rebar.llm's catalog/contract/runner entry points) and list the existing consumers of claim and signing that must be updated. Mark live-codebase confirmations as assumptions to verify.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' vs Design summary 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; and Success Criteria 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' vs scope which contains no measurement/eval deliverable (offline analysis is F1, a follow-on).
findings.finding: Two potential cross-section tensions, neither clearly a contradiction. (1) Shippable defaults are gated on 'linked experiment work' / experiments, but the plan does not state whether that experiment work is in-scope or a prerequisite; if it is out-of-scope this AC cannot be satisfied within the epic. (2) A Success Criterion requires Layer-2 findings that 'measurably help an agent revise the plan,' yet all measurement/FP-remediation tooling is explicitly deferred to follow-on F1 and 'analysis is offline; no human-feedback requirement' — so 'measurably' may be unverifiable inside this epic. Under a benign reading the experiment is a separately-tracked dependency and 'measurably' is aspirational, so this is flagged as a dependency/wording ambiguity rather than a hard contradiction.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify whether 'linked experiment work' is a blocking prerequisite or in-scope, and reword the Success Criterion to a verifiable in-epic check (e.g. 'findings conform to the coaching spec and are structured for offline remediation measurement') so it does not depend on F1.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data'; Layer-2 AC 'plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass'; 'escalation by size+complexity+centrality (centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time)'.
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred onto the executor without resolution. Default chunk size, budget cap, and depth are 'set empirically via experiments' / 'grounded in experiment results' but no concrete numbers, formula, or even ranges are given, and the experiment is referenced as unlinked external work ('see linked experiment work') — an executor cannot ship the required defaults without first deciding or obtaining them. Likewise the router's pivot ('has_children + complexity'), the escalation function over size+complexity+centrality, and how centrality/blast-radius is 'computed at plan time' are named but not specified, leaving the executor to design the algorithm. These are decision-deferrals that push design onto the executor rather than executable instructions.
findings.suggested_edit: Either supply concrete starting defaults/thresholds and the centrality and escalation formulas inline, or explicitly scope a spike/experiment child ticket as a prerequisite and mark the dependent ACs as blocked on it, so no executor has to invent these design decisions mid-implementation.

findings.location: Design summary → 'Criteria registry' and 'Polyglot tooling'; AC 'Criteria registry (declarative descriptors) + generic orchestrator, reusing rebar.llm extension points.'
findings.finding: The plan is broadly disciplined about reuse: it explicitly reuses rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, extends the existing close-gate signing rather than re-implementing it, and reuses repo-present tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard). The main residual over-engineering risk is the large configuration surface introduced at once (per-criterion blocking, chunking knob, budget cap, risk posture, INDETERMINATE behavior) plus a brand-new REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event type — but each knob is tied to a stated client-diversity need and defaults are to be set empirically, so this reads as justified rather than gratuitous. No NIH or single-call-site abstraction is evident from the plan text.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally note in the AC which config knobs ship with non-overridable defaults in v1 vs. which are exposed, to keep the initial config surface minimal and grow it per F4/F5.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list — every AC item is a feature deliverable; none describes how that deliverable will be tested. E.g. 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented in Python, polyglot, fail-open...' and 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check...'
findings.finding: The plan has no testing plan. For a verification GATE the behavioral risk is concentrated in two properties the plan itself stresses — fail-open ('IRONCLAD': unsupported lang/no server/crash/timeout → skipped=PASS, never FAIL) and material-edit invalidation of the signature — yet there are no acceptance criteria asserting tests for these. There is no mention of failure-path/boundary coverage (tool crash, timeout, unsupported language, stale-vs-fresh signature, --force override path), no RED-before-GREEN ordering, and no statement of test type (unit/integration on claim flow). The empirical-defaults work is referenced but that is calibration, not correctness testing.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC such as: 'Tests cover fail-open behavior (each of: unsupported language, missing LSP server, tool crash, timeout → skipped=PASS) and signature lifecycle (fresh attestation passes claim; material edit invalidates and blocks; non-material edit does not; --force records override). Include boundary cases for chunk-atomic checkpointing and INDETERMINATE.' Specify these as integration tests on the claim gate.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'ast-grep', 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; AC 'Attestation: ... (extends the close-gate signing)'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase claims an executor must verify: that solidlsp is 'already in repo', that rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points, that a completion-verifier close-gate signing mechanism exists and can be extended, and that syft/scc/lizard/ast-grep are available/installable in the client environments. These are plausible and internally consistent with the stated context, but they are codebase/environment claims I cannot confirm without the live repo, so I cannot assert they are wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: Before building, confirm the rebar.llm extension-point surface and the close-gate signing API are reusable as assumed, and that solidlsp/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard are present (the fail-open path covers absence at runtime, but the plan assumes presence for the supported-stack path).

findings.location: Title/Context call this an 'epic'; 'This description is the self-contained epic summary.' The Acceptance Criteria then enumerate ~12 distinct deliverables (Layer-1 criteria suite, Layer-2 criteria suite, registry+orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget, feedback authoring, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion config, bug exemption, empirical defaults, docs).
findings.finding: The ticket is explicitly an epic but contains no decomposition into child tickets — the deliverables are listed as acceptance criteria of a single ticket rather than as named children. The 'Follow-on work' section (F1–F12) is deferred scope, not a decomposition of THIS epic's in-scope work. As written, an agent claiming this would attempt to one-shot an epic-sized body of work (gate trigger + two reviewer layers + signing + sidecar event + config + docs + experiment-grounded defaults), which the plan's own premise warns against ('too big to one-shot'). The router pivots on has_children, so an epic with no children is also internally inconsistent with the design's routing assumptions.
findings.suggested_edit: Split into vertical-slice children, e.g.: (1) claim gate + attestation check + invalidation; (2) attestation signing extension + coverage record; (3) criteria registry + orchestrator; (4) Layer-1 deterministic suite (fail-open); (5) Layer-2 advisory suite + feedback authoring; (6) routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing; (7) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar; (8) config surface; (9) docs. Prefer vertical slices (a thin end-to-end gate first) over horizontal layers so an early slice is independently shippable.

findings.location: ## Context paragraph 1-2
## Success Criteria vs ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding: The plan carries TWO parallel criteria blocks (Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria) plus a Design summary, which mostly map well to each other, but a few items have weak one-to-one mapping that would benefit from a confirming pass. Specifically: the Acceptance Criteria item 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on an external 'linked experiment work' that is not described in the plan body; without seeing that linked ticket I cannot confirm the criterion maps to in-scope described work vs. orphaned dependency. Terminology is otherwise consistent (Layer-1/Layer-2, attestation, REVIEW_RESULT, fail-open used uniformly).
findings.suggested_edit: Confirm the experiment-results criterion is satisfiable within this epic or explicitly mark it as gated on the linked experiment ticket; otherwise it reads as an orphan criterion with no described body work.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Layer-2 advisory criteria ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Several criteria are not measurable/verifiable within a single session as written. (1) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' specifies no metric, threshold, or harness — 'measurably help' is unverifiable as stated and implies a multi-trajectory study. (2) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' explicitly depends on out-of-session experiment work (the design summary even says defaults are set 'EMPIRICALLY via experiments'), which cannot be completed in one working session. (3) The Layer-2 criterion list is very large (a dozen advisory criteria + triggered overlays + a broad pass) and the success bar for 'implemented' is not stated as a measurable pass condition.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with a concrete in-session check (e.g., 'findings include criterion id, file/section location, and suggested fix, verified by N fixture tickets'). Move/flag the experiment-grounded defaults criterion as gated on a separate linked ticket so this epic's acceptance is self-contained, or ship documented placeholder defaults plus a verifiable config-surface test.

findings.location: Design summary / Acceptance Criteria
## Context
Acceptance Criteria list, e.g. 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Most ACs map cleanly to the Design summary, but a few coherence gaps exist. (1) The AC 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results' references 'linked experiment work' that is never described or named in the plan body — orphaned external dependency with no anchor in Design summary. (2) The 'Docs' AC has no corresponding bullet in the Design summary (docs/CI-check pattern is only implied by 'composable CI rigor signal'). (3) The success-criteria item 'checkable by an external CI process' duplicates the AC 'signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process' — near-duplicate phrasing across the two requirement lists, which is fine as a Success↔AC mapping but worth confirming intentional. These are minor since a benign reading treats Success Criteria as outcomes and ACs as concrete deliverables.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line pointer in the Design summary naming the experiment ticket/work that grounds the defaults, and add a 'Docs' line to the Design summary so the Docs AC has a body anchor.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Two requirements appear to require out-of-session validation or are not measurable as stated within one working session. (1) 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' depends on observing an agent's revision behavior — no in-session measurement procedure or pass threshold is defined, so it reads as unmeasurable ('measurably help'). (2) 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on a separate experiment effort completing; if that experiment is not in-session, this AC cannot be satisfied within this epic's session. Other ACs (Layer-1/Layer-2 implemented, claim integration, sidecar event, per-criterion config) are concrete and verifiable.
findings.suggested_edit: Reframe the 'measurably help' criterion to an in-session-checkable proxy (e.g., 'findings include grounded location + suggested fix and conform to the coaching-spec checklist, verified against fixture plans'), and either scope the experiment-defaults AC to 'wire a default value placeholder, finalize when linked experiment completes' or explicitly mark it as dependent on the linked experiment ticket.

findings.location: Design summary vs Success/Acceptance Criteria

Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'syft + registry'; Acceptance Criteria 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding: The plan is broadly conscious of NIH risk and explicitly reuses existing infrastructure: it extends the close-gate signing rather than building new signing, reuses rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner via a generic orchestrator, and leans on already-present tooling (solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, scc, lizard). The criteria-registry + generic-orchestrator abstraction is justified by the Rule-of-Three (many criteria run through it), not a single call-site. One residual watch-item worth noting but not blocking: the config surface is large (risk posture, chunking, per-plan budget cap, per-criterion advisory|blocking, INDETERMINATE non-blocking toggle), and could proliferate; the plan partially mitigates by shipping 'sensible defaults' and deferring tuning knobs to follow-ons F2–F5.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally call out which config knobs ship as project-overridable vs hard-coded defaults in v1 to keep the surface minimal initially.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (full list) and Success Criteria — no testing/verification AC is present for the gate's own implementation; Layer-2 AC says findings 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' with no test described
findings.finding: The plan specifies many behaviors (claim blocks on stale/absent signature, fail-open skipped=PASS, --force override recorded, reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT, invalidation on material edits, INDETERMINATE non-blocking default) but contains no acceptance criterion describing how any of this gate's own behavior is tested. Critical boundary/failure paths that are explicitly part of the design have no stated test coverage: fail-open on unsupported-lang/no-server/crash/timeout (each should assert skipped=PASS not FAIL), material-edit-invalidates vs tags/comments/links/assignee-do-not (the boundary between invalidating and non-invalidating events), --force override path, and reducer correctly ignoring the sidecar type. The claim that Layer-2 findings 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' implies a measurement but no test/eval harness is described. There is no RED-before-GREEN or failure-path ordering guidance.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit testing AC, e.g.: 'Tests cover fail-open paths (unsupported lang/no-server/crash/timeout each yield skipped=PASS), the material vs non-material edit invalidation boundary, --force override recording, reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT, and claim-blocking on absent/stale signature.' Specify behavioral/contract tests for the invalidation event classification.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'ast-grep (resolution/callers)', 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'extends the close-gate signing'; AC 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase/environment assertions an executor must verify before relying on them: that solidlsp is already in the repo and usable for the needed resolution; that rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points suitable for a generic criteria orchestrator; that the close-gate signing can be extended to bind to a 'latest material event commit' and is reusable here; and that ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard are available in the toolchain. These cannot be confirmed from plan text alone and require live-codebase access. They are plausible (the plan says 'already in repo' / mirrors the completion-verifier), so this is a verification note rather than a defect.
findings.suggested_edit: Before execution, confirm: (1) rebar.llm's actual extension-point API surface supports per-criterion descriptors + chunking; (2) the close-gate signing module can be extended to material-event-commit binding; (3) the 'material vs non-material event' classification maps to existing event types in the reducer. List these as explicit pre-flight verification steps.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate'; body 'This epic builds...'; 'Follow-on work (to be filed as separate linked tickets, NOT part of this epic)'; the ~13 Acceptance Criteria spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim integration, routing, sidecar event, docs
findings.finding: This is labeled an EPIC, yet it is presented as a single ticket with one flat list of ~13 acceptance criteria and no child decomposition. The scope is very large and multi-component: Layer-1 (7+ deterministic polyglot checks each needing tool integration), Layer-2 (~11 advisory criteria + overlays + broad pass), a criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing + material-event binding + invalidation, claim integration with override, routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer change, per-criterion config, and docs. As an epic, decomposition into children should be present and sensible, but the plan only lists follow-ON tickets (F1–F12) which are explicitly out of scope — it does not decompose the in-scope work into executable children. Without children, this cannot be one-shot and risks a horizontal-layer build. Note: if children are intended to be filed separately after this plan is accepted, the plan should say so explicitly; absent that, this reads as an under-decomposed epic.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose the epic into vertical-slice children, e.g.: (C1) claim-gate + attestation signing/binding/invalidation MVP (no review yet, fail-open everywhere); (C2) criteria registry + orchestrator on rebar.llm; (C3) Layer-1 deterministic checks; (C4) Layer-2 advisory checks; (C5) routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing; (C6) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer-ignore + offline-analysis readiness; (C7) per-criterion config + docs. Add a statement that children will be filed and linked, and that this epic ticket itself is not directly executed.

findings.location: Design summary / Polyglot tooling: "solidlsp (already in repo)"; Criteria registry: "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner"; Attestation AC: "(extends the close-gate signing)"; Context: "rebar has a completion-verifier *close* gate".
findings.finding: The plan rests on several claims about existing rebar internals that an executor must verify against the live codebase before relying on them: (1) solidlsp is already in the repo; (2) rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points; (3) a close-gate signing mechanism exists and can be extended for material-event-commit binding; (4) a completion-verifier close gate exists to mirror; (5) the reducer can be made to ignore a sidecar event type. I cannot confirm these without codebase access; they are plausible but load-bearing.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Assumptions to verify' note listing these existing-component claims (solidlsp present, rebar.llm extension points, close-gate signing shape, reducer event-skip mechanism) so the executor confirms each before building on it.

findings.location: Execution: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments"; AC: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)"; AC: "a bounded broad pass" / Design summary "one bounded broad open-ended pass"; Design summary: "complexity" routing pivot.
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred onto the executor or to unspecified external work. The defaults for chunking/budget/depth are explicitly pushed to 'experiment results (see linked experiment work)' which is not specified here, so an executor cannot ship 'sensible defaults' without it. The 'bounded broad pass' and 'per-plan budget cap' name bounds without values. 'complexity' is the routing pivot but is never defined/measured. These are deferral-style placeholders that push design decisions to execution time.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline provisional default values (and mark them as to-be-tuned), or make the dependency on the linked experiment ticket an explicit blocking prerequisite; and define how 'complexity' and the 'bounded' broad-pass cap are computed/measured.

findings.location: AC: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)" combined with Success Criterion "measurably help an agent revise the plan".
findings.finding: Most ACs are individually verifiable (claim blocks without attestation; sidecar event emitted; per-criterion config exists; bugs exempt). However two end-states have a verification gap: 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criterion / Layer-2 AC) names no metric or measurement method, and the 'grounded in experiment results' AC depends on linked-experiment work whose completion criteria are not visible here, so 'done' for defaults is not self-contained. I cannot confirm whether the linked experiment scope closes this gap.
findings.suggested_edit: Specify how 'measurably help' is evaluated (e.g., remediation-rate threshold on a sample) and state explicitly whether the experiment ticket is a prerequisite whose outputs feed these defaults.

findings.location: AC: "`claim` integration: fast signed-attestation check"; AC: "reducer skips the type"; AC: "extends the close-gate signing".
findings.finding: The plan identifies the touch surfaces conceptually (claim path, reducer, signing/attestation, new criteria registry/orchestrator, config surface, REVIEW_RESULT event) and acknowledges blast radius (e.g., 'out of hot paths', 'reducer skips the type'). It does not name concrete files/modules, which is acceptable at epic level, but the consumers of the claim flow and reducer changes (any code asserting on existing reducer event sets, CLI/claim callers, CI signature consumers) are not enumerated. Whether these consumers exist and need updating requires the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: When decomposing, have each child enumerate concrete file/module targets and the consumers of the claim flow, reducer event set, and signature format that may need updating.

findings.location: AC: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)" and Design summary: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments"
findings.finding: The plan defers a real design decision (the default chunking/budget/depth values) onto an unspecified 'linked experiment work' that is referenced but never defined within the plan. An executing agent reaching this AC cannot proceed without stopping to ask what the experiment is, where its results live, or what to do if they don't exist yet. This is decision-deferral plus an undercooked placeholder ('see linked experiment work').
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) cite the concrete experiment ticket ID and where its results are recorded, or (b) ship explicit interim default values in this epic and flag them as provisional pending the experiment, so the executor is never blocked on an unresolved dependency.

findings.location: AC: "Attestation: signing + ... (extends the close-gate signing)" and "`claim` integration: fast signed-attestation check"; Design: "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner"
findings.finding: The plan names the broad subsystems it will touch (claim command, close-gate signing, rebar.llm extension points, reducer/event pipeline) but does not enumerate specific files/symbols or the consumers a change of this size would require updating (e.g. the claim command handler, the reducer dispatch that must learn to skip REVIEW_RESULT, the attestation/signing module shared with the close gate). Whether these targets are plausible and whether all consumers are covered cannot be confirmed without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a file_impact list naming the concrete modules to be edited (claim handler, signing module, reducer dispatch, rebar.llm registry) so blast radius and consumer coverage can be assessed.

findings.location: Design summary: "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner", "solidlsp (already in repo)", "extends the close-gate signing", "reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR"
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase assertions an executor would need to verify: that rebar.llm exposes a reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension surface, that solidlsp is present and usable, that the completion-verifier's close-gate signing is extensible for the start gate, and that the reducer can be made to cleanly ignore a sidecar event type. These are plausible but unconfirmed against code I cannot inspect.
findings.suggested_edit: Note these as assumptions to validate first (a brief discovery step) before implementation, citing where in the repo each extension point lives.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; and Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Several core defaults are explicitly deferred to unspecified future experiments rather than supplied as concrete values. The plan repeatedly says defaults will be set 'empirically via experiments' and points to '(see linked experiment work)' without naming the experiment ticket, its outputs, or any provisional default to use in the interim. An executing agent cannot ship 'sensible defaults' (required by Success Criteria) without either the experiment results or a stated placeholder, so it would have to stop and ask which values to bake in. Additionally the chunking knob, budget cap magnitude, and depth thresholds are described qualitatively ('finer'/'coarser', 'generous') with no committed numbers.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the specific linked experiment ticket and state explicit provisional/interim defaults (e.g. a default chunk size, a default per-plan budget cap, default complexity thresholds) to be used until experiments refine them, so the epic is executable independent of the experiment's completion.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'extends the close-gate signing', 'reusing rebar.llm extension points', 'solidlsp (already in repo)', '`claim` integration'
findings.finding: The plan names the systems it will touch/extend (close-gate signing, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, the claim command, the reducer for the new REVIEW_RESULT event, solidlsp/ast-grep integrations) but does not name concrete files/modules/symbols, and blast radius on consumers of the claim path and the reducer cannot be confirmed without the live rebar codebase. The intent to extend rather than fork is acknowledged, but the specific edit targets and any callers requiring updates need codebase access to verify.
findings.suggested_edit: List the concrete modules/files for: the claim command handler, the signing/attestation module being extended, the reducer registration point for the ignored sidecar event, and the rebar.llm extension points — and call out any consumers of claim/attestation that the change would force to update.

findings.location: Context: 'rebar has a completion-verifier close gate'; Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; Attestation AC: 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified assertions about the existing codebase: that a completion-verifier close gate with reusable signing exists, that rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points reusable by a generic orchestrator, that solidlsp is already in the repo, and that the reducer can be made to cleanly ignore a new sidecar event type. These are plausible but must be confirmed against the actual code before the executor relies on 'extends' / 'reuse' as a basis for design. I cannot confirm them without the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a brief verification step at the top of execution confirming the assumed extension points (close-gate signing API, rebar.llm runner contracts, solidlsp presence, reducer event-skip mechanism) exist as assumed, with a fallback note if any do not.

findings.location: Two parallel lists: 'Success Criteria' (6 bullets) and 'Acceptance Criteria' (13 bullets), plus the 'Design summary'.
findings.finding: The plan carries two overlapping requirement lists (Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria) covering largely the same ground, which makes mapping coherence hard to verify and risks near-duplicate requirements. Most AC items do map cleanly to the Design summary (e.g. Layer-1/Layer-2, attestation, claim integration, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion config). One possible orphan: the Design summary's 'AMBIGUOUS escalates to a higher-tier adjudicator' appears in the routing AC ('escalation by size+complexity+centrality') only obliquely — adjudication of AMBIGUOUS verdicts is not its own acceptance criterion. Also 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' is stated in both the Design summary and the routing AC (near-duplicate phrasing). Recommend (suggestion) collapsing Success vs Acceptance Criteria into one list, or explicitly stating Success Criteria = epic-level outcomes and Acceptance Criteria = deliverables, and adding an explicit criterion for AMBIGUOUS→adjudicator handling.
findings.suggested_edit: State the intended relationship between the 'Success Criteria' and 'Acceptance Criteria' lists (outcomes vs deliverables), and add an AC covering adjudication of AMBIGUOUS verdicts so it is not orphaned in the design body.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (13 bullets) and 'Design summary' — no testing/verification AC for the gate's own implementation.
findings.finding: The plan specifies extensive behavior (fail-open-on-unsupported-stack 'IRONCLAD', signature invalidation on material edits but not on tags/comments, reducer-ignored sidecar, --force override recording, chunk-atomic checkpointing, INDETERMINATE outcomes) but contains no acceptance criterion describing how any of this is tested. For a gate whose correctness hinges on fail-open and on the precise set of 'material' vs non-material events, the absence of failure-path/boundary test requirements (unsupported language → skipped=PASS; tool crash/timeout → PASS; tag edit → signature stays valid; description edit → invalidated) is a notable gap likely to cause rework. Note structural/command-output assertions are a fine pattern here for the polyglot floor — but they still need to be required by the plan.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC requiring tests covering: fail-open paths (unsupported lang, missing LSP server, tool crash, timeout → skipped=PASS), material vs non-material edit invalidation matrix, --force override logging, and the reducer ignoring REVIEW_RESULT. Specify RED-before-GREEN where practical.

findings.location: Document is titled an 'epic' ('This epic builds...') but the entire body is structured as a single ticket with one flat Acceptance Criteria list; no child tickets are enumerated beyond the deferred Follow-on F1–F12.
findings.finding: The plan self-identifies as an epic and describes a very large surface (Layer-1 with ~7 polyglot deterministic checks, Layer-2 with ~12 advisory criteria plus overlays and a broad pass, a criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing/binding/invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion config, and docs). This is far too big to execute coherently in one session, yet no decomposition into child tickets is present — only out-of-scope follow-ons (F1–F12) are listed. If this is meant to be the epic parent, it must enumerate children covering the in-scope ACs and demonstrate child-coverage of the parent; if it is meant to be a single executable ticket, it is badly oversized and should be split into vertical slices (e.g., Layer-1 floor + attestation + claim as one slice; Layer-2 criteria + registry/orchestrator as another; routing/budget; observability sidecar; docs).
findings.suggested_edit: Decide and state explicitly whether this is the epic parent (then enumerate child tickets that collectively cover the in-scope ACs) or a leaf; if leaf, split into vertically-sliced children (e.g., claim+attestation+Layer-1 floor MVP first; Layer-2 advisory criteria second; routing/escalation/budget third; observability sidecar fourth; docs/config last).

findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 ('coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan') and AC 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec'; plus AC 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results'.
findings.finding: Several criteria are not in-session measurable as written. 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria #3) and Layer-2's 'measurably help' framing imply an effectiveness measurement (remediation rates) that the plan itself says is OFFLINE replay/analysis and explicitly a follow-on (F1). So this acceptance criterion cannot be verified within the implementation session. Similarly 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on external experiment work that may not complete in-session and is not bounded here. Recommend rewording the in-epic AC to a verifiable form (e.g., findings are well-formed per the coaching schema) and moving the 'measurably helps' effectiveness claim to F1 follow-on.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword Success Criteria #3 / the Layer-2 AC to a measurable form (findings conform to coaching schema: grounded location + severity + checklist), and explicitly scope the 'measurably helps / remediation-rate' claim to the F1 offline follow-on rather than this epic's acceptance.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list vs Design summary; e.g. AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' vs Success Criteria.
findings.finding: Mapping is broadly coherent — most ACs trace to a Design-summary bullet and Success-Criteria item. Two minor frictions worth noting: (1) the AC 'Defaults...grounded in the experiment results' references 'linked experiment work' that is not described in the plan body (the body says 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' but does not name/link the experiment ticket), creating a near-orphan dependency. (2) Terminology overlaps that may read as duplicate requirements: 'INDETERMINATE outcome' appears in both the routing/budget AC and the Verdict-model bullet, and 'per-criterion config: advisory|blocking-with-override' appears as its own AC and again inside the routing AC's 'per-criterion blocking' — verify these are intentionally one requirement, not two.
findings.suggested_edit: Add the explicit linked experiment ticket id in the body where 'experiments' is first mentioned, and consolidate the per-criterion advisory/blocking statement into a single AC referenced from the routing AC to avoid duplicate-requirement ambiguity.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section as a whole; no AC names tests for the gate's own behavior. The only testing reference is the Layer-2 criterion 'testing-plan completeness' (a check the gate runs on OTHER tickets).
findings.finding: The plan describes a substantial new feature (claim gate, Layer-1 deterministic checks, attestation signing/invalidation, sidecar event, fail-open behavior) but does not describe how any of it will be tested. There is no AC for unit/integration tests of: claim-block-vs-allow on presence/absence/staleness of attestation, fail-open paths (unsupported lang/tool crash/timeout → skipped=PASS), material-edit invalidation vs non-material edits, --force override recording, or reducer-ignored sidecar handling. These are precisely the high-risk, easily-regressed behaviors and need explicit test coverage (RED-before-GREEN where applicable, plus failure/boundary cases like timeout and tool-crash).
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC: 'Tests cover gate behavior: claim block/allow across attestation present/absent/stale/material-edit-invalidated; fail-open for each tool (unsupported lang, no server, crash, timeout → skipped=PASS); material vs non-material edit invalidation table; --force override is recorded; REVIEW_RESULT is ignored by the reducer.' Specify failure-path and boundary cases explicitly.

findings.location: Document is titled an 'epic' ('This epic builds a plan-review verification gate') yet the Acceptance Criteria are a flat list of ~13 large implementation items with no decomposition into child tickets.
findings.finding: This is explicitly framed as an epic but presents no decomposition into children. The scope spans Layer-1 (7+ deterministic polyglot checks across LSP/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard), Layer-2 (12+ advisory criteria plus router overlays and a broad pass), a criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing/invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, a sidecar event with offline-analysis schema, config surface, and docs. That is far too large for one unit of work and risks a horizontal-layer build-out (all of Layer-1, then all of Layer-2...) where vertical slices (e.g. one criterion end-to-end through claim+attestation+sidecar) would be safer and de-risk earlier. The plan even ironically asserts plan-of-record detail lives in an external session log, so the child breakdown exists elsewhere but is not surfaced here.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a decomposition section listing proposed child tickets (e.g. C1 attestation+claim gate skeleton end-to-end with one trivial Layer-1 check; C2 registry+orchestrator; C3 each Layer-1 check; C4 each Layer-2 criterion group; C5 sidecar+offline schema; C6 config surface; C7 docs), and prefer at least one vertical slice (one criterion through the full claim/attest/sidecar path) as the first child.

findings.location: Success Criterion: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that MEASURABLY help an agent revise the plan'; and AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'.
findings.finding: Two criteria are not in-session measurable as written. (1) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no defined metric or pass threshold and implies an empirical study (revision/remediation rates) that cannot be verified within a single implementation session — it reads like an out-of-session validation. (2) 'Defaults grounded in the experiment results' depends on a separate experiment whose completion is outside this ticket, so it is not completable within this session unless the experiment is already done. Most other ACs are concrete and verifiable, but these two should be made structurally checkable or moved to follow-on.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the Layer-2 SC to a structural, in-session check (e.g. 'findings conform to the coaching schema: grounded location + criterion id + severity + optional suggested-fix, validated by tests on fixtures') and move the 'measurably help' empirical claim to follow-on (it overlaps F1/F4 follow-ons). For the defaults AC, either gate it behind the linked experiment ticket or specify placeholder defaults shippable now.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list vs. Success Criteria / Design summary
findings.finding: Acceptance criteria map well onto the design summary, and terminology (Layer-1/Layer-2, advisory/blocking, INDETERMINATE, attestation, REVIEW_RESULT) is used consistently throughout. The one near-duplicate worth noting: the AC bullet 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override' overlaps the earlier AC '...per-criterion blocking via config' folded into the Success Criteria 'A project can configure...per-criterion blocking', but these read as a deliberate Success-Criteria-vs-Acceptance-Criteria restatement rather than a true duplicate, so no change is required under a benign reading.

findings.location: 'Criteria registry... reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' and 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly leans on existing infrastructure (rebar.llm extension points, the completion-verifier close-gate signing, solidlsp/ast-grep already in repo) rather than reinventing it, which is the opposite of NIH. The config surface is broad (risk posture, chunking, budget caps, per-criterion blocking, INDETERMINATE behavior), which is a genuine over-engineering risk, but the plan justifies the diversity of knobs by the 'BROAD, DIVERSE CLIENT BASE' and defers FP-driven auto-tuning to follow-ons F2/F4, so it is a defensible reading. Confirming the rebar.llm/close-gate reuse claims requires live-codebase access.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' and AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Two criteria are not completable/measurable within one working session as written. (1) 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no defined metric or threshold and implies an experiment/measurement loop to satisfy — it reads as 'works well' rather than a checkable assertion. (2) 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' depends on a separate, linked experiment whose completion is outside this ticket's session; if that work is not done, this AC cannot be verified in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the coaching-help criterion to something checkable in-session, e.g. 'each advisory finding includes criterion+location+severity+suggested-fix fields and is rendered as a checklist item, verified by unit tests on the finding schema.' For the defaults criterion, either gate it on the linked experiment as an explicit dependency (and ship placeholder defaults this session) or scope it to 'defaults are config-overridable and documented as experiment-pending.'

findings.location: AC: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check...' and 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding + invalidation-on-material-edit'
findings.finding: The end-state (claim blocks without a valid non-stale attestation; --force override recorded) is clearly stated and each AC names a concrete artifact, so termination is mostly defined. However, the heavy review is explicitly OUT-OF-BAND from claim, and the plan does not state how a client invokes/produces the attestation (the 'review process' entry point/command), so the union of steps to actually EARN a fresh signature isn't fully traced from trigger to signed artifact. Confirming whether that entry point already exists or needs building requires codebase access.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC for the out-of-band review entry point (the command/process that runs Layer-1+Layer-2 and emits the signature + REVIEW_RESULT), so the path from open ticket -> review run -> fresh signature -> claim is end-to-end traceable.

findings.location: Whole ticket, titled 'epic' in Context ('This epic builds...') but presented as a single ticket with one flat Acceptance Criteria list
findings.finding: The Context calls this an EPIC and the scope is very large (Layer-1's 7+ deterministic checks, Layer-2's ~12 advisory criteria plus overlays and a broad pass, a declarative criteria registry + orchestrator, attestation signing/binding/invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget/checkpointing, a sidecar event + reducer change, per-criterion config, and docs). That is far too big to one-shot, yet no child decomposition is present — the AC list reads as a flat leaf checklist. An epic should enumerate its children (or be marked has_children) rather than carry all implementation ACs itself; ironically this is exactly the 'too big to one-shot / children don't cover the parent' failure the gate is meant to catch.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets along vertical slices (e.g. C1 Layer-1 floor + fail-open; C2 attestation signing/binding/invalidation; C3 claim integration + --force; C4 criteria registry + orchestrator; C5 Layer-2 advisory criteria; C6 routing/budget/checkpointing; C7 REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + reducer; C8 config surface; C9 docs) and convert this ticket's ACs into parent-level coverage assertions over those children.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (no testing/verification AC) and Success Criteria 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: The plan describes WHAT to build in detail but contains no testing plan: no AC asserts unit/contract tests for the Layer-1 deterministic criteria, no fixtures for fail-open behavior (unsupported language/no-server/timeout/crash -> skipped=PASS), no test that material edits invalidate the signature while tags/comments/links/assignee do not, no test that the reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT, and no boundary/failure-path coverage for the budget cap / INDETERMINATE path. For a fail-open safety gate these are exactly the paths most needing RED-before-GREEN coverage. Note the structural-grep test pattern would be fully legitimate here for the polyglot checks.
findings.suggested_edit: Add testing ACs, e.g.: 'Layer-1 fail-open paths covered by tests across >=2 unsupported/absent-tooling fixtures (skipped=PASS, never FAIL)'; 'invalidation matrix tested: material edits (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition) invalidate, non-material (tags/comments/links/assignee) do not'; 'reducer ignores REVIEW_RESULT verified by test'; 'budget-cap exhaustion yields INDETERMINATE (non-blocking default) verified by test'.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check', 'Attestation: ... extends the close-gate signing', 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event ... reducer skips the type', 'Criteria registry ... reusing rebar.llm extension points'
