findings.finding: The plan repeatedly names the systems it will modify by behavior/concept (the claim path, the reducer, the close-gate signing, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, solidlsp already-in-repo) but does not name a single concrete file, module, or symbol that will be touched or added. For an epic that explicitly invokes 'edit-set/scope accuracy' as one of its own criteria, the plan's own edit set is left entirely implicit. Blast radius is acknowledged conceptually (it touches claim, the reducer, signing) but the specific callers/modules are not enumerated, so an executing agent cannot tell whether the targets are real or hallucinated. Several integration points imply consumer updates that are not called out: the reducer must learn to skip a new event type (a consumer change in the reduction path), and the claim command must gain a new pre-check (a consumer change in the claim path).
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files/modules touched' subsection naming the concrete claim-command module, the reducer module that must ignore REVIEW_RESULT, the close-gate signing module being extended, and the rebar.llm extension points being reused, plus any new package(s) for Layer-1/Layer-2 criteria. Mark each as new vs. modified.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; and 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two criteria are unmeasurable or out-of-session as written. (1) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' specifies no metric, threshold, or dataset, so there is no way to verify it within a session ('measurably' is asserted but not operationalized). (2) Defaults 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' make this epic depend on a separate experiment whose results may not exist at execution time — an out-of-session / cross-ticket dependency that cannot be completed by the agent in one working session if the experiment hasn't run. The remaining ACs (Layer-1 checks, claim integration, sidecar event, config knobs, docs) are implementable and verifiable in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the Layer-2 AC to a concrete in-session check (e.g., 'given fixture plans with seeded defects, the reviewer emits a grounded finding citing criterion+location for each seeded defect'). For the defaults AC, either gate it behind the linked experiment ticket explicitly with a fallback default value the agent ships if results are unavailable, or move it to follow-on.

findings.location: Context section names beneficiaries (autonomous agents authoring plans, the broad client base, CI consuming the cert) and the problem (~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects; early errors compound).
Acceptance Criteria list vs. Design summary
findings.finding: Most acceptance criteria map cleanly to the design summary, and vice-versa. A couple of very minor near-overlaps exist: the AC 'Per-criterion config: advisory (default) | blocking-with-override' substantially overlaps the routing/config AC and the Success-Criterion bullet on per-criterion blocking; and 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results' overlaps the 'Configurable chunking; proportionate-depth routing... budget cap' AC. These are complementary (one covers mechanism, one covers default-derivation) rather than true duplicates, so this is benign. Terminology is consistent throughout (Layer-1/Layer-2, attestation, REVIEW_RESULT, fail-open, advisory/blocking).

findings.location: Design summary 'Criteria registry' and 'Polyglot tooling'; Config-surface AC
findings.finding: The plan explicitly reuses existing infrastructure rather than reinventing it: it extends the close-gate signing, reuses 'rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', and uses already-in-repo solidlsp. This is the opposite of NIH. The config surface is broad (chunking, budget caps, per-criterion blocking, risk posture) but each knob is justified by the stated diverse-client-base requirement, and over-aggressive promotion-to-blocking is deliberately deferred to follow-ons (F2-F4). One area to watch but not block: whether the generic orchestrator / criteria-registry abstraction is justified — it is used by many criteria (Layer-1 + Layer-2), so it clears Rule-of-Three. Confirming the actual reuse of rebar.llm extension points needs the live codebase.

findings.location: Success Criterion: 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Two criteria are not measurable/completable within one session as written. (1) 'findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan' specifies no metric or pass threshold — 'measurably help' is unverifiable as stated and risks requiring an out-of-session A/B study. (2) 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' makes acceptance depend on a separate linked experiment whose completion is outside this ticket's session; if that experiment isn't done, this AC cannot be satisfied in-session. Also, 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' in the design summary embeds the same out-of-session dependency.
findings.suggested_edit: Reframe the Layer-2 success criterion to an in-session measurable proxy (e.g., 'each emitted finding includes criterion-id + file/section location + severity + suggested-fix-when-confident, validated on a fixture set of N seeded-defect plans'). For defaults, either treat experiment-derived numbers as inputs already available (cite the experiment ticket as a hard dependency) or specify placeholder defaults shippable now with experiment tuning deferred to a follow-on.

findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria coverage of claim/attestation/Layer-1/Layer-2/sidecar/docs
findings.finding: The end-state is verifiable and reachable: 'done' is checkable via the claim gate behavior (valid signature required, --force override recorded), the signed attestation, the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, and docs. The INDETERMINATE outcome plus chunk-atomic checkpointing give an explicit termination/stop semantics, and the review-process-with-re-run model mirrors the existing completion-verifier. The union of acceptance criteria appears to cover every claimed deliverable in the design summary, with no obvious gap between described work and stated done-state.

findings.location: Document is labelled 'epic'; Acceptance Criteria list (12 ACs spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim, routing, sidecar, config, docs)
findings.finding: This is explicitly an epic but ships as a single flat list of 12 large acceptance criteria with no decomposition into child tickets, even though the plan repeatedly refers to children-as-deliverables (F1-F12 are follow-ons, not in-epic children). The body asserts each AC is itself a substantial workstream (Layer-1 has ~7 distinct polyglot tooling integrations: solidlsp, ast-grep, syft, registry, scc, lizard, plus DAG/AC-quality/destructive checks; Layer-2 has ~12 criteria + overlays + a broad pass). Executing all of this as one unit is far too big to one-shot and lacks the explicit child decomposition the criterion expects for an epic. There is also a horizontal-vs-vertical-slice risk: Layer-1 and Layer-2 are layer seams, whereas a safer first child would be a thin vertical slice (claim gate + attestation + one Layer-1 check end-to-end).
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit decomposition section splitting the epic into children, e.g.: (C1) claim gate + attestation binding/invalidation + signing (vertical slice with one trivial Layer-1 check), (C2) criteria registry + orchestrator, (C3) Layer-1 tooling integrations grouped by tool, (C4) Layer-2 advisory criteria, (C5) routing/budget/checkpointing, (C6) REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + override logging, (C7) config surface + docs. State that each AC group becomes a child ticket.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all 12 items) and Design summary — no test/verification subsection
findings.finding: The plan describes substantial behavior (fail-open on unsupported stacks, skipped=PASS, material-edit invalidation vs. non-material edits, --force override recording, reducer-ignored sidecar, INDETERMINATE non-blocking default) but never states how any of it will be tested. There is no mention of failure-path/boundary tests (e.g., tool crash/timeout → PASS, stale signature → block, non-material edit → no invalidation), no RED-before-GREEN ordering, and no behavioral/contractual coverage plan. Given the fail-open 'IRONCLAD' requirement and signing/invalidation correctness, the absence of an explicit testing plan is a meaningful rework risk.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Testing section with behavioral cases: fail-open paths (unsupported lang, missing LSP server, tool crash, timeout each assert skipped=PASS); attestation invalidation matrix (material edit invalidates; tag/comment/link/assignee does not); claim gate (no sig blocks, stale sig blocks, valid sig passes, --force recorded); sidecar event is reducer-ignored; INDETERMINATE non-blocking. Specify RED-before-GREEN for the claim-gate enforcement logic.

findings.location: Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase assertions an executor must verify before relying on them: (1) 'solidlsp (already in repo)' and the existence of 'rebar.llm extension points (catalog/contracts/prompt/runner)' to reuse; (2) that the attestation work 'extends the close-gate signing' — i.e. that a close-gate signing mechanism exists and is extensible in the assumed shape; (3) that rebar's reducer can be made to ignore a sidecar event type (the 'reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR' / 'reducer skips the type' claim). These are existence/shape claims about the live codebase I cannot confirm.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Assumptions to verify before implementation' note pinning the expected rebar.llm extension API, the close-gate signing entry points to extend, and the reducer's event-type-skip mechanism, so the executor confirms these against the code rather than assuming them.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; Layer-2 AC: 'a bounded broad pass' and 'severity-ranked + capped'
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred to the executor or to unspecified external work. (1) Defaults for chunking/budget/depth are 'grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' — the experiment is not specified here and no concrete default values or fallback are given, so an executor cannot ship 'sensible defaults' without stopping to find/run that work. (2) 'bounded broad pass', 'per-plan budget cap', 'severity-ranked + capped' specify that bounds/caps exist but not their values, pushing the actual numeric design onto the executor. This is decision-deferral the plan should resolve or explicitly mark as out-of-scope-with-placeholder defaults.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline concrete placeholder defaults (e.g. default chunk size, budget cap, severity cap N) to ship pending experiment results, or explicitly state the dependency ticket id and that this epic is blocked on it for those values.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'; Acceptance Criteria 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec'
findings.finding: Most acceptance criteria are concretely verifiable (claim blocks without signature, Layer-1 fails open, sidecar event emitted, docs exist). One end-state is hard to terminate on: 'coaching findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan' / 'measurably help' has no stated measurement or pass threshold within this epic, and the FP/remediation-rate analysis tooling is explicitly deferred to follow-on F1. So how an executor verifies the 'measurably help' criterion is satisfied is unclear, though the rest of the union of steps plausibly reaches the stated end-state.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the 'measurably help' success criterion to something verifiable within this epic (e.g. findings conform to the coaching spec format and are emitted), and move the measurement claim to F1, or cite the specific metric/threshold used to verify it.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'; 'Attestation ... extends the close-gate signing'; 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar event ... reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan names the surfaces it intends to touch (the `claim` command path, the close-gate signing module, the event reducer, and the criteria registry/orchestrator built on rebar.llm) which is reasonable for an epic. However it does not name specific files/symbols, and the blast radius of touching the reducer and the claim hot path (acknowledged via 'out of hot paths' / 'fast check') depends on the live codebase I cannot inspect. No obviously hallucinated targets, but consumer/caller impact of modifying `claim` and the reducer should be confirmed against the code.
findings.suggested_edit: When decomposing into child tickets, list the concrete entry-point files/symbols for the `claim` path, the close-gate signer, and the reducer's event-type dispatch, and call out any existing callers of those that the change touches.

findings.location: AC: 'Attestation: ... (extends the close-gate signing)' and 'claim integration'; Design summary 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' and 'solidlsp (already in repo)'.
findings.finding: The plan repeatedly names integration points and reused subsystems (close-gate signing, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, solidlsp, the reducer, the claim command) but never names the concrete files/modules/symbols it will touch or extend. The blast radius IS acknowledged conceptually (it modifies claim flow, the reducer with a new ignored event type, and the signing path), which is good, but the specific edit targets are not enumerated, so plausibility of those targets can't be confirmed from plan text and a notable consumer-update risk exists: introducing a new event type touches every reducer/replay/serialization path that enumerates event types.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Files/modules touched' subsection naming the claim command module, the reducer (and a note that REVIEW_RESULT must be added to any exhaustive event-type switch/enum and any replay/serialization code), the signing module being extended, and the rebar.llm extension points. Mark which are new vs modified. (Suggestion — verify exact paths against the codebase.)

findings.location: Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'extends the close-gate signing'; Rule-of-Three handled by 'generic orchestrator' for many criteria.

Success criterion: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'.
findings.finding: Several criteria are not measurable/verifiable within one working session as written. 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no defined metric or in-session test — measuring agent revision improvement implies an offline study, not a session-completable check. 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' makes acceptance depend on a separate, out-of-session experiment ticket, so this criterion can't be completed within this epic's session unless those results already exist. 'Feedback-authoring conforms to the coaching spec' (grounded/specific/ranked/affirming/checklist) is checkable only if the spec is enumerated as concrete assertions.
findings.suggested_edit: Reword the success criterion to a session-verifiable form (e.g., 'findings include criterion id + file/section location + severity + suggested fix when confidence>threshold, validated by unit tests on sample plans'). For the defaults criterion, either mark it as blocked-on/inherited-from the linked experiment ticket or change acceptance to 'config plumbing exists with placeholder defaults; final values set when experiment lands'.

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket ... nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound'; '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; beneficiary = autonomous agents / clients consuming the CI rigor signal.
Acceptance Criteria: "Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)"; Design summary: "default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments"
findings.finding: Multiple concrete design decisions are explicitly deferred to unspecified external work. The default chunking/budget/depth values are pushed onto an unnamed "linked experiment work" with no value, no fallback default, and no link reference provided in the plan text. An executor cannot ship the AC "sensible defaults shipped" without those experiments being done first, and the plan does not state whether the experiment is a prerequisite or whether placeholder defaults are acceptable in the interim.
findings.suggested_edit: State interim placeholder defaults to ship with (so the epic is executable independently), and explicitly mark the experiment-derived tuning as a separate sequenced dependency with the actual ticket link, or note the experiment is a hard prerequisite of this AC.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (entire) and Success Criteria "...findings...that measurably help an agent revise the plan"
findings.finding: The plan describes WHAT to build extensively but never describes HOW any of it will be tested. There is no testing strategy: no mention of unit tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks, no fixtures for the polyglot fail-open behavior (e.g. unsupported-language => skipped=pass), no test for material-edit invalidation of the attestation, no test for the claim-blocking path or --force override, no RED-before-GREEN ordering. The Success Criterion 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' implies an evaluation method that is never specified.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing-plan section/AC: e.g. fail-open unit tests per tool (unsupported lang, missing server, timeout => PASS), attestation invalidation tests on material vs non-material edits, claim-gate block/override integration test, and the eval harness used to substantiate 'measurably helps'.

findings.location: Title says "epic"; Acceptance Criteria contains ~13 large items spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim integration, routing/escalation, sidecar events, config, docs
findings.finding: This is labeled an epic but presents a single flat acceptance-criteria list with no decomposition into child tickets, despite the design summary's own routing logic pivoting on has_children and the criteria registry alone (Layer-1 ~7 checks, Layer-2 ~12 checks plus overlays) being far too large for one unit of work. For an epic, criterion G5 expects decomposition into children to be present and sensible; it is absent. Risk of horizontal-layer-seam splitting is also live (Layer-1 vs Layer-2 vs attestation could each be split as layers rather than vertical slices).
findings.suggested_edit: Add a decomposition section listing proposed child tickets (e.g. claim-gate+attestation MVP slice, Layer-1 checks, each Layer-2 criterion cluster, registry/orchestrator, sidecar/observability, config, docs) and prefer vertical slices (one end-to-end thin gate) over horizontal layers.

findings.location: Success Criteria "...findings...that measurably help an agent revise the plan"; AC "Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented"
findings.finding: Most ACs are individually verifiable, but the union-reaches-end-state check is undermined by the 'measurably help' success criterion having no defined measurement/threshold, so 'done' for the coaching layer is not objectively determinable from the plan. Whether the steps reach this end-state cannot be decided without the eval method (which lives in the referenced session log not available here).
findings.suggested_edit: Define the measurement for 'measurably help' (e.g. remediation-rate target on a sample, or a qualitative acceptance bar) so termination of the Layer-2 AC is checkable.

findings.location: Success Criteria vs Acceptance Criteria sections
findings.finding: There are two overlapping requirement lists (Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria) that largely restate the same items in different words (e.g. SC 'requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation...recorded --force override' duplicates AC 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check with --force+justification override'; SC Layer-1 fail-open duplicates AC Layer-1). This near-duplication risks divergence. Separately, the AC 'Defaults...grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' is an orphan-ish requirement with no body text describing the experiment beyond 'default set EMPIRICALLY'.
findings.suggested_edit: Either explicitly frame Success Criteria as outcomes and Acceptance Criteria as the verifiable build checklist (and state the mapping), or collapse the duplication; ensure the experiment-defaults AC ties to a named linked ticket.

findings.location: Design summary: "reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner", "solidlsp (already in repo)", "extends the close-gate signing", AC "reusing rebar.llm extension points"
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified codebase assertions: that rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points, that solidlsp is already integrated/usable, that the completion-verifier close-gate signing can be extended for plan attestations, and that a reducer can cleanly ignore a new sidecar event type. These are plausible but require verification against the actual repo, which I cannot access.
findings.suggested_edit: Before execution, confirm the named rebar.llm extension points and the close-gate signing reuse path exist as described; note these as assumptions to validate in the first child ticket.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: The plan defers a real design decision to unlinked/unspecified 'experiment work'. An executing agent cannot ship defaults for chunking, budget, and depth without those experiment results, but the plan neither contains them nor specifies provisional defaults to use until they land. This is an explicit decision-deferral ('set EMPIRICALLY via experiments') that pushes an unresolved design decision onto the executor and creates a hard dependency on work that is only vaguely referenced ('see linked experiment work').
findings.suggested_edit: Either name the linked experiment ticket explicitly and mark this AC as blocked-on-that-dependency, or specify concrete placeholder defaults (e.g. 'ship default chunk=N, budget=$X, depth=medium; revise once experiment T-xxxx completes') so the executor can proceed without stalling.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list and Success Criteria — e.g. 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC 'Layer-1 deterministic criteria implemented... fail-open'
findings.finding: The plan describes WHAT each criterion does but does not describe how the gate's own implementation will be tested. There is no mention of unit/integration tests for the orchestrator, the fail-open paths (unsupported lang/no server/crash/timeout → skipped=PASS), the signing/invalidation logic, or the claim integration. For a feature whose central guarantee is 'fail-open IRONCLAD', the absence of a stated test approach for those skip paths and for material-edit invalidation is a notable gap. The success criterion 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' also lacks any described measurement/test method.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing-plan section: e.g. golden-input tests per Layer-1 criterion (including unsupported-stack inputs asserting skipped=PASS), tests that material edits invalidate the signature while tags/comments do not, an end-to-end claim-blocked/--force-override test, and a defined metric for 'measurably help' (e.g. remediation-rate on a labeled set).

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate'; description repeatedly calls this 'This epic'; the single flat Acceptance Criteria block (~14 items)
findings.finding: The ticket self-identifies as an EPIC ('This epic builds...', 'This description is the self-contained epic summary') yet presents no decomposition into child tickets. Its acceptance criteria span Layer-1 (7 polyglot tools), Layer-2 (12+ judgment criteria + overlays + broad pass), a criteria registry/orchestrator, attestation signing/invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budgeting, the sidecar event + reducer changes, config surface, and docs — far too large to execute coherently in one session. For an epic, decomposition into children should be present; here only Follow-on (F1–F12) items are listed, which are explicitly NOT part of this epic.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit child-ticket decomposition for the in-scope work (e.g. Layer-1 floor, Layer-2 advisory criteria, registry/orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/budget, sidecar/observability, config, docs) with each child sized for a session, OR retitle/rescope as a true epic-with-children rather than a single executable ticket.

findings.location: Success Criteria vs Acceptance Criteria; e.g. SC 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' vs AC list
findings.finding: Most acceptance criteria are individually verifiable (claim blocks without attestation; sidecar event emitted; reducer skips type; --force override recorded). However the end-state for 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings that measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no described verification/termination procedure — there is no stated measurement harness, threshold, or done-condition, so 'measurably' is unreachable as written from the described steps. Because the gap hinges on the (unlinked) experiment work and offline-analysis substrate, I cannot fully decide reachability without that scope.
findings.suggested_edit: Define a concrete verification for the 'measurably help' criterion (metric + target + data source), or move the measurement explicitly to the offline-analysis follow-on and soften this AC to 'emits findings conforming to the coaching spec' which IS verifiable from the described steps.

findings.location: Success Criteria (6 items) vs Acceptance Criteria (~14 items); plus AC 'Defaults... grounded in the experiment results' vs Design summary 'default set EMPIRICALLY'
findings.finding: There is overlap/duplication and some mapping mismatch between the two criteria lists. The plan carries both a 'Success Criteria' block and an 'Acceptance Criteria' block that largely restate the same requirements in different words (e.g. SC 'requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation' vs AC 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'; SC 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar' vs AC 'REVIEW_RESULT reducer-ignored sidecar'). This near-duplication makes it ambiguous which list is authoritative and risks divergence. Additionally, the SC list has 6 high-level items while the AC list has ~14 — the mapping between them is implicit, not stated.
findings.suggested_edit: Either collapse Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria into one authoritative checklist, or explicitly state the relationship (e.g. 'Success Criteria = outcomes; Acceptance Criteria = the verifiable deliverables that satisfy them') and cross-reference which ACs satisfy which SC.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'syft + registry', 'extends the close-gate signing', 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; AC 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified codebase assertions an executor would need to confirm: that solidlsp is 'already in repo', that the completion-verifier 'close gate' signing can be extended, and that rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points. These are plausible but cannot be confirmed from plan text alone, and the entire registry/orchestrator and attestation work is predicated on them. I lack the live codebase to verify.
findings.suggested_edit: Before relying on these, add a short verification note or pointer (file/path) confirming solidlsp's presence, the close-gate signing module to be extended, and the specific rebar.llm extension points reused, so the executor isn't building on assumed APIs.

findings.location: Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'syft + registry + LLM-fallback'; Attestation AC: 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase assertions an executor must confirm before relying on them: (1) rebar.llm exposes reusable 'catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' extension points; (2) solidlsp is 'already in repo' and usable for resolution; (3) a close-gate signing mechanism exists and can be extended for attestation; (4) the reducer can be made to ignore a sidecar event type. These are plausible but unverifiable from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Assumptions to verify first' note pointing to the specific rebar.llm extension points and the close-gate signing module the attestation will extend, so the executor verifies them before building on them.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list and Success Criteria — e.g. 'Layer-2 review returns coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan' and 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: The plan describes WHAT to build but contains no testing plan for the gate itself. There is no statement of how Layer-1 determinism, fail-open behavior, attestation invalidation-on-material-edit, the reducer-ignored sidecar, or the claim/--force flow will be tested (unit/behavioral coverage, failure-path cases such as tool crash/timeout→skip=PASS, boundary cases like stale-signature vs material-edit). 'Measurably help an agent revise the plan' is asserted as a success criterion but no measurement/test method is given.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an acceptance criterion enumerating test coverage: deterministic Layer-1 checks have unit tests; fail-open paths (unsupported lang, server crash, timeout) tested to yield skip=PASS; attestation invalidation tested for material vs non-material edits; claim-block-without-attestation and --force-override tested; reducer-ignores-REVIEW_RESULT tested.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'claim integration', 'Attestation ... extends the close-gate signing', '`REVIEW_RESULT` reducer-ignored sidecar event'
findings.finding: The plan names conceptual edit targets (claim command, signing module, event reducer) but no concrete files/symbols, and the blast radius of touching the claim path and the event reducer (a hot path) is not explicitly enumerated. Whether consumers of claim or the reducer need updating cannot be confirmed without the live codebase. The plan does acknowledge hot-path sensitivity ('out of hot paths', 'reducer skips the type'), which mitigates this.
findings.suggested_edit: List the specific modules expected to change (claim handler, close-gate signing, event reducer, config schema) and call out any callers of claim/the reducer that may need updating.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two criteria are not in-session measurable as written. 'Measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no defined metric or threshold and implies a behavioral evaluation. 'Defaults grounded in the experiment results' depends on linked-but-external experiment work that may not complete within this session, creating an out-of-session dependency. Other ACs are largely measurable.
findings.suggested_edit: Either define a concrete in-session measurable proxy (e.g. coaching findings conform to the grounded/specific/ranked/checklist schema and are validated by fixtures) or move the 'measurably helps' and experiment-grounded-defaults goals to follow-on/linked tickets, shipping documented placeholder defaults here.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria checklist as a whole vs Success Criteria — e.g. 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' and 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: End-state reachability has a gap: the ACs build the mechanism (Layer-1/2, registry, attestation, claim integration, sidecar) but no AC verifies the stated end-state that findings 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' — there is no done-condition or evaluation step connecting build to that outcome. Additionally, the 'grounded in experiment results' AC depends on linked experiment work whose completion is not gated here, so 'done' for that AC is undefined within the epic. The verification story for the gate itself (how an executor confirms the whole gate works end-to-end) is also not stated.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an end-to-end verification AC (e.g. an integration test driving open→in_progress claim against a fixture ticket: no attestation blocks, fresh signature allows, material edit invalidates, --force overrides with logging) and either define the done-condition for the experiment-grounded defaults or relabel it as 'placeholder defaults, tuned in follow-on'.

findings.location: Title says 'epic'; Context: 'This epic builds a plan-review verification gate'. Acceptance Criteria list 12 distinct build items (Layer-1 criteria, Layer-2 criteria, registry+orchestrator, attestation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget, feedback-authoring, sidecar event, per-criterion config, bug-exemption, experiment-grounded defaults, docs).
findings.finding: The ticket self-identifies as an epic but contains no decomposition into child tickets within the plan itself. The 12 acceptance criteria each represent substantial, independently-shippable subsystems (a deterministic polyglot Layer-1 floor; an LLM-driven Layer-2 coaching reviewer; a criteria registry/orchestrator; signing/attestation; claim-gate integration; routing/escalation/budgeting; a sidecar event + reducer change; config surface; docs). This is far too large to execute coherently in one session and is not sliced into children here. The Follow-on (F1-F12) list is explicitly out-of-epic, so it does not constitute decomposition of THIS scope. There is also a horizontal-layer-seam risk: Layer-1, Layer-2, attestation, claim-integration, and observability are split by mechanism rather than as vertical slices, so no end-to-end 'claim is actually gated' slice is independently deliverable.
findings.suggested_edit: Either add an explicit child-ticket breakdown to the plan (e.g., one vertical thin-slice child that wires open->in_progress to a single Layer-1 check + attestation + claim-gate end-to-end, then children that broaden Layer-1 criteria, add Layer-2, add routing/budget, add the sidecar, add docs), or state in the description that decomposition into children is tracked elsewhere with a link. As written the epic's children are unspecified.

findings.location: Context section: '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; beneficiary is the autonomous agent claiming a ticket and the CI process consuming the rigor signal.

Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; 'Generous initial budget + depth posture'; intent fidelity '(2-step blind-restate)'.
findings.finding: Multiple real design decisions are deferred to the executor or to unspecified external work. (1) Default chunking/budget/depth values are explicitly contingent on 'linked experiment work' that is not resolved here — an agent executing this epic cannot ship defaults without those results, and the link is named only as '(see linked experiment work)'. (2) 'Generous initial budget' and 'proportionate depth' are unquantified placeholders the executor must invent. (3) Several Layer-2 mechanisms are named parenthetically ('2-step blind-restate', 'tool-primed', 'bounded broad pass') without enough specification to implement without choosing the design. These are decision-deferrals that push design onto the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: State the dependency explicitly (block or sequence this epic behind the experiment ticket, with its id), and either provide provisional default ranges to ship pending experiments or scope defaults out of this epic. Define what 'generous' budget and 'proportionate depth' mean as concrete parameters, and add a one-line spec or pointer for the 2-step blind-restate / tool-primed / bounded-broad-pass mechanisms.

findings.location: Success Criteria vs Acceptance Criteria; e.g. AC 'Bugs are exempt from this gate' <-> Design 'Bugs are SKIPPED'; AC 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar' <-> Success 'Every review emits a REVIEW_RESULT sidecar event'.
findings.finding: Acceptance criteria map well onto the described design and the Success Criteria, with consistent terminology (Layer-1/Layer-2, attestation, REVIEW_RESULT, fail-open, INDETERMINATE used uniformly). Minor near-duplication: the same requirements appear in both 'Success Criteria' and 'Acceptance Criteria' (e.g., REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion blocking config, fail-open coverage), and AC 'Defaults...grounded in experiment results' partially overlaps with the routing/budget AC. This is benign overlap rather than conflicting requirements, so not a defect — but the two near-identical lists could be deduplicated to avoid drift.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally fold the 'Success Criteria' (outcomes) and 'Acceptance Criteria' (build items) into clearly distinct framings, or cross-reference them, so the REVIEW_RESULT / per-criterion-config / defaults items aren't restated in two places that could diverge.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; Acceptance Criteria: 'extends the close-gate signing'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several codebase claims an executor would need to confirm against the live repo: (a) 'solidlsp (already in repo)'; (b) rebar.llm exposes reusable 'catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' extension points; (c) a 'completion-verifier close gate' with reusable signing infrastructure exists to extend; (d) ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard are available or installable in the rebar environment. These are plausible self-references but cannot be verified from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Assumptions to confirm before build' note listing the close-gate signing module, rebar.llm extension points, and solidlsp/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard availability, so the executor validates them up front rather than mid-flight.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list (Layer-1 / Layer-2 / claim integration / sidecar event) — no testing AC present except 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: For an epic that itself builds a verification gate, the plan never states how the gate's own implementation will be tested. There is no AC requiring unit/integration tests for: fail-open behavior on unsupported stacks (the 'IRONCLAD' invariant), material-edit invalidation of the signature, the fast claim check + --force override path, or the reducer correctly ignoring REVIEW_RESULT. The 'experiment results' reference is calibration, not correctness testing. Fail-open and signature-invalidation are exactly the high-risk paths most needing failure-case and boundary tests.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC: 'Tests cover fail-open on unsupported-language/missing-tool/crash/timeout (skipped=pass), signature invalidation on each material-edit class vs no-invalidation on tags/comments/links/assignee, the claim fast-check + --force override, and reducer-ignores-REVIEW_RESULT.'

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'claim integration', 'extends the close-gate signing', 'reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan names the integration surfaces (claim command, signing module extending the close-gate, reducer behavior, criteria registry/orchestrator) at a coherent level, but the concrete files/symbols are not named and blast radius into the reducer and claim hot path can only be confirmed against the live codebase. The reducer change in particular ('reducer skips the type') is a touch to a hot path with potential blast radius that is acknowledged ('out of hot paths') but not enumerated.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the concrete modules/files for the claim command, the signing extension point, and the reducer, and note any other consumers of the event-reducer that must tolerate an ignored event type.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'
findings.finding: Two criteria are not in-session measurable as written. 'Measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no defined metric or threshold and implies an evaluation that may require out-of-session experimentation. 'Grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' makes this epic's completion depend on a separate, possibly not-yet-complete experiment ticket — an out-of-session dependency that can't be validated within one working session. Most other ACs are structurally verifiable, but these two are not.
findings.suggested_edit: Make the 'measurably help' criterion concrete (e.g. on a fixed fixture set, agent-applied findings reduce Layer-1 floor failures), and either land the experiment defaults as a prerequisite or ship provisional defaults in this epic with the experiment grounding deferred to follow-on.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria checklist vs the epic scope; 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Most ACs map to verifiable end-states, and the gate provides its own done-signal (the signed attestation). However, the union of steps reaching 'done' is partly gated on external/empirical inputs: the experiment-grounded defaults AC and the 'measurably help an agent revise' success criterion have no stated stop condition within the epic, so 'when is this epic done' is under-defined where it depends on linked experiment work whose status isn't visible from the plan.
findings.suggested_edit: State the explicit done-condition for the experiment-dependent items (e.g. 'defaults set from experiment ticket X; if X incomplete, ship documented provisional defaults') so end-state reachability does not hinge on unverifiable external work.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)'; the plan repeatedly calls itself an 'epic' ('This epic builds...', 'the self-contained epic summary', 'NOT part of this epic'); Acceptance Criteria list of ~12 items.
findings.finding: This is explicitly framed as an EPIC, yet it carries no decomposition into child tickets. The body lists at least a dozen large, independently-shippable workstreams (Layer-1 deterministic criteria, Layer-2 advisory criteria, criteria registry+orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget, REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, per-criterion config, docs, experiment-grounded defaults). Each is a substantial unit of work; collectively they are far too big to execute coherently in one session. For an epic, child decomposition must be present and sensible, and it is absent. There is also a horizontal-seam risk: 'Layer 1', 'Layer 2', 'registry/orchestrator', 'attestation', 'claim integration' read as horizontal layers, whereas a safer first slice is a thin vertical (one criterion end-to-end through claim+attestation+sidecar).
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit child-ticket breakdown (or state the children are tracked elsewhere with links). Sequence them as vertical slices: e.g., (1) end-to-end skeleton = orchestrator + one Layer-1 criterion + attestation + claim gate + sidecar; then (2..n) add remaining criteria, routing, config, docs. Mark the F1-F12 list as out-of-scope (already done) and distinguish it from in-epic children.

findings.location: ## Context paragraph

Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; 'Generous initial budget + depth posture to gather data'; Layer-2 item 'a bounded broad pass' / 'one bounded broad open-ended pass'.
findings.finding: Several real design decisions are deferred onto the executor or onto unstated external work, so an agent could not execute without stopping to ask. (1) Defaults for chunking/budget/depth are explicitly punted to 'experiment results (see linked experiment work)' but no experiment ticket/values are provided here — the executor cannot ship 'sensible defaults' without those numbers. (2) 'Generous initial budget' and 'proportionate depth' are unquantified placeholders. (3) The list of Layer-2 criteria and Layer-1 criteria is large and described at a slogan level (e.g. 'anti-slop', 'destructive sniff', 'AC/DD quality'), leaving the precise check semantics to the executor. (4) 'bounded broad pass' leaves the bound unspecified.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline concrete default values (or a placeholder table the executor fills from the linked experiment ticket, with the ticket ID named) for chunk size, per-plan budget cap, and depth tiers; quantify 'generous' and 'bounded'. If these are intentionally deferred to a linked experiment, state that the experiment is a prerequisite child and that this epic blocks on it, rather than leaving it as an inline TODO.

findings.location: Two parallel lists: 'Success Criteria' (6 bullets) and 'Acceptance Criteria' (~12 bullets), plus 'INDETERMINATE' appearing in both Verdict model and AC.
findings.finding: The plan has two overlapping requirement lists (Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria) that largely restate each other, creating near-duplicate requirements and ambiguity about which list is authoritative for sign-off (e.g., claim-gate/--force appears in both; REVIEW_RESULT sidecar appears in both; CI rigor-signal appears in both). Coverage is otherwise good (each AC maps to a body concept). Minor terminology drift: 'attestation' vs 'certification'/'cert'/'signature'/'signed result' are used for what appears to be the same artifact, and 'AC/DD' is used without expansion; this risks an executor treating them as distinct.
findings.suggested_edit: Designate one list as the authoritative acceptance gate and label the other as narrative/goals (or merge them). Standardize on a single term for the signed artifact (e.g., 'attestation') and define 'DD' on first use. Confirm Success vs Acceptance criteria are intentionally distinct rather than duplicates.

findings.location: Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'
findings.finding: Two criteria depend on out-of-session activity. 'Measurably help an agent revise the plan' implies an effectiveness measurement that cannot be verified within a single implementation session, and 'defaults grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' explicitly defers to a separate experiment whose results may not exist when this epic is executed. As written an agent cannot verify these in-session.
findings.suggested_edit: Reframe the 'measurably help' criterion as an in-session-checkable property (e.g., 'findings conform to the coaching schema: each has criterion+location+severity'); for the defaults criterion, state the fallback default values to ship if experiment results are not yet available, and make the experiment-grounding a follow-on.

findings.location: AC: 'Attestation: signing + material-event-commit binding ... (extends the close-gate signing).' and 'claim integration: fast signed-attestation check'
findings.finding: The plan extends/touches existing rebar subsystems (close-gate signing, the reducer for the new REVIEW_RESULT event, the `claim` command path, rebar.llm extension points) but never names the actual files/modules/symbols to be modified. The blast radius of adding a new event type to the reducer and intercepting the claim transition is real but the specific consumers (reducer dispatch, claim handler, signing module) are not enumerated. (Plan-text only; concrete targets need the live codebase.)
findings.suggested_edit: Add an edit-set section naming the concrete modules to modify (signing module shared with close-gate, reducer dispatch where the REVIEW_RESULT type is skipped, the claim transition handler, rebar.llm catalog/runner) and the consumers that read the attestation.

findings.location: Design summary: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'extends the close-gate signing', 'solidlsp (already in repo)'
Title says 'epic'; throughout: 'This epic builds...'; Follow-on work lists F1-F12 as separate tickets but the epic's own Acceptance Criteria contains ~13 large items
findings.finding: This is explicitly an epic ('This epic builds a plan-review verification gate') yet it carries no decomposition into child tickets — instead a single flat list of ~13 acceptance criteria each of which is itself a substantial subsystem (Layer-1 polyglot criteria, Layer-2 advisory criteria, registry+orchestrator, attestation/signing, claim integration, routing/escalation/budgeting, sidecar event+reducer, config surface, docs). An epic of this scope is far too big to execute in one session and is not split into children, which is the exact failure mode this very gate is meant to catch.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into child tickets along vertical slices (e.g., 'Layer-1 floor + claim gate end-to-end', 'attestation signing+binding', 'Layer-2 advisory reviewer', 'criteria registry/orchestrator', 'REVIEW_RESULT sidecar', 'config surface', 'docs') and convert this ticket into the parent that tracks them.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria and Success Criteria sections — no testing strategy is stated anywhere
findings.finding: The plan describes substantial new behavior (blocking on stale attestations, fail-open on unsupported stacks, reducer-ignored sidecar events, --force override) but contains no testing plan: no mention of how fail-open is verified, how invalidation-on-material-edit is tested, failure/boundary paths (tool crash, timeout, unsupported language), or RED-before-GREEN ordering. The 'fail-open IRONCLAD' guarantee in particular is high-risk and untested as written.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing-plan section: tests for fail-open on each unsupported-stack path (skipped=pass), material-edit invalidation, stale-signature blocking, --force override recording, and reducer-ignores-REVIEW_RESULT; specify the test pattern (unit + structural assertions) per layer.

findings.location: Design summary: 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', '(extends the close-gate signing)'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified claims about the existing codebase: that solidlsp is already present, that rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points, and that a close-gate signing mechanism exists and is extensible. These are load-bearing for reuse but cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Note these as assumptions to verify first (presence of solidlsp, the rebar.llm extension surface, and the close-gate signing API) before relying on them, ideally with a pointer to where each lives.

findings.location: Success Criteria <-> Acceptance Criteria mapping (claim gate, Layer-1, Layer-2, config, sidecar, CI signal all appear in both)
AC: 'plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass'; 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time'; F-list deferrals
findings.finding: Several design decisions are deferred to the executor or to unspecified experiments. 'Default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' and 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results' push real values to undecided experiments; 'triggered overlays (router-selected)' and 'a bounded broad pass' name mechanisms without specifying the selection rules or the bound; 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' is asserted without an algorithm. An executing agent would need to stop and ask how these are computed/selected.
findings.suggested_edit: Specify (or scope to a child ticket) the router overlay-selection rule, the concrete bound on the broad pass, the centrality/blast-radius computation method, and provisional default values to use until experiments land.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)' vs body
## Context

Acceptance Criteria: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)' and Success Criteria: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: Two criteria are not in-session completable or measurable as stated. (1) Defaults 'grounded in experiment results' externalizes the work to a separate linked experiment whose outcomes are unknown at execution time — the agent cannot complete this within one session if the experiments haven't run. (2) 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' requires an out-of-session empirical/efficacy evaluation with no stated metric or measurement procedure. Both read as 'works well'-style unmeasurable goals.
findings.suggested_edit: Split the empirical-defaults work into the linked experiment ticket and have this epic ship explicit placeholder defaults plus a config surface; restate the efficacy AC as a concrete, in-session check (e.g. 'findings include criterion+location+suggested-fix fields verified by a fixture test on N sample plans').

findings.location: Design summary 'Execution' / Acceptance Criteria block
findings.finding: Verification of 'done' is mostly implied per-AC (tests for each criterion) but the review-loop termination ('client revises and re-runs to earn a fresh signature') has no stated end-state for what happens when a plan never converges past blocking criteria except --force. The union of steps plausibly reaches the success criteria, but the stop condition for a failing review loop (other than override) is not explicitly defined.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit termination statement: e.g. a blocking criterion is resolved by passing OR by recorded --force override OR by INDETERMINATE-non-blocking, and nothing else loops indefinitely.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Attestation ... (extends the close-gate signing)' and '`claim` integration', 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'
findings.finding: The plan names integration points (close-gate signing, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, solidlsp, the reducer, claim command) but does not name concrete files/symbols/modules to edit, so edit-set plausibility and the blast radius into the reducer/claim/signing paths cannot be confirmed from plan text. The sidecar 'reducer skips the type' change touches a hot path and its consumers are not enumerated.
findings.suggested_edit: List the concrete modules/symbols to be modified (claim command handler, reducer event dispatch, close-gate signing module, rebar.llm runner) and the consumers of the reducer change; mark blast radius explicitly.

findings.location: whole plan
Design summary 'Criteria registry' and 'Polyglot tooling' (reuse rebar.llm, solidlsp 'already in repo', extends close-gate signing)
Title 'epic' + Acceptance Criteria (12 distinct AC bullets spanning Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim, routing, sidecar, config, docs)
findings.finding: This is explicitly an epic ('This epic builds...') yet the plan ships no decomposition into child tickets — it presents ~12 large, independently-substantial workstreams (Layer-1 polyglot tooling, Layer-2 ~12 advisory criteria, registry/orchestrator, attestation+signing, claim integration, routing/escalation, sidecar event, config) as one unit. This is far too big for one coherent session and the criteria mix horizontal layers without a vertical first slice.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into children (e.g. registry+orchestrator skeleton; Layer-1 floor; attestation+signing+claim gate as one vertical slice; each Layer-2 criterion or small clusters; sidecar event; config surface; docs) and have this ticket act as the parent epic, with a thin end-to-end vertical slice first.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria and Success Criteria blocks
findings.finding: The plan describes WHAT must be built extensively but never describes HOW any of it will be tested — no mention of behavioral/contract tests, failure-path/boundary cases (e.g. fail-open on tool crash/timeout, stale-signature invalidation, --force path, INDETERMINATE), or RED-before-GREEN ordering. Fail-open behavior and material-edit invalidation are exactly the boundary cases that need explicit test coverage.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing strategy section: contract/behavioral tests for the claim gate (valid/stale/absent signature, --force), fail-open tests per tool (unsupported lang, missing server, crash, timeout → skipped=pass), invalidation-on-material-edit vs non-material event, and INDETERMINATE-non-blocking.

findings.location: 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'extends the close-gate signing', 'the reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified codebase claims: solidlsp is 'already in repo', rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points, the close-gate signing is extensible, and the reducer can be made to ignore an event type. An executor must verify each against the actual code before relying on it; I cannot confirm them from plan text.
findings.suggested_edit: Mark these as assumptions to validate first, or cite the specific modules/APIs that provide each (solidlsp location, rebar.llm extension API, close-gate signing function, reducer type-dispatch).

findings.location: Success Criteria vs Acceptance Criteria

Execution: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC: 'Defaults ... grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; 'plus triggered overlays (router-selected) and a bounded broad pass'
findings.finding: The plan defers real design decisions to the executor / external work: default chunking/budget/depth values are 'set empirically via experiments' that may not be done, the router's overlay selection and 'bounded broad pass' bound are unspecified, and the per-criterion blocking thresholds are undefined. An executing agent would need to stop and ask what defaults to ship and how the router selects overlays.
findings.suggested_edit: Specify concrete placeholder defaults and the bound for the broad pass, and define the router's overlay-selection rule (or explicitly scope the empirical tuning out to the linked experiment with stated interim values).

findings.location: Title vs Design summary / Acceptance Criteria
AC: 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work)'; Design summary: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'
findings.finding: Multiple key numeric design decisions are explicitly deferred to experiments whose results are not in this plan. The default chunking size, per-plan budget cap, and depth posture are all 'set EMPIRICALLY via experiments' with no values, ranges, or fallback defaults stated. An executing agent cannot ship the 'sensible defaults' required by Success Criteria #4 without those experiment results, which are referenced as separate ('see linked experiment work') but not summarized here. This is decision-deferral that pushes a real design choice onto the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline provisional defaults (e.g. 'ship default chunk=N, budget=$X until experiments land') or make the experiment ticket a hard prerequisite dependency and state that this epic is blocked on it. State what happens if experiments aren't done: do we ship a placeholder default?

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list — no AC describes how the gate's own implementation is tested; only 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' (Success Criteria #3) and 'Defaults ... grounded in experiment results' appear.
findings.finding: The plan describes a substantial new feature (Layer-1 deterministic checks, Layer-2 advisory reviewers, attestation signing/invalidation, claim integration, sidecar events) but contains no testing plan for any of it. There is no mention of unit/contract tests for the deterministic Layer-1 criteria, fail-open behavior tests (the 'IRONCLAD' fail-open claim is untested), signature-invalidation-on-material-edit tests, or claim-gate integration tests. Fail-open correctness in particular is safety-critical and asserted but never accompanied by a test obligation.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC requiring tests for: Layer-1 fail-open on unsupported lang/no-server/crash/timeout (skipped=pass), signature invalidation on each material vs non-material event type, and claim blocking/--force behavior. Specify these as RED-before-GREEN where the gate's blocking logic is involved.

findings.location: AC: 'Attestation: ... (extends the close-gate signing)'; 'claim integration'; 'reusing rebar.llm extension points'; 'reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan names the integration surfaces it will touch (close-gate signing code, the `claim` command, rebar.llm catalog/contracts/prompt/runner, the event reducer) but gives no concrete file/module/symbol paths, so plausibility and missing-consumer analysis cannot be confirmed from plan text. The reducer change in particular ('reducer skips the type') is a hot-path edit whose consumers (other reducer call-sites, event replay) would need checking against the live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the actual modules/files for: the claim command handler, the existing close-gate signing module being extended, and the reducer being modified, so blast radius can be assessed.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate'; body repeatedly calls it 'This epic'; the Acceptance Criteria list ~12 large independent deliverables.
findings.finding: The ticket is self-described as an 'epic' and enumerates ~12 substantial, independently-shippable workstreams (Layer-1 suite, Layer-2 suite, criteria registry+orchestrator, attestation signing/invalidation, claim integration, routing/escalation/budget, coaching feedback authoring, sidecar event+reducer, per-criterion config, bug exemption, experiment-grounded defaults, docs) — far too big to execute in one session. Yet no child decomposition into children is present; the Acceptance Criteria are written as if this single ticket does it all. For an epic, child decomposition into sensible vertical slices should be present and listed.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into linked child tickets (e.g. one per AC cluster: Layer-1 floor, Layer-2 reviewers, registry/orchestrator, attestation+claim gate, routing/budget, sidecar+config, docs) and convert this ticket into the parent that tracks them. State whether each AC maps to a child.

findings.location: Design summary explicitly reuses solidlsp/ast-grep/syft/scc/lizard 'already in repo' and 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner' and 'extends the close-gate signing' — the plan deliberately reuses existing infra rather than reinventing.
'solidlsp (already in repo)'; 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; 'extends the close-gate signing'; 'a reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR event ... reducer skips the type'
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified codebase assertions that an executor would need to confirm: that solidlsp is present and usable, that rebar.llm exposes catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points reusable here, that the close-gate signing module can be cleanly extended, and that the reducer can be made to ignore a new event type without side effects. These are plausible but cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'assumptions to verify' note pointing at the actual rebar.llm extension API and the close-gate signing module, so the executor confirms reuse feasibility before building.

findings.location: Context section: 'when an agent claims a ticket ... nothing checks that the ticket's implementation plan is sound', '~44% of agent failures are spec/plan defects'; beneficiary = autonomous agents + the broad client base + CI rigor signal.

Title 'Plan-review verification gate (open->in_progress)' is faithfully served by the design summary and ACs, which all concern the open->in_progress gate; out-of-scope items (bugs, F1-F12) are explicitly excluded.
Success Criteria #3: 'coaching findings ... that measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented'
findings.finding: Success Criteria #3 requires findings that 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' but gives no measurement method, threshold, or harness — 'measurably help' is unmeasurable as stated and likely requires multi-trajectory evaluation that can't be completed in one session. Similarly the experiment-grounded-defaults AC depends on out-of-session experiment work. These criteria are not in-session-completable or verifiable as written.
findings.suggested_edit: Reframe #3 as an in-session-checkable property (e.g. 'each finding is grounded/specific/severity-ranked/checklist-form per the coaching spec, verified by fixture cases') and move the 'measurably help' efficacy claim to the linked offline-analysis follow-on (F1).

findings.location: AC 'Docs: ... CI-check pattern' vs Success Criteria #6; AC 'Bugs are exempt' vs design 'Bugs are SKIPPED'; AC list vs Success Criteria list
findings.finding: Mostly coherent, but there are gaps. The 'experiment-grounded defaults' AC and Success-Criteria-#3's 'measurably help' have no corresponding implementation step in the design summary (orphan-ish criteria pointing at unstated linked work). The plan uses both 'attestation' and 'certification'/'cert'/'signature'/'signed result' for what appears to be the same artifact, and 'SKIPPED' (bugs) collides conceptually with 'skipped = PASS' (fail-open), risking terminology confusion. No duplicates, but terminology consistency could be tightened.
findings.suggested_edit: Standardize on one term for the signed artifact (e.g. 'attestation') and avoid reusing 'skipped' for both bug-exemption and fail-open. Ensure every Success Criterion maps to an AC and design step (esp. #3).

findings.location: Execution section: 'default set EMPIRICALLY via experiments'; AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results (see linked experiment work).'
findings.finding: Multiple core design values are explicitly deferred to a not-yet-completed experiment, and the experiment work is referenced as 'linked experiment work' that is not included in this plan. An executing agent reaching the defaults/chunking/budget/depth ACs cannot proceed without those results — this is decision-deferral that pushes a real design decision (the shipped defaults) onto the executor, who must either wait for or run the experiment.
findings.suggested_edit: Either inline provisional placeholder defaults that the executor can ship (and mark them as 'to be tuned by F-experiment'), or make the dependency on the linked experiment an explicit hard prerequisite/sequencing note so the executor knows to block on it rather than guess.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria list — covers Layer-1, Layer-2, registry, attestation, claim, config, REVIEW_RESULT, docs; no AC describes how the gate itself will be tested.
findings.finding: The plan describes a substantial new verification feature but contains no testing plan: no unit/integration tests for Layer-1 deterministic checks, no fixtures for the fail-open/skipped-on-unsupported-language path, no tests that material edits invalidate the signature, no tests that 'claim' blocks without a valid attestation. Given the fail-open posture is called 'IRONCLAD', the absence of stated tests for that path is a notable gap that will cause rework.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC enumerating the test strategy: behavioral tests for claim-blocking/override, signature invalidation on material vs non-material edits, fail-open behavior on an unsupported language/missing-LSP/timeout (skipped=PASS), and REVIEW_RESULT emission + reducer-ignore. Structural/command-output assertions are acceptable here.

findings.location: Success Criteria 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' and AC 'Layer-2 advisory criteria implemented... measurably help an agent revise the plan'
findings.finding: Most ACs have clear done-states, but the 'measurably help an agent revise the plan' end-state has no verification path described within this epic (the measurement substrate REVIEW_RESULT is capture-only and the analysis is offline / a follow-on F1). Whether this epic's union of steps reaches that stated end-state depends on out-of-scope follow-on work, which cannot be confirmed from plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify that 'measurably help' is demonstrated within-epic by a concrete proxy (e.g., a fixture plan with seeded defects → reviewer surfaces grounded findings), separate from the offline FP/remediation analysis deferred to F1.

findings.location: Design summary 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner'; AC 'Attestation: ...extends the close-gate signing'; AC '`claim` integration'.
findings.finding: The plan names the major edit targets at the component level (rebar.llm extension points, the close-gate signing path, the claim command, a new REVIEW_RESULT event + reducer) and acknowledges blast radius (reducer must skip the new event type, signing path is shared with close-gate). However it names no concrete files/symbols, so plausibility of specific edit targets and missing consumers (e.g., other reducers/event-handlers that iterate event types) cannot be confirmed from plan text — needs live codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Name the concrete modules/files for: the claim command handler, the existing close-gate signing module being extended, and the reducer that must ignore REVIEW_RESULT, so blast radius can be validated before execution.

findings.location: Title 'Plan-review verification gate'; description 'This epic builds...'; the single flat Acceptance Criteria list (~13 ACs spanning Layer-1 polyglot tooling, Layer-2 LLM criteria, registry, attestation, claim, routing/escalation, REVIEW_RESULT, docs).
findings.finding: This is self-described as an EPIC but presents one flat AC list with no decomposition into children, while the criterion expects an epic/parent to have present-and-sensible children. The scope (multiple polyglot tools, ~12 Layer-2 criteria, a registry+orchestrator, attestation/signing changes, claim integration, routing/escalation, a new event + reducer change, docs) is far too large to execute coherently in one session and should be split into vertical-sliceable children rather than left as a single unit.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit decomposition into child tickets (e.g., Layer-1 floor + fail-open; criteria registry+orchestrator; one or two Layer-2 criteria as a vertical slice; attestation/signing extension; claim integration; REVIEW_RESULT sidecar; config surface; docs) and confirm children cover the parent.

findings.location: 'reuse rebar.llm's catalog/contracts/prompt/runner', 'solidlsp (already in repo)', 'extends the close-gate signing', 'a reducer-IGNORED SIDECAR REVIEW_RESULT event'.
findings.finding: The plan rests on several unverified codebase assertions: that rebar.llm exposes reusable catalog/contracts/prompt/runner extension points suitable for this orchestrator, that solidlsp is present and usable for resolution, that the close-gate signing is structured to be extended, and that the reducer can be made to cleanly ignore a new event type. These are plausible but must be verified against the actual code before relying on them; they cannot be confirmed from plan text.
findings.suggested_edit: For each reused component, add a one-line verification step / note pointing the executor to confirm the extension point exists and is shaped as assumed before building on it.

findings.location: AC 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'; Success Criterion 'measurably help an agent revise the plan'; AC 'Defaults for chunking + budget + depth grounded in the experiment results'.
findings.finding: Two acceptance criteria are not in-session measurable as written. 'Measurably help an agent revise the plan' has no defined metric or in-session verification path (the measurement is offline/follow-on). 'Defaults grounded in the experiment results' makes completion contingent on separate, possibly long-running experiment work outside this session. Both risk being unverifiable at execution time.
findings.suggested_edit: Restate 'measurably help' as a concrete in-session check (seeded-defect fixture → expected grounded findings produced). Restate the defaults AC as 'ships defaults; tuning of values tracked in linked experiment ticket' so it is completable in-session.

findings.location: Success Criteria vs Acceptance Criteria sections (overlapping, partly duplicative); 'Docs' AC vs no Success Criterion for docs; 'centrality/blast-radius computed at plan time' appears in both design summary and routing AC.
findings.finding: There are near-duplicate requirement blocks: the Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria lists largely restate the same six/thirteen items in different words (e.g., claim-blocking, fail-open Layer-1, advisory Layer-2, configurable posture, REVIEW_RESULT, CI signal), creating two parallel checklists that can drift. Additionally the 'Docs' acceptance criterion and the per-criterion-config AC have no corresponding Success Criterion, so the two lists are not 1:1. This is mostly coherent but the duplication/asymmetry invites ambiguity about the authoritative checklist.
findings.suggested_edit: Designate one list (Acceptance Criteria) as authoritative and reduce Success Criteria to outcome-level goals, or explicitly map each Success Criterion to its implementing AC(s) to eliminate the parallel near-duplicates.
